My hand has essentially zero realistic equity vs his continuing A/Q/strong pair range, and a raise to the 1600 minimum would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity against this opponent. Calling to try to realize tiny backdoor outs is not +EV here. I preserve my 8,200 stack for better spots and fold.

Button with Q♠6♦ (Qh6d) — marginal hand but great position. Pot is 150, only 100 to call; the minimum raise is 200. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and leverage positional advantage postflop. Claude (SB) has shown aggression and can 3-bet; Flash (BB) tends to call and check down. I open to a standard 3x BB (300) — big enough to get folds and isolate callers but small relative to stacks so I retain plenty of maneuvering room. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet from Claude (Q6o is too weak to face a serious 3-bet out of position), but if called I will play aggressively in position — c-bet appropriate flops, pot-control or check-back dry boards, and leverage blockers when Q hits. This raise maximizes fold equity and preserves stack depth for postflop play.

I'm in the small blind with K♠5♠ against Minni's button raise to 300. While I have a healthy stack (21,275) and Minni likely has a wide range from the button (7,900 stack makes her aggressive), K5 is a marginal hand with poor playability. Key factors: (1) I'll be out of position post-flop, (2) Even if I hit a King, I could easily be outkicked, (3) The backdoor flush draw has minimal value preflop, (4) Flash hasn't acted yet, adding another variable, (5) My previous winning hands have been with premium/strong holdings (AQ, JT, AJ, KK). With a 21k+ stack and no urgency, I should preserve chips and wait for better spots. This is not a profitable long-term call.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h 6s. Minni has raised to 3x from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450), 76o is a weak hand to play out of position against a Button range. My previous calls from the blinds (Hands 6 and 9) were with much stronger hands like ATo and KJo. Given the current stack sizes and my position, I prefer to fold this mediocre hand and wait for a better opportunity.

With 3d9d on the button, I'm holding a marginal weak hand. While position is valuable in 3-handed play, this specific hand lacks sufficient potential to justify playing. The hand has limited straight potential and no pairing strength. At 50/100 blinds with my stack of 21,275, I can be disciplined and wait for better opportunities. Even though I have a positional advantage, good poker requires strong hand selection fundamentals. Folding weak hands consistently is a cornerstone of profitable play. I'll fold and wait for premium or speculative hands in favorable positions.

I am in the Small Blind with AQo, a very strong hand for a 3-handed game. Claude on the button has already folded. I have 10,325 chips, and Minni in the Big Blind has 8,250. My goal is to maximize value from my strong starting hand while remaining aggressive. Raising is the standard play here to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position/with the initiative. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

Call 300. QcTd is a defendable hand from the big blind vs an SB open to 300. I only need to add 200 (total 300) and the pot odds (~33%) make calling correct against a likely wide SB opening range. Stacks are deep (~82 BB effective), so there are implied odds to hit top pair, two pair or straights. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand, so I’ll avoid a 3-bet here and keep the pot controllable. Postflop plan: act cautiously OOP — check most dry boards, c-bet selectively or fold to heavy aggression when missed, and extract value/be aggressive when I hit top pair or strong draws.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit, but the flop of 9c-6c-4c is very unfavorable for my hand. I have the Ace of Diamonds, not a club, so I have no flush draw. The board is monotone, meaning any club in my opponent's hand makes a flush or a very strong draw. Minni called my pre-flop raise from the big blind, and her range includes many suited hands and pocket pairs that connect with this board. Since I am out of position and the board texture is highly coordinated without giving me any real equity (no pair, no flush draw), checking is the most prudent play. I will evaluate based on her action, but I'm prepared to fold to significant pressure.

I hold QcTd on 9c-6c-4c — I currently have a queen-high flush (only A♣ or K♣ beat me). Flash checked the flop, which often indicates either a non-club/pair or a pot-control line rather than a locking nut flush. With deep stacks and a wide SB opening range I should be extracting value and charging any smaller clubs/pairs/draws rather than slow-playing. I will make a value bet roughly half the pot (300) to get called by worse clubs, pairs and draws while keeping the pot manageable out of position. If Flash check-raises small I can call and re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise or shove I will heavily discount my hand (likely behind A♣/K♣) and fold to avoid risking a large portion of my stack.

The flop is extremely unfavorable for my hand. I hold Ace-Queen offsuit with no clubs on a monotone 9-6-4 clubs board. This means I have no pair and no flush draw. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard sizing that could represent a made flush, a single club draw, a pair, or a bluff. However, without any equity or a redraw to a flush, calling here is mathematically poor. Even if I hit an Ace or a Queen on the turn, I could still be drawing dead against a made flush or a hand containing a single club. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with a strong pocket pair (tens). In a three-handed game, this is a premium hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain initiative.

6d2h is the worst offsuit hand with almost no playability. Facing a button open and the big blind still to act, I'd be out of position and likely dominated post‑flop. Calling 250 into a 450 pot OOP to see a flop is a losing long‑term play, and a 3‑bet bluff is unnecessary risk. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

I'm in the big blind with 7c-Jh, a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Flash raised from the button to 300, and Minni folded. While I have decent pot odds (2.25:1 to call 200 into 450), my hand is too weak to justify playing here. 7-Jack offsuit is a marginal hand that's vulnerable and will be difficult to play post-flop when out of position against Flash's range. With a healthy 21,175 chip stack, I can afford to wait for stronger holdings in better spots. This is a straightforward fold.

Button vs the blinds, three-handed — this is a spot to open wide. Q♦3♠ is marginal offsuit, but in heads-up-ish dynamics on the button it has enough equity and fold equity to raise rather than limp or fold. A 3xBB open (300 total) applies pressure to the blinds, avoids multiway limp pots, and gives me position post-flop where I can play the hand flexibly. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (Q3o is dominated often), consider calling very small 3-bets if the sizing and opponent tendencies justify it, and if called play aggressively in position — c-bet dry flops for value/folds, slow down on heavy, coordinated boards. 300 is a standard, exploitative size here to maximize fold equity and keep post-flop options wide.

Premium hand analysis: Pocket Kings is one of the strongest starting hands in poker. I'm in the small blind with 21,125 chips, and Minni (button) has raised to 300. Opponent profiling shows Minni is a frequent raiser with a fairly loose range. 

Strategic reasoning:
1. KK is a premium hand that should be played aggressively pre-flop
2. A 3-bet raise (to 850) accomplishes multiple objectives: building value into the pot, narrowing the field, and establishing strength
3. With my chip stack advantage and position dynamics, this raise puts pressure on both opponents while extracting value
4. The raise size of 850 is a standard 3-bet that isn't exploitatively small but also doesn't overcommit chips pre-flop
5. This hand plays well in all-in scenarios and against medium-strength holdings from both opponents

Expected outcome: Either win the pot pre-flop or proceed to favorable flop situations against a likely weaker range.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10 of Clubs and 7 of Hearts. Minni opened for 300 from the Button and Claude 3-bet to 850 from the Small Blind. 10-7 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range, especially when out of position against two active players. Folding is the clearly superior play here to preserve my stack.

I opened wide from the button as planned, but Claude’s 3‑bet to 850 is large enough and represents a range that heavily dominates Q♢3♠. The pot is 1,250 and it costs 550 to call, so I’d need roughly 23–24% equity to justify a call. Q3o has poor equity and playability versus a typical SB 3‑bet (lots of better Qx, broadways, pairs and suited hands) and I’ll run into reverse implied odds on most turn/river decisions. With 83BB stacks there’s no need to spew chips out of position into a polarized 3‑bet — better to preserve my stack for better spots or to 4‑bet as a balanced bluff with stronger holdings. Fold here.

4-2o is the worst starting hand in poker. Despite being on the button with a positional advantage, this hand has no value. It lacks connectivity, suitedness, or high-card strength. Even though the call is only 100 chips (relatively small), this is a clear fold. The expected value of playing 4-2o out of position against two blind defenders is significantly negative. My stack (21,575) gives me the flexibility to fold marginal hands and wait for better opportunities. Folding unplayable hands is fundamental to long-term profitability.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 6h. The Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. Q6o is a weak hand to play out of position, even in a short-handed game. While I could attempt to steal the blind with a raise, Minni has shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively in previous hands. Completing the small blind often leads to difficult post-flop situations with marginal hands. Therefore, I will choose the conservative route and fold this hand.

I am on the button with 9h 3s. This is a very weak starting hand that lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. In a three-handed game, while ranges are wider, 93o is still well below the threshold for a standard opening raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with J♠8♣. Pot is 150 and it’s just me vs Claude (big stack, somewhat aggressive). J8o is well within a standard heads-up opening range — I want initiative and fold equity rather than limp OOP. The min-raise is 200, but I size to 300 (3BB) to discourage light 3-bets and give a cleaner postflop spot. If called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate vs heavy aggression; if Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand. Overall this is a standard open-raise to seize the initiative.

Calling with A5o in the Big Blind heads-up against Minni's Small Blind raise. Minni has shown wide raising tendencies throughout the session. While A5o is marginal, I have reasonable pot odds (2.5:1), the largest stack (21,475), and solid implied odds if I hit an Ace. Being out of position is a concern, but heads-up dynamics make this more playable. The call allows me to see a flop and play exploitatively against Minni's aggressive patterns.

Flop is K♦ 4♠ 5♥ — very dry and square. I was the preflop raiser with J♠8♣ and currently have no pair and no realistic draws, so my hand has very little showdown value. Standard plan as the preflop aggressor heads‑up is to c‑bet high frequency on these kinds of boards to pick up pots and fold out A‑high/overcards and weak floaters. Claude is deep and somewhat aggressive, so I want a size that preserves fold equity but doesn’t bloat the pot if I get called — half the pot is appropriate. Bet 300 into 600 to take it down now; if he folds great, if he calls I’ll proceed very cautiously (fold to sizable raises, check the turn on blanks and only continue with real improvement or a very good read).

Calling the flop bet with middle pair and ace kicker. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (900+300 pot for 300 to call) which justifies calling with A5o. Minni's small bet size (1/3 pot) suggests weakness. Being in position to check behind or apply turn pressure while maintaining my stack advantage makes this the optimal exploitative play against her aggressive tendencies.

Turn paired the board (Kd Kc). I c‑bet the dry K‑4‑5 flop and got called, which means Claude’s calling range likely includes real hands (Kx, pocket 4s/5s, or disciplined floats). With J♠8♣ I have no pair and no meaningful draw — my hand has essentially zero showdown value and no fold equity versus a caller who can now have trips or a pair. Checking controls the pot (pot is 1,200) and preserves my stack against a likely better holding; I’ll fold to turn aggression and re‑evaluate the river only if I improve or he gives a clear bluffing line. Therefore: check.

Minni's turn check after flop aggression suggests weakness or uncertainty. My pair of fives with ace kicker is marginal and vulnerable. Checking is the optimal exploitative play: it keeps the pot small, allows me to see the river for free, maintains my position, and avoids overcommitting with a hand that's likely either beat or barely ahead. This aligns with my stack advantage and cautious approach to marginal hands in position.

I have J♠8♣ with no pair and no draw on K♦ 4♠ 5♥ / K♣ / A♥. I was the preflop & flop aggressor but checked the turn to control the pot after being called — that line and Claude’s check on the turn point to real showdown value (Kx, Ax, or pocket pairs) or a safe showdown. A river bluff here would be very thin: most hands that beat me will call and most worse hands that might fold are marginal and unlikely to have called earlier. Preserve stack and go to showdown: check. (Plan: fold to any river bet.)

Betting 500 on the river as a value bet with two pair (Aces and Fives). The Ace on the river significantly improved my hand from the marginal pair-of-fives I had on the turn. Minni's passive check-down (checking turn and river) suggests weakness - she likely has a medium-strength hand like a single pair (KQ, KJ) or a medium pocket pair. A 500 bet represents reasonable value extraction: it's substantial enough to get called by marginal hands but not so large that it's polarizing. Given Minni's remaining 7750 stack, this bet size applies appropriate pressure while capitalizing on my strong hand and her demonstrated weakness in this spot.

Holding J♠8♣ on K♦‑4♠‑5♥ / K♣ / A♥. Pot is 1700 and Claude bets 500. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor but checked the turn; my hand has no pair and no draw — effectively zero showdown value. Claude’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) looks like real value (Kx, Ax, or pocket pairs) and there are very few believable bluffs on this texture. Calling 500 to win ~2200 gives ~22.7% pot odds, but my equity vs a value‑heavy range is well below that. No blockers or reads justify hero‑calling here. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Button with 8♥6♥ — suited, playable, and much stronger in position. With 77.5 BB effective and only the blinds to beat, I want to seize initiative and apply pressure rather than limp. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) gives good fold equity against the blinds, keeps the pot manageable if Claude (SB) shows aggression, and lets me play postflop in position with decent equity and backdoor flush/straight potential. If I face a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve chips; if called I can c-bet or realize equity from position.

Folding K4o from the small blind facing a button raise. The hand is too weak to call out of position, and there's still action from the big blind. Preserving stack for better spots is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Seven of spades. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude has folded the Small Blind. King-Seven suited is a strong hand to defend against a standard Button opening range in a 3-handed game. I am getting good pot odds (calling 200 to win 450) and the hand has excellent playability post-flop. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is too tight and 3-betting might inflate the pot unnecessarily against an active opponent. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six completes my straight) on a very connected and wet board (Td-8d-9s). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to them. If Minni bets, I have more than enough equity with my straight draw and one overcard (King) to continue in the hand. Leading out (donk betting) here would be unusual and might expose the strength of my draw or force out hands I can beat later. I will check and see how the Button reacts.

I raised preflop and got called by the BB; pot is 650. Flop Td‑8d‑9s gives me middle pair (8s) with a gutshot to a 7 and I’m in position with range advantage as the preflop raiser. This is a fairly connected board so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, but I do want to extract value from worse pairs (77–22, single tens/overcards that will fold) and charge draws. A small-to-medium c‑bet (~35–40% pot) achieves that — applies pressure, realizes equity, and keeps the pot manageable if I get raised. Plan: bet 250; if called, reassess on the turn (check to control on blanks, bet for value if I improve). Fold to a large raise — my pair is too vulnerable to continue without improvement.

On a board of Td-8d-9s, I have an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six gives me a straight) and one overcard (King). Minni's continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot is quite small (less than 40%). With 8 outs to the straight (approximately 32% chance to hit by the river) plus potential King outs, I have massive equity to continue. I will call the bet to see a turn card, as raising on such a coordinated board might overplay my hand and face a re-raise from Minni's strong range (which includes many pairs, sets, and two pairs), and my current hand is still only King-high.

On the turn, the King of hearts (Kh) gives me top pair to go along with my open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six). While my hand has improved significantly, the board is extremely coordinated (Td-8d-9s-Kh), making it likely that Minni's range contains many hands that still beat me, such as straights (QJ or J7) or two pairs. As the out-of-position player who has been calling so far, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and see how Minni responds to the King. If Minni bets, I will likely call given my top pair and straight draw, but leading out myself might run into a raise from a superior hand.

Check. I opened and c‑bet the flop and Flash called; the pot is 1,150. The K♥ on the turn is a clear overcard to my pair of eights and significantly improves the caller’s likely range (Kx, KT, some two-pair/sets). I only have ~6 clean outs to improve (four 7s for a straight, two 8s for trips ≈ ~13% on the river), so this is a marginal hand to be building the pot with. With SPR ≈6 and stacks deep enough to get committed on a raise, I prefer pot control: check to see a free river, induce bluffs, and avoid facing a big turn raise with a vulnerable pair. Plan: fold to large turn aggression; if checked through, re-evaluate on the river (value-bet if I improve, consider a small blocker-bet or check behind if I don’t).

I have top pair (Kings) with a weak 7 kicker on a very coordinated board (Td-8d-9s-Kh-4s). The 4s on the river is a brick. On the turn, the action went check-check after the King arrived, which suggests that Minni's range is likely capped; she probably doesn't have a straight (QJ) or a set, as those hands would typically bet for value on such a wet board. However, my kicker is weak, and a bet from me is unlikely to get called by many worse hands while potentially getting raised by better ones. Checking is the most prudent move to reach showdown cheaply or to induce a bluff from missed diamond draws or high-card hands like AQ/AJ.

Check and take showdown. I have only a single pair of eights (8♥6♥) and the turn K♥ likely improved a lot of Flash’s calling range (Kx, Tx, 9x, two-pair combos). I have very few clean outs and limited fold equity against a passive caller who has shown a fit‑or‑call line. Betting risks building a pot where I’m often behind and will be called by better hands; checking preserves stack and forces Flash to show real value. Plan: check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

With pocket queens on the button, this is a premium hand that should be played aggressively. I'm in excellent position (button) versus two blind opponents. A standard 3x raise to 300 (3x the 100 BB) is the correct play here. This raise accomplishes several goals: (1) It wins the blinds immediately some of the time, (2) It builds the pot when I have a strong hand, (3) It gives me position advantage post-flop if called. My stack of 22,125 is healthy, and with my track record in this session of aggressive, winning poker, continuing this pattern with a premium hand is the optimal strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with J4 offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 3BB (300). J4 is a weak hand and does not play well out of position against a raise from the button, especially since Claude has a very deep stack and has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid being out of position in a bloated pot with a mediocre hand.

I'm in the BB with K♥4♣ facing a BTN open to 300 (SB folded). Pot is 450; I need to call 200 more (total commit 300). K4o is a marginal hand and is often dominated, but versus a likely wide button opening range it retains some showdown value and occasional two-pair/top-pair potential. Immediate pot odds (450:200) require ~30–31% equity — borderline but defensible here given Claude's frequent opening and the deep stacks. 3-betting with K4o is suboptimal (poor blockers/playability) and folding would be too tight for these odds. I will call to see a flop and play cautiously OOP: fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, proceed with top pair or strong draws, and avoid bloating the pot with weak kickers or single-pair weak hands.

I hold K♥4♣ — bottom pair on a 4♦6♠5♦ flop. I’m out of position to a BTN open-raiser and the board is very draw-heavy (two diamonds, connected: 3/7/2/8 outs for straights) and vulnerable to 6/5 pairs. With a weak pair and no backdoor diamond, I want to keep the pot small, induce bluffs, and re-evaluate on the turn. Checking controls the pot and lets me call a small c-bet but fold to heavy aggression unless I improve. Plan: check now; call modest bets (~≤1/3 pot) but fold to large bets/raises; if villain checks back I take a free turn card and decide next street based on action and improvement.

I hold pocket queens, one of the premium hands in poker, and the flop is relatively safe (4d-6s-5d) with no broadway cards. Minni checked to me, which indicates weakness or a hand that's not immediately strong. With 650 in the pot, a standard continuation bet of approximately 65% pot (425 chips) is ideal here to:

1) Extract value from Minni's weaker holdings that may call (mid-pairs, weak broadway, draws)
2) Protect my strong hand and gain information about the true strength of their holding
3) Build the pot while I have a favorable equity position

My stack is healthy at 21,825, and aggressive betting with premium hands has been successful throughout this session. This bet is neither too small (which would allow cheap draws) nor too large (which would overcommit with a hand that's likely already ahead).

I have K♥4♣ — bottom pair with a decent K kicker on a 4♦6♠5♦ board. Claude is the BTN open-raiser and will c-bet a wide range, including many bluffs and single-pair hands. His flop bet of 425 into a 1075 pot gives me roughly 28% pot odds (425 to win ~1500), so a call is defensible to realize equity and see a turn card. I don't want to 3-bet with this weak, vulnerable holding (no diamond backdoor and likely dominated by 6s/5s), and folding here would be too tight given the price. Plan: call the 425, keep the pot controlled, and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve to two pair/top pair with a K or get a clear read to continue.

I have K♥4♣ — bottom pair on 4♦6♠5♦8♠. Claude c‑bet the flop but checked the turn, which looks like weakness (missed draws or marginal pairs) rather than strength. The board is very draw‑heavy (3/7 complete straights), and my pair is vulnerable, so I want to keep the pot small and realize equity. Betting here would bloat the pot and likely get called by hands that already beat me (6x/5x/8x) or fold out worse; checking controls the pot, allows me to see a cheap river, and keeps bluffs in his range. Plan: check the turn, call modest river bets for showdown value, and fold to large river pressure unless I improve to two pair or better.

I'm holding pocket queens (QQ) with a healthy stack and strong hand. The board is 4d-6s-5d-8s, which is a dry, non-threatening texture. Minni has checked to me on the turn, indicating either a weak holding, a draw, or a hand that's not immediately strong.

My QQ is an overpair to the board and is very likely the best hand at this moment. Minni's checking pattern (checked flop, called my bet; checked turn) suggests a medium-strength hand or a draw trying to get to the river cheaply.

Key considerations:
1. I've successfully extended my advantage with aggressive plays earlier in this session
2. A turn bet extracts value from weaker holdings (mid-pairs, weak broadway hands, draws looking for a free card)
3. One card remains (river), so I want to build the pot while ahead
4. A reasonable turn bet is 50-60% of pot, approximately 750-850 chips
5. This sizing is not too aggressive (which risks over-committing) but maintains value extraction

I'll bet 800, which represents solid value betting with my premium hand.

I hold K♥4♣ — bottom pair on 4♦6♠5♦8♠. I called preflop and the flop c‑bet, and Claude's turn stab (800 into a 2300 pot, ~34–35% pot) is a relatively small probe that can include both value hands (6x/5x/8x/pairs) and a lot of bluffs/continuation hands from a BTN opener. Calling 800 to win ~3100 requires only ~25–26% equity, which is reasonable with a vulnerable but live pair that beats many of his bluffs and missed overcards. Raising is thin and will often put me in a bad spot with a marginal hand; folding here surrenders good pot odds. Plan: call the 800 to see the river and re‑evaluate — fold to large river aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a clear read to continue.

I just improved to two pair on the river (Ks + 4s with K♥4♣ on 4♦-6♠-5♦-8♠-K♠). That’s a strong showdown hand vs Claude’s flop/turn betting line — it beats single-pair Kx and most weaker pairs and will often be best when he was c-betting as a semi-bluff or thin value. The board is, however, dangerous: 4-5-6-8 completes straights (7x/7x combos) and there are set combos (66, 55, 88) that beat me. Claude has shown willingness to bet small and probe, so he has a mix of value and bluffs/marginal hands.

Given that he hasn’t bet the river and I’m first to act, I want to extract value from worse one-pair hands (Kx, Qx, Jx, medium pairs) but avoid bloating the pot vs straights/sets. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) is the best line: it prices in calls from weaker Ks and many weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable if I’m raised. Pot is 3100, so I’ll bet 1200. Plan if raised: a small-to-medium raise (tester) I will likely call; a large, polarized shove/raise will force a sharper decision since many of those raise hands will be straights/sets that beat me.

With pocket queens on a 4d-6s-5d-8s-Ks board, I'm facing a river raise from short-stacked Minni who played passively throughout. The pot offers 3.58:1 odds, requiring only ~22% equity to call. Minni's check-call pattern on flop and turn before suddenly raising suggests they likely improved with the river King or are making a desperation bluff, rather than having a premium holding (which would have played more aggressively earlier). My QQ is strong and likely ahead of most reasonable hands in their range. The call is mathematically sound and strategically justified.

I have Ace-Nine of Spades on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Holding 3♠7♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (I have to call 250 into a 450 pot) is a clear fold. This hand has very poor equity vs a standard opening range, I'll be out of position postflop with the big blind still to act, and a call would commit chips into a marginal spot (calling 250 to win ~700 requires ~36% equity heads-up). A 3‑bet bluff isn’t justified here against two unknown opponents and would put me in difficult, bloated pots. Preserve the stack and fold.

I'm holding 6s 5s in the big blind facing Flash's 300 raise from the button after Minni folded. This is a weak hand with poor playability out of position. While I have approximate 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to call into 450), my hand equity against Flash's range is insufficient to justify calling. 

6-5 offsuit plays poorly postflop, particularly against an aggressive button raiser when out of position. With a healthy 19,300 stack and 150 hands to play, this is a spot to fold and wait for stronger holdings. The pot odds don't overcome the positional disadvantage and weak hand strength.

9♠4♥ is a very weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand — not something I want to build a pot with by raising. However the price to see a flop is only 100 chips (≈1% of my stack) and I have position on both opponents postflop, which makes a cheap look acceptable. I won't invest more preflop with this hand; I will call 100 to see the flop, fold to any meaningful 3‑bet preflop, and proceed postflop only with a pair, a good draw, or a clear position/realization spot to bluff or extract value. This preserves stack while keeping pressure on later streets when I have position.

With 7h-Jd in the small blind facing a button call from Minni, folding is the optimal play. This is a weak hand with poor kicker strength, and I'm out of position for the entire hand. While I've already committed 50 chips to the pot, my pot odds (250:50) don't justify playing a hand this weak from the worst position at the table. I should preserve chips and wait for stronger opportunities in better positions. The big blind also hasn't acted yet, which adds additional pressure.

I'm in the big blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8h-As), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Minni, on the button, has just limped (called 100). Claude in the small blind has folded. Given that it's just the two of us and Minni's limp often indicates a weaker range or a desire to see a cheap flop, I have a clear opportunity to raise for value and to take control of the pot. Raising will help me define her hand and potentially end the pot right here. A standard raise to 3 or 4 times the big blind is appropriate.

I limped with 9♠4♥ for a cheap look and position, but Flash’s raise to 400 after my call is a clear, meaningful raise. Calling another 300 into the 550 pot (making it ~850) requires roughly ~26% equity; 9-4o has negligible equity and very poor playability postflop. I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

Raising to 300 from the button with Ac6s. Position is strong with only two opponents. The raise puts pressure on the blinds, who have shown willingness to fold to aggression. Even if called, I'll have position post-flop with an ace-high holding and multiple ways to win (top pair, improving board texture). This aggressive button play aligns with my profitable table image from earlier hands.

I have been dealt pocket Queens (Qd, Qc) in the Small Blind. This is a premium hand. Claude, on the button, has opened with a raise to 300. Given the 3-handed nature of the game and my position, I need to 3-bet for value and to protect my equity out of position. I will raise to 1000, which is roughly 3.3x Claude's opening size, to build the pot and discourage the Big Blind from entering cheaply with marginal hands.

Facing a button open to 300 and a large small‑blind 3‑bet to 1000. I hold 2♣3♦ — offsuit, very poor playability and unlikely to have the ~39% equity needed to call 900 into a 1400 pot, especially multiway and out of position. The SB 3‑bet signifies a strong or aggressive range and a 4‑bet bluff with this hand is a leak (no useful blockers). With deep stacks and 150 hands to maximize, preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with Ac6o, I'm likely dominated or flipping at best. My weak kicker is vulnerable, and even in position, the risk-reward doesn't justify calling 700 more. With strong chip position (18950), I should fold and wait for premium hands where I can play with better equity.

I am on the button with A5o. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. A5o also has some wheel straight potential and the Ace high value. I will raise to the standard 3x big blind to keep my opening range consistent with previous hands.

Call to 300 (add 250). Q♠9♠ is a defendable SB hand vs a BTN open: suited, decent playability (backdoor flush/straight and can make a good top pair). The pot is 450 and calling 250 makes it 700, so I need ~36% equity — reasonable given deep stacks (~9.7k) and implied odds. I don’t want to 3-bet here because I’ll be out of position and Q9s is often dominated by a value 3-bet. Folding is too tight versus a standard button raise. Plan: call, see the flop, proceed cautiously OOP; fold to a large, commit-level 3-bet from Claude but continue vs single caller or a controllable board.

Pocket sevens in the big blind against a button raise and small blind call. With 200 to call and a pot of 700, I have good pot odds (~3.5:1), but being out of position in a 3-way pot makes this hand vulnerable. My aggressive table image and solid equity (~35-40%) warrant a raise here. A raise to 750 total (650 more) accomplishes multiple goals: it leverages my table image, narrows the field, gives me better post-flop control, and takes down some equity immediately. This is preferable to calling and playing out of position in a multiway pot.

I raised to 300 from the button with Ad5c, a standard move in a 3-handed game. Minni called from the small blind, but Claude (Big Blind) has put in a 3-bet to 750. A5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range from a competent opponent like Claude, especially when the small blind has already called the initial raise. Calling or 4-betting here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak Ace in a bloated pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Claude (BB) has 3-bet to 750 after I defended the BTN open with Q♠9♠ from the SB. Flash folded, so it's heads-up. Pot is 1,350 and I must call 450 more — break-even equity = 450/(1350+450) = 25%. Q9s is suited, has good playability (backdoor spade/folding/straight potential) and reasonable equity vs a wide/small-sized 3-bet. I don't want to 4-bet out of position with a hand that is often dominated by value 3-bets (AQ, KQ, big pairs). With deep stacks, implied odds justify calling one more street and seeing a flop. Plan: call to see the flop; continue on Q/9/spade hits or strong draw, fold to heavy commitment/large 4-bets or multi-street pressure on dry boards. I will not attempt a small isolation 4-bet here — calling keeps options open and uses the pot odds.

Check. On 4♠-8♥-K♥ I have Q♠9♠: no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade possibility (need both turn+river spades). Claude 3‑bet preflop, so his range is heavy with Kx/QQ+/AK/AQ and I’m usually behind. With ~9k effective stacks and OOP, pot control is correct — don’t throw chips in as a thin bluff on this texture. Plan: check and evaluate his action. If he fires a small cbet (~≤1/3 pot) I can call as a cheap bluff‑catcher (I have 6 clean Q/9 outs plus remote runner‑runner spade potential) and fold to strong multi‑street pressure. If he checks back I’ll realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet only with improvement or a clear plan).

Continuation bet on the flop. Minni checked, giving me the lead. A solid c-bet of 1100 (about 60% pot) applies pressure, is consistent with my aggressive pre-flop play, and may take the pot immediately. My pocket sevens have decent equity against most of Minni's calling range, and this bet accomplishes multiple goals: building the pot when ahead, taking the initiative, and potentially winning the hand right here.

I checked the flop (4♠-8♥-K♥) and Claude fires a small c‑bet (1,100 into a 2,900 pot). Calling 1,100 requires ~27.5% equity (1,100 / (2,900+1,100)). Q♠9♠ has no pair and only remote backdoor spade/runner-runner possibilities, with ~24% to make a Q/9 by the river plus a tiny extra from backdoors — slightly below break‑even vs a very strong, value-heavy range. However Claude 3‑bet preflop but is c‑betting small here, which suggests a mix including missed hands and bluffs. With deep stacks and reasonable implied odds, I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a turn rather than folding or making a thin bluff raise OOP. Plan: call one street to see the turn; fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair (Q or 9) or pick up clear additional equity (e.g., a spade on turn that improves my redraw prospects). I will not raise — too risky and thin with no pair and out of position.

Check. I have Qs9s on 4s-8h-Kh-Jh: no pair and my backdoor spade draw is now dead (turn is not a spade). The turn also brings a third heart, which makes Claude’s 3‑bet/cbet range much stronger (he can now have two hearts for a made flush or strong K/Ax/Qx combos). My plan preflop/flop was to call one street and fold to turn aggression without improvement — I didn’t improve. I’m out of position with ~7.9k behind and the pot is ~4k, so pot control is correct. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back I’ll see the river and only continue with a pair or very cheap price.

Minni has checked on the turn, showing weakness or at least lack of aggression. With pocket sevens on a board of 4s-8h-Kh-Jh, my hand is moderately strong but vulnerable. The board has developed significantly with three hearts visible, making flush draws likely in Minni's range. 

Key considerations:
- My 7s are a one-pair hand with no kicker advantage
- The turn brought the Jh, adding another heart and broadway card that potentially helps Minni's calling range (AQ, AJ, AT, or flush/straight draws)
- I've already committed 1100 on the flop with my c-bet
- A bet here risks getting called by hands that beat me (two pair, sets, flush draws) or getting check-raised in a marginal situation
- Checking gives me a free river card where I could hit another seven
- The pot is substantial (4000), so I don't need to win immediately

This is a spot to exercise restraint. My aggressive image is already established from the pre-flop 3-bet and flop c-bet. Checking back allows me to realize my hand's equity while keeping the pot manageable and avoiding unnecessary variance with a medium-strength hand. I'll see a free river and reassess.

I rivered top pair (Q♠9♠ on 4♠-8♥-K♥-J♥-Q♦) but with a mediocre kicker. Claude 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — so his range after those actions is heavy with value (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQ and strong Kx) and contains many hands that still beat my Q on the river. A thin value bet here has little fold equity and will often be called by better hands (AQ/QQ/KK/AA) while not getting called enough by worse hands to be +EV. I’d rather keep the pot small and go to showdown with top pair rather than risk getting value‑bet/raised off my equity. Plan: pot control — check back and see showdown (be prepared to call a small river bet but fold to a large raise).

This is the river with all five community cards exposed (4s-8h-Kh-Jh-Qd), and Minni has checked showing weakness or a marginal holding.

Hand analysis: My pocket sevens is a one-pair hand with no kicker advantage on a well-coordinated board. The turn brought Jh (adding another heart and broadway card), and the river completed the board with Qd.

Strategic considerations:
1. **Hand strength**: 77 is vulnerable but may be ahead if Minni whiffed a draw or holds a weaker pair
2. **Minni's line**: Two checks (turn and river) strongly suggest weakness - she either missed a draw or has a marginal made hand
3. **River dynamics**: This is the final card with substantial pot (4,000). Betting risks losing more chips if Minni has two pair, a set, or a made flush
4. **Pot realization**: Checking realizes my equity immediately and goes to showdown with what I have

My aggressive pre-flop 3-bet and flop c-bet have already established table image and taken down equity. On the river, betting with a one-pair hand in a strong showing-weakness spot is marginal EV. Minni's two street check strongly indicates I'm ahead against her range, and checking avoids the variance of getting called by better hands while still winning against her whiffed draws.

Checking to showdown is the disciplined play here.

I’m on the button with 6♠8♥ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. I’ll open to 300 (3x BB). A 3x open gives good fold equity and keeps the initiative while still being small relative to deep stacks; it lets me play postflop in position if called. Claude (SB) has shown frequent preflop aggression, so if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand — 6-8 offsuit performs poorly vs a strong re-raise. If called, I’ll c-bet selectively on dry flops and realize implied odds to hit straights; overall the raise maximizes long-term EV from the button.

Folding 7-9 offsuit in small blind against button raise. Weak hand, out of position, risk of big blind squeeze, and marginal equity. With a healthy 17050 chip stack, I should be selective with my small blind calls and reserve them for stronger holdings. Preserving chips for better opportunities is more profitable than playing marginal hands out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h2c. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard late-position open. However, 92o is one of the weakest possible hands in Hold'em and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Even getting 2.25-to-1 odds to call the extra 200, the hand is simply too weak to justify a defense. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

4d 7s is a weak hand that doesn't meet my standards even on the button. While I'm in a favorable position and only facing a 100 chip call into 150, the fundamental issue is that 4-7 offsuit is at the very bottom of hand rankings. I have a solid stack (17,050) and can afford to be selective. Even with reasonable pot odds, playing weak hands typically leads to difficult post-flop situations and marginal decisions. I'll fold and wait for better opportunities where I can leverage my position more effectively.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 7d. The Button has folded. Against Minni in the Big Blind, who has shown a tendency to defend and play post-flop, I will choose to complete the bet by calling the additional 50 chips. This allows me to see a flop with a speculative hand at good odds without bloating the pot out of position.

Heads-up in the big blind with 8♦5♠. Flash (SB) just limped, pot is only 200 and I have no additional amount to call. 8-5o is a weak, speculative hand and I don’t want to build a multi-street pot out of position against a wide but potentially sticky limp-calling range. Raising to isolate is an option, but given I’ll be OOP and stacks are deep, that often creates difficult spots for little value. Preferred play is to check and see a flop for free. Plan: fold to meaningful aggression on most dry flops, but continue (call or lead) when I pair, pick up strong draws (open-ender, two-pair, trips), or when the villain gives a clear sign of weakness (checks and I can take it down with a small c-bet on a favorable board).

I am in the Small Blind and checked the flop in a limp pot. The flop is 3s-3h-Qc. I have 8s 7d, which is essentially air on this board. In a small pot with no pre-flop action, there is little reason to bet out of position into a paired board that could easily have hit Minni's range or where she might have a Queen. I will check and see what Minni does, looking to fold if she bets unless the price is extremely cheap or the board develops favorably.

Flop: 3s 3h Qc. I have 8d5s — no pair, no real draws (only 6 outs to pair by the river, ~24% equity). Flash limp-called preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide (Qx, small pairs, air). Pot is 200 and the minimum bet here is 100, which would give me ~33% pot odds to call a 100 bet into 300 — worse than my ~24% equity. Out of position with a weak, non‑improving hand, I want to control the pot and avoid difficult decisions; checking is the least costly line. I will check and fold to meaningful aggression (any standard bet here is unprofitable to call).

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 7d. The board is 3s-3h-Qc-6h. There was no action on the flop. The 6h on the turn is unlikely to have improved my hand, and I still have zero equity. In a limped pot, there is very little incentive to bluff out of position against Minni, who has been sticky in several previous hands. Since the action is checked to me, I will check again to see a free river and likely fold to any significant bet.

Check. I’m out of position with 8♦5♠ on 3♠ 3♥ Q♣ 6♥ — no pair and only ~6 outs (~24% to improve). The pot is small (200) and Flash (the limper) has checked twice, so his range is wide (Qx, small pairs, air). Betting out of position with this weak holding will often create difficult multi-street decisions and is thin for value; a minimum bet of 100 would require ~33% equity to justify a call, which I don’t have. I’ll control the pot by checking and plan to fold to meaningful aggression on this street. If checked through to the river I’ll re-evaluate with the free card (possibly check-back or take a small bluff if the river is favorable).

The hand has been checked through on every street in a limped pot. I have 8-high (8s 7d) on a board of 3s-3h-Qc-6h-9h. I have no showdown value. However, the pot is very small (200), and there is little reason to attempt a bluff out of position against Minni, who has shown a tendency to call down light in previous hands. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or simply concede the small pot if she bets.

I have 8♦5♠ on 3s 3h Qc 6h 9h — no pair and no hearts. Flash limped and then checked flop/turn/river, so his range is wide (Qx, small pairs, missed draws, air), but the river brought a third heart and I don’t block many of the sensible bluff-catchers. Pot is tiny (200) and any stab here is a thin bluff that will often be called by Qx or small pairs; I also don’t want to get check-raised or face a value bet from a genuine made hand. My prior plan was to check and fold to meaningful aggression; that remains the best line — control the pot and avoid a marginal bluff. Check and fold to any bet.

I am on the button with 9c4c. Minni is in the small blind and Claude is in the big blind. While suited, 9-4 is a weak starting hand even for button standards. I have a healthy stack of 10,925, but there's no need to play trash hands when I have both blinds still to act and potentially defend. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid getting into a difficult post-flop spot with a marginal hand.

Heads-up situation (only Claude in the big blind). I’m on the small blind with 5♣4♦ — a weak, but playable hand in heads-up because I’ll have postflop position if I take the initiative. Pot is 150, call is only 50 but opening a wide range from the small blind is standard to pressure the big blind and steal the blinds. I’ll raise to the min (200 total) to take the lead and capitalize on position; if Claude 3-bets heavily I’ll fold this hand and preserve my stack. Postflop I’ll c-bet on favorable textures and play straightforwardly.

Calling with A5o in the big blind against Minni's raise from small blind. With 3:1 pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot) and only two opponents remaining, this is mathematically justified. While A5o is not a premium hand, the ace gives me a strong kicker if I improve, and I'll have position post-flop to make decisions based on board texture and Minni's play. This allows me to see more cards and evaluate hand strength before committing significant chips.

I opened wide preflop as standard heads-up, but this flop (9♥-3♥-J♥) is extremely unfavorable for 5♣4♦. It’s a monotone heart board that heavily weights Claude’s calling range (any heart, J/9/3, many pairs and flush draws). I have no hearts and no pair — effectively air with only remote backdoors — so a continuation bet has poor fold equity and very poor showdown equity when called. With position and deep stacks, the lower-variance, higher-expected-value line is to check and control the pot: see how Claude reacts and fold to a meaningful bet, or take a free turn if he checks back and re-evaluate.

I have A♣5♠ on a flop of 9♥3♥J♥ - this is a complete miss for me with no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. My only equity is the ace-high with a weak kicker. Minni's check from the small blind suggests weakness, but the all-hearts flop is unusual and difficult to connect with without hearts. Given my poor hand strength, lack of any real draws, and out-of-position status, checking allows me to see the turn card for free and potentially improve. Betting with no showdown value in a position where Minni showed weakness would be unnecessarily putting more chips at risk. The prudent play is to give up the pot on the turn if I don't improve and preserve chips for stronger situations.

I have 5♣4♦ on 9♥-3♥-J♥-3♣. I checked the flop to control the pot; on the turn I still have essentially no made hand (only six outs to a pair on the river ≈13%) and no hearts, while the board heavily favors any heart in Claude’s calling range (they already have a flush) or pair hands (Jx/9x/3x). Betting here as a bluff has very low fold equity and will be called or raised by most hands that beat me, so it’s a losing line. I’ll check to keep the pot small, preserve stack, and see the river for a cheap chance to hit. Plan: fold to a meaningful bet on the turn; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river — if I pair up (4 or 5) I can look for value, otherwise I’ll avoid bloating the pot.

I have A♣5♠ which is just ace-high on a 9♥3♥J♥3♣ board. This is an extremely weak holding with no pair, no draws, and no realistic equity. Minni has checked twice (flop and turn), suggesting weakness or a hand that doesn't want to build the pot. In this situation, checking down to the river is correct: (1) I have minimal showdown value, (2) betting with ace-high will likely only be called by hands that beat me or fold hands I already beat, (3) Minni's twice-checked line suggests she's not committed to the pot, (4) seeing the river for free preserves chips while keeping my options alive. I'll evaluate the river after it comes. This is a clear spot to take a free card and minimize losses on a complete miss.

Board: 9♥ 3♥ J♥ 3♣ A♠. My hand: 5♣4♦. Pot 400, to act with no bet. I opened wide preflop heads-up, but this runout completely misses my range — no pair, no heart, no meaningful blocker to A or hearts. Claude checked flop and turn, which suggests either weakness or a desire to control size with made hands; bluffing the river here would be very thin (I don’t block many Ax or heart combos) and faces a calling range heavy with Jx/9x/3x and hearts. A small stab might fold out some air, but expected fold equity is low and the stacks make getting called costly. Best low-variance, +EV line is to check and fold to a river bet; with no bet now we preserve stack and avoid a losing bluff.

River value bet with a strong hand. I improved significantly from A5o to a pair of aces. Minni's three consecutive checks (flop, turn, river) indicate weakness or a bricked draw on this heavily-hearts flop. She's checking it down rather than building the pot, suggesting most of her range is weak or missed. A 300 chip bet (~75% pot) extracts value from hands I beat (bricked flush draws, weaker pairs) while being reasonable enough that hands better than mine will call anyway. This maximizes EV on the river.

Pot is 700, Claude bet 300 (to call 300). Board 9♥-3♥-J♥-3♣-A♠ and my hand 5♣4♦ has no pair, no flush, no meaningful blockers. Claude checked flop and turn then fires a small ~43% pot river bet — line consistent with thin value (Jx/9x/3x), slow-played flushes, or a blocking/top-pair type value — not a polarizing bluff line. Calling requires ~30% equity to break even, which I do not have; bluff frequency here is likely low. Preserve stack and fold to a river bet with nothing.

Three-handed and on the button with K♠4♥ — a marginal hand, but position gives a lot of postflop leverage. Limping here simply gives the big blind correct odds and invites multiway pots; against two opponents in 3-handed play you should open a wide range to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I’ll open to 3bb (300) to apply pressure, narrow the field, and play the hand in position if called. If Claude (SB) 3-bets to a sizable amount I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and otherwise proceed cautiously.

Calling with As4s in the small blind. This suited ace has sufficient equity against Minni's button raise range and good pot odds (250 into 700). With my larger stack, I can play postflop flexibility. The hand has multiple ways to improve (ace, flush draw, wheel), making it worth seeing a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with AJo, a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Minni) opened to 3x, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. Both opponents are relatively deep-stacked. While AJo is strong enough to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot, I have two opponents to deal with. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop, especially since Claude has already capped his range by just calling from the SB. However, 3-betting would likely isolate Minni or win the pot immediately. Given the current dynamics and the fact that I am closing the action if I call, calling is a solid, lower-variance play. I will call the 200 more.

With As4s on a 3h 2c Qh flop, I'm first to act with a strong draw (12 outs: 9 flush outs + 4 wheel outs - 1 overlap). However, I'm out of position in the small blind, and this is a relatively dry flop that likely connected with Minni's pre-flop raise range (potential pairs, overcards, stronger hands). Checking allows me to: (1) control pot size and gather information from Minni's and Flash's actions before committing more chips, (2) see a turn card for free if both opponents check, (3) avoid being check-raised when I don't have a made hand. With deep stacks and a good draw, I can afford to play cautiously and let my opponents define their hands while keeping options open for future streets.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on a Queen-high board. While I have two overcards (Ace and Jack) and the nut flush backdoor draw with the Ace of hearts, I missed the flop. The Small Blind (Claude) has already checked. Since the Button (Minni) was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play here is to check and see how she proceeds. Betting into two opponents on this board with only Ace-high is unnecessary. I will check and evaluate based on the Button's action.

I opened preflop and both opponents called, so my betting range is wide and I have positional advantage. The flop (Qh 3h 2c) missed my K♠4♥ (only a backdoor heart). Both checked to me, so a small continuation bet is the highest EV play: it folds out many overcards and weak pairs, denies equity to backdoor draws, and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand. I’ll size small (≈1/3 pot = 300) to maximize fold equity while preserving stack depth. If I get raised strongly I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy pressure).

Calling Minni's 300-chip raise with As4s on a 3h 2c Qh flop. I have a strong draw with approximately 12 outs: 9 spade outs for a flush draw plus 3 non-spade fives for a wheel (A-2-3-4-5 straight). This gives me roughly 48% equity. The pot odds are favorable - calling 300 to contest a 1200-chip pot offers approximately 5:1 odds, while my 12-out draw needs closer to 2:1. Flash has shown weakness by checking twice, suggesting he's likely checking down with a weak holding. While I'm out of position in the small blind, the deep effective stacks and strong draw justify continuing. If I miss on the turn, I can reassess, but for now, seeing another card with a premium draw is the mathematically sound play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit (holding the Ace of hearts). The board is Queen-high (Qh-3h-2c). After I checked, the Button (Minni) bet 300 into a 900 pot, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. The pot is now 1500, and it costs me 300 to call, giving me 5:1 pot odds. My hand has two overcards and the backdoor nut flush draw. Given the excellent price and the fact that my Ace-high still has significant equity against a wide range of hands from the Button and a potentially capped range from the Small Blind, a call is the most mathematically sound play. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn, looking for an Ace, Jack, or Heart to improve my hand.

Betting 900 on the turn with a completed wheel (A-2-3-4-5 straight). The 5s on the turn completed my primary draw from the flop. I'm betting for value against Minni's likely pair-based or draw-based range. While my hand is technically vulnerable to the river (sets or flush completions), betting now extracts immediate value while I have the best hand and takes initiative. Checking would give Minni a free card and dilute my equity. The 900 bet size is balanced - strong enough to get value but not so large that I'm overcommitting relative to the pot.

On the turn, the board is 3h-2c-Qh-5s. Claude (Small Blind) has led for 900 into a pot of 1800 after check-calling the flop. This lead often indicates a hand like a Queen, a set, or potentially a straight (A4), but it could also be a semi-bluff with a draw. I hold the nut flush draw (Ah) and one overcard (the Ace). The pot is offering me 4:1 odds (calling 900 to win a total pot of 3600). My equity with the nut flush draw alone is roughly 20%, and including the Ace outs gives me enough to justify a call, even with the risk of Minni (the flop aggressor) acting behind me. If I hit the Ace or a heart on the river, I have significant implied odds given Claude's shown interest in the pot.

I opened and c‑bet small on a dry Q-3-2 with K♠4♥ to take the pot; both called, so their ranges include hands with real showdown value (Qx, 3x, small pairs, some draws). On the turn Claude leads for 900 and Flash calls — pot is 3600, so a call costs 900 for ~4:1 odds (need ~20% equity). My hand has no pair and only a two-card backdoor heart possibility, so its equity vs two opponents continuing a value-heavy line is well below the break-even threshold. Raising into two opponents is thinly credible at best and I lack the blockers to make a bluff work; calling just to see a river leaves me in tough spots versus further pressure. Fold and preserve stack is the +EV play here.

Checking on the river with my completed wheel. While As4s makes a 5-high straight, this hand is vulnerable to a pair of queens (Flash's most likely holding given the 900 turn call) or any higher straight. Checking accomplishes multiple goals: (1) If Flash has a weaker hand (lower pair, etc.), Flash will likely check back and I win the pot; (2) If Flash has a queen or strong hand, checking leads to a showdown where I lose the minimum; (3) If I bet and Flash has a queen, Flash will call and I lose additional chips. The risk-reward favors controlling the pot size with a vulnerable hand on a completed board where Flash has likely strengthened.

On the river, the board is 3h-2c-Qh-5s-8d. Claude, who led the turn for 900 into two players, has checked the river. My hand is Ace-high with the Jack of diamonds and the Ace of hearts. While I missed my nut flush draw and my overcards didn't pair up, I have some showdown value against Claude's missed draws (like KhTh, KhJh, or other heart combinations). Claude's turn lead suggests he had either a made hand (like a Queen or a small pair) or a semi-bluff. By checking the river, he may be giving up on a bluff or looking to check-call with a marginal hand. Given the 3-handed dynamics and the fact that I block the nut flush with my Ace of hearts, betting as a bluff is risky against an opponent who has shown stickiness in previous hands. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and potentially win the pot if he was on a draw, without risking more chips against a hand that won't fold.

8-5 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant playing in most situations. While I have positional advantage on the button, this holding lacks the strength and playability needed. The hand has poor equity against typical blind ranges and would face significant difficulty post-flop. 8-5o is in the bottom tier of starting hands and should be folded from the button in standard strategy, especially when not needed to defend blind equity aggressively. Folding preserves chip value and maintains a solid win rate by avoiding marginal situations.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten suited, which is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to take control of the hand and extract value from the Big Blind's wider defending range.

Heads-up vs a standard SB open to 300. I have pocket 5s — a medium pocket pair that plays well by set-mining against a wide opening range from the small blind. With both stacks deep (≈90–110bb) I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop rather than 3‑bet and bloat the pot out of position with a hand that is often a one-pair hand on many flops. Calling costs me 200 more for a total commitment of 300, giving ~3:1 immediate pot odds and strong implied odds if I hit a set. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board with one overcard (Queen) and potential draws (flush and straight). Betting here serves as a continuation bet to protect my hand against free cards for draws and to extract value from weaker hands like lower pairs or straight/flush draws in the Big Blind's range. I will bet 300, which is half the current pot.

I flopped a set of fives (5♠5♣ on 5♥ Q♠ J♥) — an extremely strong, value-heavy hand on this dry texture. Flash opened from the SB and c-bet very small (300 into the pot), which indicates a wide, often vulnerable continuing range (Qx, Jx, Kx, Tx, suited connectors, and draws). I want to build the pot and charge all of those hands and draws while stacks are deep, so I will raise for value and protection. A raise to 1500 total (well above the 600 min-raise) is large enough to extract calls from top pairs and charge draws, yet leaves room to continue on later streets. If called I’ll keep extracting on favorable turns and re-evaluate against any strong action (big 3-bet/shove).

I hold middle pair (Jacks) on a wet board of 5h-Qs-Jh. While I have a decent hand, Minni has responded to my 300 continuation bet with a large 5x raise to 1500. This polarizes her range significantly toward either very strong hands (like QJ, 55, or a strong Queen) or high-equity draws (like KhTh or AhXh). Given that I am out of position and the board is very coordinated, calling 1200 more is risky as many turn cards will be difficult to play and I am likely behind her value range. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit a large portion of my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. This hand is significantly below the standard opening range for a button in a 3-handed game. Given the recent aggressive play from both Minni and Claude, attempting a steal with such a weak hand is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up, I’m the small blind (button post‑flop) with T♠8♦ — a very playable hand in heads‑up. With only Claude left in the big blind and a deep stack (~118 BB), I want to take the initiative and build the pot on my terms while retaining postflop position. Completing to call (total 100) surrenders initiative and gives Claude chances to dictate play; a min‑raise to 200 is a bit weak and invites wider counter‑pressure. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure and get value/folds from worse hands. Plan: if Claude calls we play postflop in position and c‑bet appropriate textures; if he 3‑bets large I’ll fold since T8o doesn’t fare well vs a strong 3‑bet range; consider a call vs a small sizing depending on reads.

Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. I'm in the big blind with A2o, which is a weak hand but has showdown value with the ace. 

Key considerations:
1. Pot odds: I need to call 200 into a 400-chip pot (2:1 odds, requiring ~33% equity). A2o likely has 30-35% equity against Minni's typical SB raising range, especially given her aggressive tendencies shown in the hand history.

2. Position: While I'm out of position postflop, I'm last to act preflop, which is valuable. Also, my large stack (19250) allows for flexibility postflop.

3. Opponent profiling: Minni has been quite aggressive throughout the session, raising frequently. This suggests her SB raise could include a wider range, making my ace-high more relevant.

4. Stack dynamics: With healthy stacks on all sides, I can afford to see a flop and play the hand based on the board texture.

5. Hand exploitability: Folding every weak ace from the blind would be exploitable. Calling with A2o against an aggressive opponent is reasonable from the blind.

The pot odds justify a call, and the ace provides both showdown and improvement value.

I was the preflop aggressor and now have position with T♠8♦ on a very dry Q♥2♦4♣ flop. This board misses a lot of the big blind’s calling range (many broadways and small pairs) and has little draw potential, so a continuation bet will fold many hands and preserves initiative. I size to ~50% pot (300 into 600) to get decent fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: take it down if he folds; if called I proceed cautiously — only continue on clear improvement (T/8) or very benign turns and be prepared to fold to heavy turn aggression since T8 has poor showdown equity against made hands.

Folding A2o on Q♥ 2♦ 4♣ flop. Bottom pair with weak kicker is vulnerable to Minni's likely range (QQ+, AQ, AK, draws). Out of position, I face unfavorable future street decisions while likely being behind. Marginal pot odds don't justify the combination of weak hand strength, poor position, and opponent likely having the best of it. Preserving stack for higher-equity spots is the disciplined play.

Button vs two blinds with 5♠2♠: this is a weak hand but suited and very playable in position. Standard exploitative play is to open a wide button range to steal the blinds and realize equity in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB) — large enough to apply pressure and discourage easy multiway calls, but small relative to stacks so I can fold to a polarized 3-bet without being committed. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (typical 3-bets here are ~700+); if called I’ll use position to c-bet dry boards, realize equity with backdoor flush/straight possibilities, and play cautiously on coordinated/overs cards. Calling 100 is too passive and misses opportunities to take the pot preflop; folding is too tight in this spot.

Folding 4h-8h from the small blind against Minni's raise. This is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position post-flop. With 4-8o, I lack the equity needed to justify calling 250 into a 450 pot with sub-35% equity against a reasonable raising range. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the session, and I'd be entering a multi-way pot with weak cards and positional disadvantage. With 150 hands total and a healthy 19k stack, I can afford to be selective and fold marginal hands. The risk/reward doesn't favor calling with this holding.

I am in the big blind with 3h 5h, which is a suited connector-type hand. The button (Minni) has opened for 300, requiring me to call another 200 to see a flop. Given the pot odds (200 to win 450, or roughly 2.25:1) and the fact that 3h 5h has some playability post-flop, a call is reasonable to defend the big blind, especially since we are deep-stacked. However, 3-5 suited is on the bottom edge of a defending range. Considering Minni's frequent opening raises and our position, calling is a standard way to see if we can hit a strong draw or a low two-pair/straight, although folding is also acceptable. I will call and see the flop.

I have 3h 5h on a Kc 8c 2c board, which is an extremely unfavorable texture for my hand. I have no pair, no draw, and no clubs. Minni raised pre-flop from the button and is likely to continue her aggression. Since I am out of position and have absolutely no equity in this pot, checking is the only logical move to see if Minni checks behind, though I will fold to any bet.

Flop is Kc‑8c‑2c and Flash checked to me. I’ve paired my 2 (5♠2♠) but it’s the absolute bottom pair with a weak kicker on a monotone club board. Flash’s defending range to a Button 3x open is wide and contains lots of club combinations, Ks/8s and some pocket pairs — many of which already beat me (flushes, Kx, 8x, sets) or have good equity against me. Betting has poor fold equity and just bloats the pot when I’m likely behind; checking preserves stack and lets me control the pot size and gather more info on the turn. Plan: check to pot‑control and call small turns to realize equity, but fold to significant aggression (especially if a club or a strong bet/raise arrives).

I have 3h 5h on a board of Kc 8c 2c Ts. I have no equity, no pair, and no clubs. The flop was checked through by both players, which suggests Minni might not have a strong hand or a high club. However, betting into the pre-flop aggressor with absolutely nothing on this texture is risky. I will check and hope to see a cheap river, though I am prepared to fold to any aggression.

I have 5♠2♠ — bottom pair (deuces) with a weak kicker on a three‑club flop (Kc‑8c‑2c) and a Ten of spades on the turn. Flash checked the flop and checked the turn, which is consistent with a wide defending range that includes lots of clubs, Kx/8x, and pocket pairs — many of those hands already beat me (flushes, Kx, sets) or have much more equity. The turn didn’t meaningfully improve me (still a vulnerable single pair) and I can’t make a spade flush with only one card to come. Betting here would have very little fold equity and will bloat the pot when I’m often behind; it also opens me up to larger river pressure. I’ll control the pot and keep the river cheap — check and evaluate the river card, calling small river bets or folding to large aggression. If the river is a harmless low off‑suit I may try a small value bet; if a club or K/T pairs the board I will be prepared to fold to strong action.

I have 5-high on a board of Kc 8c 2c Ts 7d. The hand has been checked through on the flop and turn, indicating that Minni likely does not have a strong hand or a flush. However, even with her checking, my 5-high has zero showdown value. While I could attempt a bluff to take down the 650 pot, the risk-to-reward ratio isn't favorable given I have no blockers to the flush or any high-card equity. I will check and hope for a check-back, though I expect to lose at showdown or fold to any bet.

I hold 5♠2♠ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) with a very weak kicker on a dry-ish river after a three‑club flop (Kc‑8c‑2c) and a Ts/7d runout. Flash called my preflop raise and then checked flop/turn/river, so his range is wide and includes made flushes (any two clubs), Kx/8x, and mid pocket pairs — many of which beat me. 4d Kd (K4o) is a weak hand, and while I'm in a favorable button position with decent pot odds (3:1 to call 100 into 150), this holding doesn't have sufficient equity to justify entering the pot against two opponents. Minni's check suggests she either missed the flop or has a marginal hand. Given the action patterns and her small blind call, she likely holds a weak pocket pair, Ax, or connected cards.

I should bet for value here. Top pair is worth playing for, and betting will:

2. Get information about Minni's holding
3. Charge draws and weak holdings

A standard flop bet of around 125-150 chips (roughly 1/2 to 3/4 of the pot) is appropriate. This is consistent with my aggressive but measured play shown in earlier hands (betting flops with strong holdings like top pair).

Facing a min raise to ~200 from the small blind with T8o on the button. I'm in the Small Blind with KhTs, having raised preflop to 200, and Minni called. Flash folded, so this is heads-up on the flop of Jc4hQd.

Betting 300 is optimal here.

4c9h is a weak hand that doesn't justify further investment even from the button position. I am in the Small Blind with a small pocket pair (4d 4h). Claude has already folded from the button. The pot is 150 and it is 50 for me to call the big blind, or I can raise. I have Qc9d on Qd9sJs — top two pair in position. Heads-up from the small blind with J♥9♣ — a hand with decent playability that belongs in my raising range. Minni has min-raised from the Small Blind (raising from 50 to just 200), which is a weak raise pattern that often indicates either a marginal holding or a probe bet. Minni's flop min-raise from 50 to 200 is a classic weakness indicator on a K-7-5 board. This sizing suggests either a marginal holding or a probe bet rather than a strong hand. I have J♥9♣ on a K♥ 7♦ 5♠ 4♣ board. I missed — no pair, only one heart and no meaningful draw. Minni checked the turn on a 7d-Kh-5s-4c board after his flop min-raise. My hand is 3c 5c, giving me a pair of fives with 7 outs to improve (4 sixes for a straight to 3-4-5-6-7, and 3 remaining threes for two pair).

This is a straightforward check-through situation. My analysis:

1. **Hand Strength**: Pair of 5s is mediocre - it has showdown value but likely trails most of Minni's continuing range. I'm not in a position to bet for value.

2. **Minni's Line**: He min-raised the flop (weakness indicator) then checked the turn (further weakness). A turn bet would not drive out weaker hands that I'm actually beating, and stronger hands (like pairs of Kings, sets, or straights) won't fold.

3. **Equity & Strategy**: With 7 outs to improve on the river and favorable stack-to-pot ratio, I should take the free river card. If I hit a 6 (straight) or 3 (two pair), I'll have a strong hand. If I miss, I still have showdown value with pair of 5s.

4. **Pot Management**: The pot is 800 chips. Betting here would commit chips with a marginal holding against a player showing consistent weakness. Better to see the river and win or lose the current pot rather than inflating it with a mediocre holding.

Checking is optimal - it preserves my equity, takes a free card, and maintains position for river decision-making.

I have J♥9♣ and missed every street on K♥ 7♦ 5♠ 4♣ 10♦. Pot is 800 and there is no bet to call. Checking the river with bottom pair (5s-3) against Minni's extremely weak line of min-raises and checks. Three-handed on the button with T♣5♦ (T5o) — marginal but a clear steal/opportunity spot. On a board of As-Kd-9s, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing any Jack). Minni, the pre-flop raiser, has made a relatively small continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot. I have the nut straight (10-J-Q-K-A) on a board of 9s-Kd-Ah-Js-Th. The board contains four cards to a straight (9-10-J-K-A), and any Queen completes the Broadway straight. Q5o (Queen-Five offsuit) is a weak hand in Small Blind after Minni's raise from Button. While the cost to call is only 250 chips, I'm playing out of position with marginal hand strength.
