5. Expected value: Q5o will struggle against Minni's range and Flash could 3-bet or call behind me

This is a disciplined fold. With 150 hands to play, I should preserve chips for premium situations rather than gamble with weak hands out of position. Folding keeps me healthy and focused on value-oriented decisions.

I am in the Big Blind holding K7o. Minni raised from the button to 300, and Claude folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.25 to 1. K7o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise, as Minni likely has a wide range of opening hands. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the strength of the King and the price offered make calling the most standard and profitable play.

On a paired board of Th-6d-Ts, I have King-high (Kh7c) with no direct draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out into a paired board usually doesn't accomplish much with my current hand strength. I will check and see how Minni reacts.

I opened wide preflop and have position; board is T♥ 6♦ T♠ — dry, paired, and not favorable for my 9♣5♠ (no pair, no meaningful draw). As the aggressor I should c-bet a small size to leverage fold equity and keep the initiative rather than giving Flash a free turn card. A ~1/3-pot continuation bet targets his missed Broadway/connector holdings and small pairs that will fold, while keeping my investment small with essentially zero showdown value. Plan: bet 225 into 650. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and give up to heavy action or large raises since I have almost no equity; if he check-raises I will concede unless it’s a very small raise I can exploit.

On the flop of Th-6d-Ts, I have King-high. Minni has continued for a small sizing (225 into 650, approximately 1/3 pot). This is a standard continuation bet on a paired board where the range of the pre-flop aggressor often has an advantage. However, my King-high has decent showdown value and beats all of her air/bluffs like high-card hands lower than a King. With pot odds of nearly 4:1 (225 to win a total pot that will be 1100), folding is too tight. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on the next card and her subsequent action.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds has improved my hand to top pair (Kings and Tens). While my kicker (7) is weak, top pair in a blind-vs-button battle is a very strong hand. I am out of position, so I will check to the aggressor. This allows Minni to continue bluffing with her air or smaller pairs, and it protects my range. If she bets, I will comfortably call to see the river.

I opened wide preflop to take the initiative and c-bet a small 225 on the T♥6♦T♠ flop to fold out overcards and gain fold equity. Flash called the flop, which keeps a lot of hands in (pocket pairs, tens, some Kx, broadways and occasional drawing combos). The K♦ on the turn significantly improves parts of his calling range (any Kx now has top pair; pocket pairs and tens still beat me), and his check suggests either pot control with a real hand or a refusal to fold marginal holdings. I have essentially no showdown value or meaningful equity vs that range, so barreling again has low fold equity and just risks more chips when I'm behind. I’ll check behind to control the pot, preserve my stack, and re-evaluate on the river — looking to bluff only on very favorable river cards or to fold to substantive aggression.

On the river 8d, the board is Th-6d-Ts-Kd-8d. I have top pair (Kings) with Tens on the board. Minni checked back the turn, which typically indicates a medium-strength hand or a hand looking to get to showdown cheaply. By checking the river, I allow her to potentially bluff with her missed draws or air, while also keeping the pot small in case she was slow-playing a Ten or happened to back into a flush (though unlikely given the turn check). If she checks back, I expect my Kings to be the winning hand most of the time.

I have nothing (9♣5♠) and zero showdown value on T♥6♦T♠ K♦ 8♦. Checking guarantees I'm often losing at showdown to Kx, any T and most pocket pairs. Flash’s line — calling my small c-bet on the flop then checking both the K-turn and the 8-river — looks like pot control with marginal/medium hands (Kx, small pairs) or a slow-played strong holding. The river completed diamond draws, but his check makes a flush less likely. I should bluff a portion of the time to maximize EV rather than auto-check. I’ll make a half‑pot river bet (550 into 1100): big enough to apply pressure and fold out Kx and medium pairs, small enough to keep risk reasonable if called. If he raises, I’ll fold — this is a pure value-seizing bluff. Pot 1100; bet 550.

On a board of Th-6d-Ts-Kd-8d, I have top pair (Kings) with a 7 kicker. In a blind-versus-button situation, this is a very strong hand. Minni's line is interesting: she continuation-bet the flop, checked back the turn when the King hit, and is now betting 1/3 of the pot (550 into 1100) on the river. Her turn check suggests she either has a medium-strength hand (like a weak King or a mid-pair), is slow-playing a monster (like a Ten), or has given up with air. By betting the river after I checked, she could be value-betting a King with a better kicker, but she could also be trying to buy the pot with missed draws or under-pairs, assuming my checks mean I have nothing. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the strength of top pair in this scenario, folding is not an option. Raising is unlikely to get called by worse hands. Calling allows me to win against her bluffs and thin value bets while losing the minimum if she has me outkicked or has a Ten.

Raising with 8-Jack offsuit from the button. While this hand is marginal, I'm in the best position with a healthy stack (19,600) against two opponents with similar, slightly smaller stacks (~10k each). A standard 3x BB raise (300) is appropriate to apply pressure, take down the blinds, or play heads-up in position post-flop. From the button, I have significant positional advantage that makes this hand playable. This is a standard opening strategy that balances my range while applying healthy aggression with my stack advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o. Claude, the button, has raised to 3x. 92o is one of the weakest hands in poker and is easily dominated. Even with decent pot odds, playing this hand out of position against a strong opponent like Claude is a losing strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 6♦3♥ in the big blind facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. This hand has virtually no playability OOP (poor high cards, weak straight/flush potential) and will be dominated by much of the button’s opening range. Calling 200 to see a flop gives marginal pot odds but forces multi-street decisions out of position with little equity to realize; a 3-bet bluff is also a poor choice because I don't hold relevant blockers. With deep stacks and many hands left, conserving chips is the +EV play — fold.

On the button with As8s, I have a very strong opening hand in a three-handed game. It's well-suited and provides nut flush potential. I will raise to 300, which is my standard open size, to build the pot and take control of the hand in position.

Jh7o is a weak, unsuited J-x with very poor playability out of position. Flash's button open to 3bb is standard and contains lots of hands that dominate mine; Claude on the big blind often defends, making this likely a multiway OOP spot. Calling 250 to see a flop here is marginal (and I’ve already invested 50), and a min-raise to 500 would be an overcommitment with little equity or fold equity vs two players. With a healthy stack and many better spots to exploit later, I fold and preserve chips for higher EV situations.

I'm in the big blind with 64o (the worst starting hand in poker), facing a 300 chip raise from Flash on the button. After Minni folded from the small blind, this is now heads-up. 

Key factors:
1. 64 unsuited is fundamentally weak - poor playability and low showdown value
2. I'm out of position (BB vs button raiser) - significant positional disadvantage
3. Flash's button raise is standard aggression; I don't have enough information to assume they have a weak hand
4. Calling 200 more to win 450 in a pot is 2.25:1, but with 64o OOP, my win rate is too low to justify calling
5. Even heads-up, this hand plays poorly out of position postflop

This is a straightforward fold. 64o is a defensive-fold hand from the big blind, and nothing about the action suggests I should deviate. I should preserve chips for better spots.

8♣4♣ is a very weak, low-suited hand. Against two players left to act — especially with Claude (SB) who has shown aggressive 3-betting and Flash (BB) who defends — I don't want to inflate the pot with a raise and risk a 3-bet or multiway call. Calling 100 is a small, cheap investment (~1% of my 10,050 stack) that lets me see a flop in position. From there I can fold to heavy action or try to realize equity if the flop is favorable. A fold is too tight for the price; a raise is too spewy with this holding. Call 100.

Calling to complete the small blind with Q7o. Getting 5:1 pot odds (250:50) which is profitable with this holding. In a 3-player game with a button limper, I have reasonable implied odds to see a flop and can make decisions based on my hand strength and post-flop action. Q7 can make top pair or gutshots on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 2h. The pot is limped by both Minni (Button) and Claude (Small Blind). I have the option to check and see a free flop with a very weak hand. There is no reason to bluff or build a pot with this hand pre-flop, as it performs poorly post-flop and the stack-to-pot ratio is high. Checking is the standard and most profitable play here to see if I can hit something unlikely on the flop.

Checking Q7o on a 3s-As-4h flop with two opponents. This is a marginal hand that doesn't have a made hand or strong draw potential. The Ace on board is concerning. Betting would be speculatively representing strength, which is risky against two opponents. Checking allows me to control pot size, gather information about opponent holdings, and get a free card to improve. With plenty of chips in a 3-handed game early in the session, this is the prudent play to preserve equity and make better decisions post-flop based on opponent actions.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot with 4d 2h. The flop is 3s As 4h, giving me bottom pair (fours) with a deuce kicker. This is a very weak made hand, but in a limped pot, it has some showdown value. Claude (Small Blind) has checked to me. Minni (Button), the pre-flop limper, is still to act. My hand is not strong enough to bet for value, and betting as a bluff doesn't make much sense since I have a piece of the board. Checking is the best option to see how the button reacts and to keep the pot small while trying to improve or reach a cheap showdown.

I have 8♣4♣ on A♠3♠4♥ — middle pair with a weak kicker. Both opponents checked, so their ranges are likely wide and include many missed hands, but this is still a vulnerable one-pair holding on an Ace-high board. I want to take the initiative and extract value from worse pairs and unpaired hands while denying free cards to overcards/backdoor draws, but I don’t want to bloat the pot multiway. A small c-bet (~1/2 pot) achieves that: it charges floats and weak holdings, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me fold to significant aggression. If I face a large raise from Claude (who has shown aggression) I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn with positional advantage.

Folding Q7o on this flop. While I had reasonable pot odds preflop to complete the small blind and checked flop to gather information, Minni's raise reveals strength on a board with an Ace that I cannot beat. I have no made hand (no pair), no flush draw (would need another spade for that), and no straight draw. My queen is a weak kicker at best. Given that I'm out of position facing a raise from Minni, and Flash is still to act behind me, this is not a profitable spot to continue. My Q-high essentially has showdown value only and is vulnerable to continued aggression. With 19,550 chips and 150 hands to play, folding marginal hands out of position is profitable long-term strategy.

In this limped pot, I have bottom pair (fours) with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni has bet 150 into a 300 pot. This is a small bet, but my hand is extremely vulnerable and difficult to play on future streets. I have zero potential to improve to a strong hand (two pair or trips) that wouldn't be beat by an Ace or a higher pair. While the pot odds are 3:1, calling with bottom pair on an Ace-high board against a player who has shown aggression throughout the match is generally a losing play. It's better to let this go and wait for a more favorable spot rather than getting bogged down in a marginal situation with a very weak hand.

On the button with 5-9 offsuit, I'm calling 100 to win 250 with 2.5:1 pot odds. Position is significant here—I can see what the blinds do postflop and make better decisions. This hand has reasonable equity to see a flop and potential to improve. My deep stack allows me to fold postflop if needed, making this a positionally profitable spot to see the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 87s, a very strong speculative hand in a three-way pot. The Button (Claude) has limped in, and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds and the playability of suited connectors, calling is standard, though a raise could also be justified to take control. However, with the Big Blind still to act and a deep-stacked limper on the Button, I will complete my small blind to keep the pot manageable and look to hit a strong flop.

I’m in the big blind with K♥3♦ facing two limpers (button called 100, SB called 50). Pot is small (300) and there’s no raise — this is a multiway, out-of-position spot with a weak kicker. K3o has little value to build a pot preflop; a raise to isolate would be thin and bloats a pot I’d likely play OOP postflop against deeper stacks. I’ll check and see a cheap flop, plan to fold to significant aggression if I miss, defend or extract value selectively if I hit a king (be cautious with the 3 kicker), and use position/stack dynamics to choose lines on later streets.

The flop is 6c 3h 3s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 or 5) and one overcard (the 8c is technically higher than the board, but 7 and 8 are both overs to the 6). In a limped pot, no one has shown significant strength. Checking is the most standard play here to see how the other players react. I have a backdoor club flush draw as well, so there is some equity, but not enough to lead into two opponents on a paired board where someone might easily have a 3 or a pair of 6s.

Flop: 6c 3h 3s. I hold 3d Kh — trips with a top kicker. Both opponents limped preflop and Flash checked the flop; this is an extremely strong hand in a multiway pot. Only a very small number of combos (pocket 66 or a 3+6 combination) can already beat me, so I should bet for value and to deny free cards. I want a bet size that keeps worse hands and medium pairs in but charges any draws, so I'll bet a medium-pot amount to build the pot and extract value. If I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely call small–medium raises, fold only to an extreme line that strongly represents the few full-house combos).

With 5-9 offsuit on a 6-3-3 paired flop, I have no made hand (9-high) and no meaningful drawing potential. I lack flush draws, straight draws, or even a gutshot. Minni's aggressive raise to 400 from the big blind on a relatively coordinated flop suggests she has a strong hand—likely a pocket pair, 6x, or strong overcards. My equity against her raising range is significantly below the 36% needed to call the pot odds (700:400 = 1.75:1). Additionally, I'm out of position and vulnerable to further aggression. This is a straightforward fold—no hand, no draw, poor equity, and poor position. With 19k chips, I can afford to fold weak holdings and wait for better spots.

I have 7-high with two overcards to the board's 6 and a backdoor club flush draw. Minni, the Big Blind, has led for 400 into a 300 pot, which is an overpot bet (133% of the pot). This sizing on a paired board (6-3-3) usually indicates either a strong hand (like a 3 or a pair of 6s) or a polarized bluff. Given that I have very little equity and am out of position, calling such a large bet with only backdoor draws is mathematically incorrect. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not perform well against typical defending ranges from the blinds. While a button open can often steal the blinds, 96o is at the bottom of the range and generally considered a fold in a standard three-handed game. I will preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I’m in the small blind heads-up with Tc5d and the button has folded. Heads-up I should open a wide range; T5o is marginal but playable and raising takes the initiative. I’ll raise to the legal minimum, 200 (2x BB), to deny a free flop, apply pressure, and keep pot control with a small investment relative to our 10.4k stack. If Claude folds we pick up the blinds; if he calls we proceed cautiously OOP, c-betting favorable textures and folding to large preflop 3-bets since this hand has limited equity versus strong ranges.

8-6 offsuit is a weak, non-premium hand that falls below my defending range from the big blind. Against Minni's raise from the small blind, this hand lacks the strength, connectivity, or suitedness to play profitably. Being out of position postflop further compounds the weakness. Only needing to call 100 more is not sufficient compensation for the poor hand quality and positional disadvantage. With a strong stack of 19,350, I can afford to wait for better opportunities.

Button in 3-handed — open a wide range. K♣7♦ (K7o) has a useful K-blocker and plays well in position. The table standard and previous action favor a 3x open. Raising to 300 (3x BB) maximizes fold equity vs the blinds, keeps our range uncapped, and gives us position postflop. Plan: if both blinds fold we take the pot; if called I'll C-bet most favorable flops and play straightforwardly for value/protection; if I face a sizable 3-bet from Claude or Flash I’ll fold (their 3-bet range is likely stronger), but I can consider a call versus a small isolate depending on stack-to-pot and player tendencies. 300 keeps lines clean and exploits our button advantage.

With pocket jacks in the small blind facing a button raise, I'm raising to 850 total. This is a standard 2.8x reraise that applies pressure, builds the pot with a premium hand, and establishes hand strength. JJ is a top-tier holding that should be played aggressively preflop. The raise size is appropriate given stack depths and pot dynamics, with good potential to either take it down immediately or play a 3-handed pot with position advantage post-flop.

I am in the big blind holding Q4o. The button (Minni) opened to 300, and the small blind (Claude) 3-bet to 850. This is a clear fold. My hand has very poor equity against two aggressive ranges, especially since Claude is 3-betting from the small blind into a button open. There is no reason to put any chips into the pot with such a weak hand out of position.

Fold. Claude’s 3‑bet to 850 is a sizable, likely value‑heavy/ polarised response to my button open. K♥7♦ offsuit is marginal and is often dominated by the kinds of hands he 3‑bets (broadways, strong Ks, pocket pairs). While I’d normally exploit position with a call against a light isolate, this hand has poor multi‑street playability and weak kicker equity. A 4‑bet would be a thin bluff (I lack the right blockers) and calling leaves me playing a dominated hand out of necessity. Per my preflop plan, preserve the stack and fold to a sizable 3‑bet.

From the button with A4o, I'm in excellent position. While this hand isn't premium, position is crucial in poker. A standard preflop raise (3x BB = 300) accomplishes several things:

1. **Position advantage:** Acting last preflop and postflop gives me maximum information to make decisions
2. **Aggression:** I've been playing well and winning pots with aggressive play (see hands #1, #13, #14). Button raises put pressure on the blinds.
3. **Win the blinds:** Many times the blinds will fold immediately, winning 150 chips with a clean pot
4. **Equity if called:** If called heads-up, ace-high with position is playable. I can control the pot size and leverage position on later streets

Looking at the recent action, Minni is the big blind - they've been active but I have good reads. A raise to 300 is standard button play and maintains my aggression. The stack sizes are reasonable (19.7k for me vs ~10k for opponents), so I can afford to raise.

Folding from the button with any ace would be too tight and waste this premium position.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 3h. Claude, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a weak suited-connector/one-gapper that does not play well out of position against a strong opponent like Claude, who has a significantly larger stack. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid being trapped in a bloated pot with a marginal hand.

Claude opened to 300 from the button, SB folded — I’m in the BB with K♣J♠ and ~101bb effective. Folding is too weak; calling 200 keeps me out of position and lets Claude see cheap flops. KJ offsuit is a strong hand vs a wide button open, so I want to take the initiative and extract value / fold out weaker holdings. I’ll 3-bet to 1,000 (≈3.3x) — big enough to generate meaningful fold equity and charge broadways/small pairs, but small enough to leave room for post‑flop play with ~90bb behind. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll c-bet dry boards for value and control size on wet/connected boards, and fold to clear heavy action if the board runs out very bad for KJ.

Calling 700 more (1000 total) to see a flop. With A4o, I have roughly 33-35% equity against Minni's likely 3-bet range when accounting for hands like AA, AK, QQ, JJ, and other strong/medium hands. The pot odds of roughly 3:1 are acceptable. Though out of position is a drawback, my strong stack (19.45k) gives me flexibility to play cautiously postflop. Folding would abandon showdown value and waste my initial button raise investment. This is a call-down situation where I can control post-flop play based on the board texture.

I’m the preflop 3‑bettor (K♣J♠) on a 9♦‑8♥‑6♠ flop, heads‑up, acting first. The board is connected but still misses a lot of the button’s calling range (broadways, A‑x, many suited broadways), and I have two overcards and a small backdoor spade. I want to apply pressure and fold out missed hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. A small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) achieves good fold equity and leaves room to fold to heavy resistance or continue if I improve or get a favourable turn. Plan: bet a small c‑bet (700). If he folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn — barrel only if I improve or the turn is safe for my range, otherwise check/control and fold to large aggression since KJ has limited redraw equity.

Folding A4o out of position. While pot odds are marginally acceptable at 3.9:1, my hand has insufficient equity (~25%) and weak showdown value against Minni's strong 3-bet range. Out of position with a weak hand facing aggression is a clear fold spot. I preserve my 18.75k stack for better opportunities where I have edge (premium hands, better position, clearer situations).

I am on the button with 3c 2s, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My opponents in the blinds, Minni and Claude, have been relatively active and aggressive in previous hands. Given that the pot is only 150 and I am holding a junk hand, there is no reason to put any money into the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Holding Queen of Spades / 3 of Hearts — a weak, offsuit Q-rag with poor kicker and little connectivity. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to see a flop (good pot odds). Claude in the big blind is capable and defends widely, so raising from the small blind with this holding would often get me 3-bet or put me out of position in a bloated pot. Best line is to call the 50 to see a flop cheaply. Postflop plan: continue only with real equity (top pair with a decent kicker, two pair or better, or strong draws). On dry boards or against heavy aggression I will fold marginal hands; if Claude checks a favourable board I may c-bet to seize initiative.

QJ offsuit is a marginal hand in this situation. Minni's small blind call shows weakness and doesn't warrant me raising with a non-premium hand. Checking allows me to see the flop cost-effectively. While I'm out of position post-flop, keeping the pot small with a marginal hand is prudent. I can evaluate the board texture and Minni's actions to determine my next move.

Check. Holding Qs/3h on 9h Ah Th is very marginal: I have no pair, only one heart and no straight. With three hearts on board I have 9 heart outs to make a flush on the turn/river (~35% by river) and 3 queens to pair (~12% by river), but pairing a queen will often still be behind Ax or Tx. Claude checked preflop and defends widely, so his continuing range contains many A/T/9 combinations that will call a c-bet — betting here will bloat the pot with little fold equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see a turn. Plan: call small probes that give correct pot odds to realize equity, fold to large polarized bets, and become aggressive only if I hit a heart, pair a queen, or get a favorable turn texture.

With QJ on a 9h-Ah-Th board, my hand strength is quite marginal. The Ace severely limits my holding - I'm essentially queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (any King completes my straight for 4 outs). While I do have some equity and potential, this is not a hand that warrants a bet in this spot. Minni's check signals weakness, likely indicating they either missed the flop entirely or have a vulnerable draw. Betting here doesn't extract value from better hands and would lose to any hand that's already ahead (any pair, any ace, any king). Checking allows me to see the turn card cheaply while maintaining pot control with my marginal holding. Given I'm out of position and my hand has legitimate draw equity plus some showdown value, a check is the disciplined play to evaluate the next street without bloating the pot.

Check. I have Qs3h on 9h Ah Th Ts — nothing improved on the turn and I only have a one-heart backdoor/queen runner draw. On the river I have 9 heart outs + 3 queens - 1 overlapping Qh = 11 unique outs → 11/46 ≈ 24% naive equity, but many of those queen outs are poor against Ax or Tx and my real hand strength vs Claude's defended range is lower. Claude checked flop and likely defends a wide, often-A/T-heavy range; betting here will bloat the pot with weak equity and invite calls or check-raises from dominating hands. Best line is pot control: check to see a cheap river and only continue with a clean improvement (heart or a queen) or call very small probes that price me correctly. I will fold to any large polarized bet and reassess on the river.

With QcJs on a 9h-Ah-Th-Ts board, I hold queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (4 Kings give me a straight, plus 6 more outs with remaining Queens/Jacks for pair). Minni's two checks indicate weakness—likely a missed hand or marginal holding. The pot is only 200, and I'm out of position. Given that: (1) my hand is marginal and needs to improve, (2) Minni has shown weakness twice, (3) betting would likely only extract value from better hands or scare away worse hands, and (4) I have legitimate equity with my draws, checking is the disciplined play. This allows me to see the river card cost-free while maintaining pot control. If a King, Queen, or Jack completes on the river, I'll have made a strong hand and can bet for value. If the river blanks and Minni checks, I can check back safely with my showdown value.

I have Q♠3♥ and missed completely on the river (board: 9♥ A♥ T♥ T♠ 9♦). This is a double‑paired board (Tens and Nines with an Ace) that gives many hands in Claude’s check‑through range at least a pair (A, T, 9) or even a full house. Claude checked every street — that could be weak or a slow‑played strong hand — so a bluff is very thin (I have no blockers to 9/T/A) and is likely to be called by Ax/Tx or punished by a check‑raise. The pot is small (200) relative to my stack, so risking chips on a marginal bluff is negative EV. I’ll check to avoid getting bluffed out or creating a bigger pot with no showdown value.

On the river with Qc-Js on a 9h-Ah-Th-Ts-9d board, I hold queen-high (essentially the board's 10-10-9-9-A). Minni has checked three consecutive streets - flop, turn, and now river - which is an extremely weak action pattern indicating either a missed hand or a hand they're giving up on. Given this analysis: (1) My hand (Q-high with queen kicker) is marginal showdown value at best; (2) Minni's three checks strongly suggest weakness and an intention to go to showdown cheaply; (3) Betting here extracts minimal value from better hands and likely wins only against hands I was already crushing; (4) The pot is small (200) and doesn't justify aggressive value-building with a marginal holding; (5) Checking allows me to go to showdown where Minni's weak action pattern suggests I might win the pot or at minimum see what they have. This is a disciplined check-back that respects Minni's weakness while avoiding bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Button, 3-handed, Q7h is a playable suited queen with good position. Limping (call 100) gives up initiative and lets the blinds see a cheap flop; opening raises here are standard to seize fold equity and play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) — big enough to discourage light defense, small enough to keep poorer hands and draws in. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, play cautiously vs large 3-bets (fold to heavy 3-bet unless odds/reads justify), extract value from worse queens and heart draws when called.

With Q2o (Queen-deuce offsuit) from the small blind, this is a weak hand to play out of position. Minni's button raise could contain a wide range of hands, and Flash still has to act from the big blind, creating additional complexity. Q2 doesn't have good equity against a raising range and is particularly vulnerable out of position. With a solid 18700 chip stack and only 40 hands remaining (we're at hand 51 of 150), there's no need to gamble with marginal holdings. I'm folding to preserve chips for better spots where I have positional and hand strength advantages.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Minni opened from the Button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds (2.25:1) to call, J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni has been playing aggressively, and defending such a marginal hand will likely lead to difficult decisions on later streets where I will be at a disadvantage. Position is critical in poker, and I'm on the button - the best position available. Quads/set combos are possible but unlikely given the lack of turn/river aggression. Betting a small–medium size extracts value from worse Jx, lower pairs (8x, 6x) and some A‑x that will call, while a large bet would fold out many of those hands and only get called by stronger hands (trips/quads). I’ll make a value bet around half the pot (1600 into 3250). If I face a large raise I’ll heavily discount my hand (check‑raise would be very polarized to beats) and fold; if called, I expect to win most showdowns against the caller’s range.

I hold top pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker on a board of Js-6c-2h-2s-8h. Minni has triple-barreled, betting 1600 into a pot of 3250 on the river. My hand is essentially a bluff-catcher at this point, as I lose to almost any other Jack and any set or trips. However, in a 3-handed game, top pair is a very strong hand to fold on such a dry board. The pot odds are roughly 3:1, meaning I only need to be right about 25% of the time to justify a call. Minni has shown high aggression in previous hands, and there are several missed gutshots (54, 43) or overcard hands (KQ, KT, QT) that she could be turning into a bluff. Given the board texture and the frequency of her betting, I believe my hand is good often enough to make the call.

Raising on the button with K-J is profitable. I have positional advantage and a solid hand. A 3x BB raise (300 chips) is standard and allows me to either take down the blinds or play a stronger position post-flop. This is a clear raise situation given my position and hand strength.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four suited. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand has some post-flop playability due to being suited, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the original raiser. Given my current stack size and the fact that I just lost a significant pot in the previous hand holding a similar Jack-Four offsuit, I should play more conservatively. Folding here avoids being caught out of position with a marginal hand.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Qc8h. Calling the extra 200 (total commitment 300) is the best play: I only need ~31% equity to break even and Q8o has reasonable equity and playability vs a BTN open. Claude’s 3x size indicates a fairly wide range so I can realize equity and get implied odds with deep stacks (16.9k). I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position with a marginal hand, and folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call and play the flop cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws or turn-based hands, check/float on dry boards, and fold to heavy sustained aggression when missed.

Check. I’m OOP with Qc8h on Jc7c5s — no pair, one overcard to the 7/5 and only a backdoor club draw (Qc + two clubs on the flop needs two running clubs). I called preflop to realize equity vs Claude’s wide 3x BTN range; on this dry/paired-low board I prefer pot control and the option to float. Checking preserves stack depth, lets me see a free turn, and gives me the chance to call a reasonable C-bet and bluff-catch if I pick up a Q or a club. Plan: check-call small-to-medium C-bets (roughly ≤1/2 pot ≈ 300–325) to see the turn; fold to large, pressure-dependent bets or raises OOP since my hand is marginal. If checked to, I’ll use the turn to continue only with a pair (Q), a club, or a clear improvement; otherwise keep the pot small and avoid bluff-raising with this holding.

I raised preflop with K♠J♥ on the button and got called by Minni from the big blind. The flop came 7♣J♣5♠, giving me top pair with the top kicker - a strong hand. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a draw-heavy holding. This is a perfect spot for a continuation bet. The board has some flush draw possibilities (two clubs), but I'm not concerned about those right now. A bet of 350 chips (approximately 53% of the 650-chip pot) is a standard c-bet sizing that extracts value from weaker pairs, charges any draws, and maintains my aggressive narrative as the preflop aggressor. This is straightforward value with a premium hand on a favorable flop.

Claude (BTN) fires 350 into the pot (pot was 650, now 1000). A call of 350 requires ~35% equity to break even (350/1000). My Qc8h has very limited equity: ~24% to pair by the river (3 Qs + 3 8s) plus only a tiny (~4%) runner-runner club backdoor, so total equity is roughly ~28%, well below the 35% needed. Claude’s button open and c-bet sizing includes many hands that dominate or have decent equity (Jx, pairs, KQ/AQ, clubs), and I’m out of position with a marginal hand. I planned to check‑call only small-to-medium c-bets (≈≤1/2 pot); this bet is slightly larger and not profitable to chase. Fold and conserve chips for better spots OOP.

I am holding 7h 2h on the button. While it is a suited hand, 72 is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but as the shortest stack at the table, I should avoid playing such low-equity hands. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Heads-up from the small blind with T♠3♥: a weak hand, but in heads-up I should be opening a wide range to seize initiative and use my postflop positional advantage. Raising to the minimum open (200 total) gives me fold equity, keeps the pot modest with a marginal hand, and lets me c-bet favorable flops or check and control the pot on dry/high boards. If Claude 3-bets large I will concede the hand; if he calls I will proceed cautiously, c-betting selective textures and folding to heavy aggression. Raising is better than simply limping here — limp would be passive and allow the BB to dictate play.

I'm facing a 200 raise from Minni in the small blind. With Q9o in the big blind, this is a marginal hand, but several factors favor a call: (1) Pot odds are favorable at 3:1 (300 chips to call 100), (2) Minni has been aggressive throughout the session and could be raising light, (3) My stack (16,800) is deep and similar to Minni's (16,550), allowing me to outplay post-flop, (4) Q-9 has decent equity with possibilities to make a straight or pair, (5) Position disadvantage is mitigated by stack depth and fold equity post-flop. A fold would be overly conservative given the odds and dynamics. I'll call and evaluate the flop.

Flop is 7d 5d 6d — an extremely wet, monotone diamond board that heavily favors the BB calling range. Claude called my wide SB open, so his range contains lots of diamonds, medium pairs (55/66/77), and strong draws. My T♠3♥ has no diamonds, no pair and essentially no realistic backdoor equity. This is a classic spot to take a passive line: c-betting here is thin and will be called or raised by too many strong hands. I will check to control the pot and preserve my stack. Plan: give up to any meaningful continuation bet (I have almost no equity), re-evaluate if he checks back and consider a delayed bluff only on safe turn cards.

With Q9o on a 7d-5d-6d flop, I have a weak holding (Q-high) and limited equity. While I have a gutshot straight draw (any 8 wins the hand), the board is highly coordinated with three diamonds and three cards in sequence. Minni's check from the small blind suggests weakness or a hand that needs to improve. Given this dynamic, I should check back to: (1) keep the pot small with a marginal hand, (2) avoid putting more money into a pot where I'm likely behind any pair or drawing hand Minni might hold, (3) control the pace and see what the turn brings. If an 8 comes, I make a straight. If a diamond comes, I improve slightly. This is a good spot to take a free card and reassess on the turn based on board development and Minni's action.

I checked the flop because the 7d-5d-6d board was highly weighted toward Claude's calling range (lots of diamonds, medium pairs and strong draws) and my T3 had almost no equity. Turn pairs my Ten, giving me top pair with a weak kicker in a heads-up pot. This is a spot to charge one-card diamond draws and weaker pairs while keeping the pot under control—checking back lets single-diamond hands see a free river and gives flushes a free card to bluff on later. I will bet roughly half the pot (200) to get value from worse pairs and charge draws. If Claude check-raises large (representing a flush or set) I'll concede and fold; if he calls I will re-evaluate on the river and proceed with thin value or pot control depending on the river texture (fold to heavy turn shove, call small raises to see a cheap river).

With Q9 on a board of 7d-5d-6d-Th, I'm facing a 200 bet on the turn. While I currently hold Q-high (a marginal hand), several factors favor a call:

1. **Pot Odds**: 200 to win 800 (600 current pot + 200 call) = 4:1 odds. I need only 20% equity to break even.

2. **Equity Assessment**: Q9 has approximately 20-25% equity with:
   - 4 clean 8-outs for a gutshot straight (any 8 makes Broadway)
   - 3 Queen outs for top pair
   - Potential additional equity with Q-high itself

3. **Minni's Range**: Minni checked the flop with a coordinated three-diamond board, then bet the turn after the 10h appeared. This pattern suggests either:
   - A medium-strength hand (like a pair of 10s, or a diamond draw)
   - A marginal hand that improved on the turn
   - Possible semi-bluff with a draw

4. **Stack Depth**: Both stacks are deep (16,700 vs 16,350), enabling post-river decisions with position.

5. **Position**: I act last on the river, giving me information advantage for final street play.

Folding would be overly conservative given favorable odds and equity. Raising with Q9 is risky. Calling maximizes my expected value and keeps the hand alive with one card to improve.

I have T♠3♥ on a river of 7♦-5♦-6♦-T♥-T♦. I made a small turn bet and Claude called — his calling range contains many hands that benefit hugely from this river: any single diamond in his hand now makes a 5‑card flush (the river is the 4th diamond on board), and pocket 7s/6s/5s (or other pocket pairs) convert into full houses when the board pairs tens. My trips are strong versus many missed hands, but extremely vulnerable to those flushes and full houses. Betting risks getting check‑raised or raised by virtually all hands that now beat me and only gets value from very few worse hands. I’ll check to control the pot and get to showdown cheaply; if Claude bets a small amount I’ll call for value vs bluffs/weak pairs, but I’ll fold to large raise-shoves that are far more likely to be flushes or full houses.

On the river with Q-high (Q9 on a 7d-5d-6d-Th-Td board), I should check and go to showdown. This hand has no made value and bluffing with a river bet is poor EV—Minni's turn bet followed by river check likely indicates either a marginal made hand (that will call) or a semi-bluff (with low fold frequency). Checking preserves chips while still giving my hand a chance to win if Minni's hand is weaker than expected. The pot is 800; bluffing would require risking chips with very little fold equity and a weak hand.

9♣6♠ on the button facing only the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). This is a marginal hand but very cheap to see a flop in position. I prefer to call and use position to evaluate the flop rather than min-raising and risking a 3-bet or a shove from Flash. Plan: proceed postflop—continue with any pair, two pair, good draws or favorable board textures; fold to large, disciplined aggression if I miss.

Calling 50 more (150 total as SB) with A9s. Excellent pot odds (5:1), strong hand with multiple equity sources (top pair potential, flush draw, straight draws), and favorable position. Clear call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Four of Diamonds. The Button (Minni) limped and the Small Blind (Claude) completed. Since there is no raise and I am already in the pot, I will check to see a free flop with a suited hand that has some potential but is too weak to justify a raise against two opponents.

Betting 200 on the 7d-4h-Jd flop with A9s. This hand has excellent equity: ace-high (likely best hand currently), open-ended straight draw (K or 8), and flush draw (any heart). The bet serves multiple purposes - building the pot for value, protecting against free cards, and establishing aggression. Flash's check showed weakness, and this is standard play when holding strong hands with multiple draw possibilities on a relatively dry board.

On the flop of 7d 4h Jd, I have bottom pair (4s) and a flush draw with the Td and 4d. Claude leads for 200 into a 300 pot. I have significant equity with both the pair and the flush draw (roughly 11 outs). Calling is the most sensible play here to see a turn card, as raising might force me into a difficult spot if Claude or Minni (who is still to act) decides to shove, and calling allows me to realize my equity relatively cheaply while keeping the pot size controlled.

Board J♦ 7♦ 4♥. I hold 9♣6♠ — no pair, no diamond backdoor, no clean straight draw. Claude leads 200 and Flash calls, so this is a multiway pot. Calling 200 into ~700 gives ~22% break-even equity, but my actual equity vs Claude’s c-bet range (Jx, 7x, diamond draws, some overcards) and Flash’s calling range is well below that — likely in the low teens or worse. Raising as a bluff here is thin: two players in the pot will call with many better hands or draws, and I have no fold equity or meaningful fold-preventing blockers. My preflop plan was to continue only with pairs, two-pair, strong draws or favorable textures; this flop is a miss. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Betting 350 on the turn with Ah9h on 7d-4h-Jd-3d. I have excellent equity with multiple draws (flush draw + straight possibilities + top pair outs). Flash checked, indicating weakness or a modest holding. This bet accomplishes three goals: (1) builds value when I have favorable equity (~33%), (2) denies Flash a free card on the river, and (3) gathers information about their hand strength. The bet size (50% pot) is standard and not so large as to be over-committing, while still applying meaningful pressure. With approximately 12-15 outs and two cards to come, I have plenty of ways to improve, making this an offensive value play rather than speculative.

On the turn, the 3d completes my diamond flush. The board is now 7d-4h-Jd-3d. Claude has continued his aggression by betting 350 into a pot of 700. My hand is very strong (a flush), though it's not the nuts (the Ad, Kd, Qd, and Jd are higher, and the Jd is on the board). However, against Claude's range in a limped pot, a flush is usually the best hand. Raising here is an option to build the pot, but calling might be better to keep Claude betting his bluffs or weaker value hands like a single high diamond or two pair. Given the relatively small bet size and the fact that I want to extract value, I will call to see the river, keeping his bluffs in and potentially inducing another bet on the river.

Checking down to showdown with a pair of fours (board pair of 4h and 4s with A-J-9 kickers). Flash has been passive, checking the river after calling flop and turn bets. My made hand has reasonable equity in their likely range (missed draws, small pairs, ace-high hands). Checking allows me to reach showdown without risking additional chips to potential better holdings like J-x (two pair) or larger pocket pairs. This is a risk-minimization play with a legitimate showdown hand.

On the river, the board is 7d-4h-Jd-3d-4s. I have a Ten-high flush (Td 7d 4d 3d Jd). Claude, who led the betting on the flop and turn, has checked on the river. While the board pairing (the 4s) introduces a remote possibility of a full house and there are higher flushes possible (Ad, Kd, Qd), my hand is still very strong in a limped pot. Claude's check likely signifies a hand like a Jack or trips (a 4) that is looking for a cheap showdown, or perhaps he is wary of the flush. I will bet 800 for value, as I expect to be called by weaker hands that Claude doesn't want to bet himself.

With A♥9♥ on a final board of 7d-4h-Jd-3d-4s, I hold ace-high (A-J-9-7-4). Flash is raising to 800 after calling my bets on both flop and turn.

Pot odds analysis:
- Current pot: 2200
- Cost to call: 800
- Pot odds: 2.75:1, requiring ~27% equity to break even

Hand evaluation:
Flash's raising line after passive calls suggests either: (1) a made hand like Jx (pair of jacks), (2) a completed flush draw (has diamonds), or (3) a river bluff/marginal hand. Against this range, my ace-high has reasonable showdown value against bluffs and missed draws, but loses to J-x and flushes.

Estimated equity: ~32-35% (accounting for ~30% of range being J-x, 20-25% being flushes, 20-25% being bluffs/weak hands, 25-30% being other strong hands where I'm ahead or close).

The call is marginally close based on pure EV, but:
- My aggressive action throughout the hand suggests strength to Flash, increasing the probability of bluffs in their range
- Ace-high with J-9 kickers is a legitimate showdown hand
- The 2.75:1 odds are reasonable given hand uncertainty
- Flash's passive pre-flop and flop/turn calling before river raise is more consistent with draws or marginal hands than strong value hands

This is a reasonable call to reach showdown with a credible hand against a player whose range includes sufficient bluffs and worse holdings to justify the pot odds.

Calling from the button with 9c-Js. The pot odds (150:100) and positional advantage justify seeing a flop with this marginal hand. I can play my position effectively post-flop and fold equity makes this profitable. The small stack risk-to-reward is favorable.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5s. The Button (Claude) has limped in for 100. This is a very weak hand, but it is a small blind situation where the pot odds for calling are 4:1 (I need to put in 50 more to call a total pot that will be 250). However, 75o does not play well out of position, and with Minni in the Big Blind, there's a risk of being squeezed or playing a multi-way pot with a sub-par hand. Given my stack size and the poor quality of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than bloat the pot from out of position.

6-2 offsuit is essentially the worst starting hand. Claude just limped/called 100, and there’s no reason to invest more preflop out of position with this trash. Raising would be a pure bluff and bad use of my stack; I’ll check to see a cheap flop. Plan: fold to any significant C-bet unless I pair the 2 or 6 or pick up a strong draw — otherwise conserve chips and wait for a real hand.

I flopped two pair (6 and 2) with 6h2d on 5c–2h–6d — a very strong hand vs Claude’s limp/call preflop range. The board is rainbow and fairly dry, so there are few realistic draws; this is a clear value-bet spot to extract from worse pairs (5x, 6x, 2x, A-x) and charge the rare runner-runner combos. I’ll bet a meaningful but not pot-committing size to get value and deny free cards: 200 into a 250 pot (~80%). If he raises a normal-sized raise I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he jams huge I’ll proceed cautiously since sets are possible but not extremely likely.

Folding is the correct play here. I'm holding 9c-Js, a weak hand that completely misses this coordinated flop (5c-2h-6d). My only draw is a gutshot (8 outs), which even with two cards to come doesn't give me sufficient equity to justify calling, especially when considering the likelihood of additional aggression from Minni on future streets. Although I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (need to call 200 into a 450 pot), my equity is approximately 30-35%, which is below the threshold needed for profitability. The flop is more likely to have connected with Minni's raising range from the big blind than with my weak preflop call. Given that I'll be out of position on future streets and face potential continued aggression, folding preserves my stack for premium opportunities and higher-equity situations.

I am on the button with K5s, which is a decent hand to open in a three-handed game. My stack is currently 7850, making me the short stack, but I still have 78.5 big blinds. I'll open this hand to 300 to try and steal the blinds or play in position.

Facing a CO/Button open to 300 from the button, I have Q♥7♥ in the small blind. Pot is 450 and I must add 250 (300 total) to continue. Q7s has decent post‑flop playability (backdoor hearts, decent two‑pair/top‑pair potential) and is worth defending versus a wide button steal. 3‑betting only to 500 here would iso‑raise out of position and risk escalation from the big blind (or a 4‑bet) with deeper stacks; I prefer to call and see a flop while keeping my range balanced. Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me evaluate the board before committing more chips. Action: call 300 total.

With 2d Ks in the big blind facing Flash's raise to 300 and Minni's call, this is a straightforward fold. King-deuce offsuit is a marginal-to-weak hand with limited potential. Key factors:

1. **Poor hand quality**: 2d Ks has no flush draw potential and is at the bottom of my starting hand distribution.

2. **Position disadvantage**: I'm out of position post-flop against two opponents who have already invested chips, meaning they'll act after me.

3. **Pot odds mediocre**: While the 200 to call is only about 3:1, calling weak hands out of position is a leak. The implied odds don't justify calling with such a weak hand.

4. **Player tendencies**: Both Flash and Minni are aggressive players. Minni in particular has shown strong aggression throughout the session, and Minni's call of 250 suggests reasonable hand strength.

5. **Stack efficiency**: With 14,850 chips, I should be selective with my marginal hands and preserve my stack for premium situations.

Folding here is correct. I'll wait for stronger holdings where I have better equity and position.

I have Q♥7♥ on A♦6♠4♦ — no pair and only a very remote backdoor heart/runner-runner possibility. The flop heavily favors the button’s raising range (lots of A-x and diamond combos), and I’m out of position. Leading here would bloat the pot and play into his range; instead I want to control the pot and let him reveal himself. I’ll check and plan to float a small c-bet (call modest sizing) to realize equity and potentially take the pot on a favorable turn, but fold to significant pressure.

I opened pre-flop from the button with Kc5c and was called by the small blind. The flop comes Ad-6s-4d. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on an Ace-high board. My hand has no direct connection to the board, but a standard continuation bet here often forces folds from middle pairs or smaller pocket pairs that Minni might have called with from the small blind. I will bet 300 into the 700 pot to represent the Ace and take control of the hand in position.

Flop: A♦ 6♠ 4♦. I have Q♥7♥ — no pair but a Q blocker and backdoor hearts. Flash (button) c-bet a small 300 into the ~700 pot (pot now 1000). That sizing signals a wide continuation range vs a button open; I get ~23% pot odds to call (300/(1000+300) ≈ 23%). Versus a wide c-betting range Q7♥ has roughly break‑even to +EV equity and is worth a float to realize equity and potentially take the pot on a favorable turn. Out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with a bluff-raise or fold away good price. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, fold to significant turn/river pressure unless I pick up a heart/queen or a clear fold indicator from my opponent.

I called the flop as a float, but the turn (Ah) pairs the board and massively improves the button’s continuing range (A-x, sets, or hands that connect with the flop). I have Q♥7♥ — still no pair and no realistic draw (a flush is impossible with one card to come), only a couple of weak pair-outs on the river. With the pot at ~1,300 and me out of position, I want to control the pot and avoid bloating it when I’m almost certainly behind to Ace-containing hands. Plan: check and preserve stack; fold to sizeable turn aggression, but be prepared to call a very small bet if the sizing looks like a pure bluff (cheap odds). If Flash checks back I can re-evaluate river options (bet a Q/7 or attempt a bluff against checked ranges).

On the turn, the board pairs the Ace. While this card is generally good for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, a draw, or a weak Ace. By checking back, I control the pot size and can potentially realize my King-high showdown value against her missed draws or smaller pairs. It also allows me to induce bluffs on the river or evaluate based on the final card without over-committing with a hand that has limited equity if called.

River: 6♠ A♦ 4♦ / A♥ / 4♠. I hold Q♥7♥ — no pair, no flush, essentially zero showdown value. Flash c‑bet small on the flop and both of us checked the turn; that line is consistent with a wide range (A‑x, medium pairs, missed diamonds, some bluffs), but many hands in his continuing range beat my Q‑high (any A, any 4, most pairs). The pot is 1,300. A river bluff would need significant fold equity (e.g. a 400 bet needs ~24%+ folds) and would be unlikely to succeed on a double‑paired board where he checked the turn — he can comfortably call thin. If he raises, I'm dead. No good reason to invest chips with no equity and poor blockers. Best play is to check back and conserve stack.

On the river, the board is double-paired (Ad 6s 4d Ah 4s), giving everyone at least two pair: Aces and Fours with a Six kicker. My hand, Kc 5c, improves this to Two Pair (Aces and Fours) with a King kicker (AA44K). However, I lose to any Ace (Full House), any Four (Full House), any Six (Aces and Sixes), or any pocket pair 55 or higher (which would make a better two pair). Minni called a pre-flop raise and a flop bet, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board (a 6 or an Ace) or a pocket pair. Given her check on the river, I have some showdown value against missed draws or lower kickers, but I am unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call if I bet. Therefore, checking to go to showdown is the most prudent play.

I’m on the button with pocket nines (9c9h). This is a standard spot to take the initiative and isolate the blinds: pocket pairs perform well vs SB/BB calling ranges and I want fold equity and to build a pot with a strong but not premium hand. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total) — big enough to charge worse hands and discourage light defense while keeping the pot controllable. Postflop I’ll c-bet on dry/low boards, control size on coordinated/paired boards, and re-evaluate against 3-bets (call small 3-bets for set-mining given deep stacks; fold to large, polar 3-bets).

QJ is a premium starting hand (top 8%). Calling 250 into a 450 pot with 14,800 stack gives reasonable odds. Minni's button raise is standard; this allows me to see a flop and play positionally post-flop. The hand has good flexibility and playability in a 3-way pot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Minni opened from the button and Claude called from the small blind. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 into a 700 pot), K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially out of position. The risk of being dominated by a better King or having to fold on most flops makes this a disciplined fold.

With QJ on a paired board (8d-7c-8s), I have Queen-high with two overcards and an inside straight draw (to a 9). In a heads-up pot against Minni, who raised pre-flop from the button, I should check my marginal holding out of position. This gives me several advantages: (1) I can let Minni continue with her aggressive range and gain information about the strength of her hand, (2) I can evaluate her bet sizing to better understand her holdings, and (3) if she checks back, I keep the pot manageable and can reassess on the turn. A check here is standard for marginal hands without position, allowing me to play more accurately post-flop rather than committing chips without positional advantage.

Flop is 8♦7♣8♠ and I hold 9♣9♥ — an overpair but the board is paired and fairly coordinated (possible sets and open-ended straight combinations: 6-9, 9-10, etc.). My preflop plan was to c-bet dry/low flops and to control pot size on paired/coordinated boards. Betting here risks bloating the pot vs sets and giving free cards to straight completions; checking lets me keep the pot manageable, see how Claude reacts, and call reasonable turn bets or extract more if I improve. Plan: check to control size; call small turn bets, re-evaluate to fold to a large polarizing raise, and bet bigger if I improve to a set or Claude shows weakness.

The turn brought the Jack of Diamonds, giving me a pair of Jacks with a Queen kicker - a strong hand. Minni's flop check indicates a weaker holding (she would likely bet premium hands like sets, strong draws, or overpairs). The Jack dramatically improves my hand equity.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: Pair of Jacks with Queen kicker is strong against Minni's checked-down range
2. Minni's range: Her check on the flop suggests she doesn't have a premium holding - likely a marginal hand, weak draw, or medium pocket pair
3. Value extraction: I should bet to get value from weaker holdings (lower pairs, weak Aces, missed draws)
4. Pot control: A moderate bet avoids overcommitting while building the pot

Bet sizing: 450 chips (~65% of pot) is standard. This represents strength, gets value from Minni's checking range, and maintains good stack-to-pot ratios for the river decision. A check would be passive and miss value; a larger bet might fold out the hands I want to win from.

I opened to 300 preflop with 9♣9♥ and intentionally checked the paired, somewhat coordinated flop to control size. On the turn J♦ Claude leads for 450 into a 1150 pot — a relatively small probe bet. I still have an overpair (99) and against Claude’s checking-then-small-betting range he can have Jx, weaker pairs, some bluffs, and the small but real portion of hands that beat me (77 as a full house or an 8x/88 quads). Calling 450 to win a 1600 pot requires ~28% equity; 99 has >28% vs Claude’s likely range, so a call is +EV. Raising risks bloating the pot and getting committed against the rare full houses/quads or turning his thin value hands into bluffs; folding loses too much value vs his weaker holdings. Plan: call the 450, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river (value-bet or fold to heavy aggression depending on river texture and Claude’s action).

River value bet with pair of Jacks and Queen kicker. The 5c is a blank that doesn't improve Minni's checking range. She called my turn bet, indicating a moderate hand. A 700 chip bet extracts value from weaker pairs (Ts, 9s, Qs, etc.) and Ace-x holdings while maintaining proper pot control. This is standard value betting with a strong made hand on a safe river card.

Board: 8♦ 7♣ 8♠ J♦ 5♣. I hold 9♣9♥ — an overpair to the board but there are clear hands that beat me (any 8, a set of 77, or quads). Claude called preflop, checked the flop, led small on the turn (450) and now bets a small river sizing (700 into a 2300 pot). That sizing is consistent with thin value / blocking bets (Jx, weaker pairs) and some bluffs, not exclusively huge-value monsters. Calling 700 to win ~3000 requires ~23% equity; my 99s have well over that against Claude’s likely calling/lead range. Raising here would bloat the pot and commit me unnecessarily against the small subset of hands that crush me, while folding surrenders value to many worse hands. I’ll call 700 to reach showdown and extract value from Jx, weaker pairs, and bluffs.

4♠6♠ is a weak hand that doesn't warrant playing from the button in this spot. While position is favorable, this hand has poor equity against typical blind defense ranges and limited showdown value. The hand is dominated by most hands both opponents would play. With decent stacks and many hands remaining (67 of 150), I should be selective and wait for stronger holdings where I can generate positive expected value. Folding preserves chips for premium situations.

I am in the small blind with 4-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands. Since the button has folded, it's just me and the big blind. Given my hand strength and being out of position, it is best to fold and not invest more chips.

I am on the button with K6o. In a three-handed game, K6o is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. I will raise to 3x the big blind to try and take the pot down pre-flop or play in position against one of the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with Qc4h facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs 250 to call. Q4 offsuit is a poor hand to defend with out of position—often dominated by the button's opening range and with very limited postflop playability. Calling 250 into 450 (~36% equity needed) is not justified, and a 3-bet here would be too light without strong blockers or a much better hand. With deep stacks and better spots ahead, I preserve chips and fold.

Folding 3-8 offsuit from big blind against button raise. This is one of the worst hands in poker with no meaningful equity. Out of position and no implied odds justify playing this holding. Preserving chips for better situations.

Button with Q♦9♠ in a 3-handed pot — marginal but well within an open-raise range from the button. With deep stacks and only the blinds to beat I want the initiative, fold equity, and to avoid playing a dominated hand multiway. A standard 3x size (300) matches table norms, denies the big blind cheap flops, and keeps pressure on SB/BB. Plan postflop: c-bet when I connect or when board favors a continuation, and fold to a sizable 3-bet from opponents unless they’re overbetting.

Folding 5-4 offsuit from the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with minimal equity against Minni's likely range. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (250 into 450), I'm out of position and the big blind (Flash) hasn't acted yet, creating additional pressure. Even if the flop comes, I'll have poor equity and play in an awkward spot. Preserving chips for stronger starting hands is the mathematically correct decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4d. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude folded. 9-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Even though Minni is active, it is better to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity than to defend with such a poor holding.

I'm on the button with Td-Jc, a solid speculative hand with good positional advantage. The pot is 150 and I need to call 100. T-J offsuit is playable from the button, especially against the blinds. I'll raise to build the pot and assert positional control. A raise to 300 is standard - it's not overly aggressive but shows strength and allows me to take down the pot preflop if the blinds fold, or play the hand profitably with position postflop. This also isolates the action and avoids multiway pots where T-J is weaker.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 7s, a marginal offsuit connector. Claude has opened from the Button to 300. While 87o is a playable hand in some 3-handed scenarios, playing it out of position against a button raise and an active Big Blind is generally a losing proposition. My stack is also the shortest at the table (84 BBs), so I prefer to wait for better spots or hands that play better post-flop than mediocre offsuit connectors. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I 3-bet to 900 with A♦J♠. Facing a button open to 300, AJo is strong and has useful Ace-blockers; I don’t want to play it passively out of position for a cheap flop. A raise to 900 (~3x the open) gives value vs worse Ax/Kx and KQ-types, creates fold equity to isolate Claude heads-up, and is small enough relative to our deep stacks to keep postflop options flexible. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, consider calling a moderate 4-bet depending on sizing/player, and if called proceed to play postflop as the preflop aggressor (target c-bets on favorable textures, pot-control on dangerous boards).

Calling the 3-bet with Td-Jc from the button. The hand has decent equity, I'm getting good pot odds (2.1:1), and critically I have position post-flop which allows me to play profitably. Minni is aggressive enough that her 3-bet range includes hands I can beat. Deep stacks and positional advantage make this a sound call rather than folding a marginal hand. I can navigate post-flop decisions based on board texture and action.

Flop comes Ac 2c 5c and I hold Ad Js — top pair but with no club on my ace. This is a very wet/monotone club board: Claude called my 3-bet preflop so his calling range contains a lot of clubs and suited connectors that either have a made flush or strong club equity. As the preflop aggressor I'm out of position now; c-betting here will often get called by made flushes or decent club combos and can put me in bloated pots where my hand is frequently second-best. I want to control the pot size and gain information. Plan: check to keep worse Aces and some bluffs in, re-evaluate on the turn. If Claude leads small (~<=1/3 pot) I’ll call to realize equity; if he barrels big (>~1/2 pot) I’ll fold most of the time since I’m usually behind to made clubs.

I have Td-Jc on a 2c-Ac-5c flop - this is essentially a complete miss with no real equity. My T-high has minimal value, and the Jack is dominated by her 3-bet range which likely contains high pairs, strong holdings, or flush draws. The ace on the board is particularly bad for my hand. While Minni checked (suggesting she may have missed or is playing a draw cautiously), betting with this hand makes no sense - it has neither strong equity to bet for value nor sufficient equity to justify a semi-bluff. By checking, I keep the hand cheap, maintain some fold equity as a balanced line, and give myself a chance to improve on the turn. If a favorable card comes (3, K, or club for a flush draw), I can reassess on the turn.

Preflop I 3‑bet A♦J♠ to 900 and Claude just called. Flop A♣2♣5♣ I checked to control the pot vs a club‑heavy calling range; he checked back. The turn 9♥ is a relatively safe card — it doesn’t complete clubs and it doesn’t pair the board in a way that helps a lot of Claude’s likely floats. I still have top pair with a decent kicker and I also block some of his Ax combos (having the Ace). Claude’s flop check narrows his range toward A‑high without clubs, small pairs, or missed draws; many of the made club hands would have bet the flop. Betting here takes the initiative, extracts value from worse Aces and small pairs, and charges any remaining backdoor club equity. I want a size that gets called by worse A‑hands but leaves room to fold to big aggression, so I’ll bet roughly half the pot (~900). If he raises large I’ll treat it as a strong club/two‑pair and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (bet small for thin value if he checks a safe river, fold to large river barrels or rivers that complete clubs).

Folding T-J on a 2c-Ac-5c-9h board where I have no pair, no draw, and no equity. Minni's turn bet after checking the flop strongly suggests she has a made hand (likely AA). I have virtually no winning chances and poor pot odds (4:1 with ~5-8% equity). Minimizing losses by folding is the correct play.

I am on the button with 8h 6s. This is a weak offsuit hand that does not play well against the aggressive tendencies shown by Minni and Claude in recent hands. While it's a 3-handed game and button opening ranges are wide, 86o is generally at the bottom or outside of a standard opening range. Given the stacks and the activity of the blinds, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket eights (8c8s) in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete, but I don’t want to play this medium pair out of position by just calling. Raising gains initiative, creates fold equity, and narrows Claude’s calling range. Min-raise is 200, but I’ll make a standard, slightly larger open to 300 total — a modest size relative to our deep stacks that still builds the pot when I’m likely ahead and discourages marginal calls. Plan: c-bet on dry flops, exercise caution on coordinated/overcarded boards, and re-evaluate if Claude 3-bets (size/read dependent).

I'm holding 6h9h (weak offsuit hand) on the Big Blind. Minni raised to 300 from Small Blind, and Flash folded from Button. While the pot odds are reasonable (200 to call into 400), several factors make folding correct:

1. **Hand strength**: 6h9h is a weak hand with no high cards and poor playability.

2. **Position**: I'm out of position post-flop. Minni will act last on every street, giving her a significant information advantage.

3. **Opponent**: Minni has played aggressively throughout the session and has positional advantage. Playing marginal hands out of position against a positional opponent is unprofitable.

4. **Stack depth**: While I have a healthy stack (15,250), there's no need to play marginal spots out of position. My equity in this hand is likely 30-40% at best.

The fold is standard here. I should look for better spots where I have either better hand strength or position advantage.

3♠7♦ is one of the weakest starting hands — very poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability. Although I’m on the button and could attempt a steal, there are two defenders (SB Claude who can 3‑bet and BB Flash who calls wide) and stacks are deep enough that I’d often face tough decisions out of position. Calling 100 just to play a marginal hand multiway is negative EV, and raising to try to steal with 3‑7o is overly speculative. Preserve the stack for a better opportunity — fold.

Folding 3-9 offsuit from small blind in heads-up. This is a weak, easily dominated hand with minimal equity. Being out of position postflop against Flash makes this play unprofitable. The small 50-chip cost to fold preserves equity when playing marginal hands, which is crucial over a 150-hand session.

Raising to 300 with KJ suited from the button. This is a standard 3x BB raise that applies pressure while maintaining good position. KJ suited is a strong enough hand to play from the button, and aggressive play from late position is a key profit source in cash games. The raise is sized appropriately for stack depths and pot geometry.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o. Claude has opened from the button for 300. While a button opening range is typically wide, K2o is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, especially with an aggressive player like Minni still left to act in the Big Blind. Conserving my stack for better spots is the most prudent move here.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with K♠7♣ — a marginal, often-dominated hand with poor OOP playability. Pot is 450 and I need to put 200 more (total 300) to call, giving ~30.8% break-even equity. With Claude in position postflop and deep stacks, I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with K7o (min-raising to 500 would invest more with a hand that fares poorly vs his Kx/broadway-heavy range). I’ll flat-call to see a flop cheaply, keep his weaker hands in, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards or play for value if I pair my king or pick up meaningful draws.

I called preflop with K♠7♣ to see a cheap flop. The 5♦8♠4♥ flop is very dry and I have no pair or workable draw, and I’m out of position to Claude (button). Pot control is the priority: I want to keep his bluffs and weaker pairs in and avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check and plan to fold to heavy, value-sized aggression (roughly ≥ ~1/2 pot ≈ ≥325) but will consider calling small c-bets (≈ ≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤220) to see a turn and re-evaluate — only continuing aggressively if I pair up or pick up a meaningful draw.

Standard continuation bet on a dry flop after my pre-flop aggression. Minni checked showing weakness from the big blind. KJ has excellent equity with two overcards and the board texture (5d 8s 4h) is favorable for my hand range. Betting 425 (65% of the 650 pot) applies appropriate pressure while maintaining fold equity and extracting value from weaker holdings. This is a core profitable spot in button vs big blind dynamics.

I fold. I’m OOP with K♠7♣ on a dry 5♦-8♠-4♥ board and Claude bets 425 into a 1,075 pot (~40% pot). My hand has little showdown equity vs a realistic BTN c-bet range (many Kx/overcards and small pairs that dominate or continue), and out of position I’ll face very difficult turn decisions if I call. Calling gives ~22–23% pot odds, but the actual equity against his range is likely too low and the playability is poor. I’ll preserve my 16k+ stack and wait for a better spot rather than calling with a marginal, often-dominated hand.

I am on the button with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While a wide range can be played from the button in a 3-handed game, this hand does not meet the threshold for a standard raise or even a limp. Given the stack sizes of my opponents, there is no reason to risk chips on such a low-equity hand.

I’m in the small blind with 6♣5♣ heads-up vs the big blind. This is a playable suited connector with good multi-street potential, and in a heads-up SB vs BB spot I want to take the initiative rather than limp and play OOP for free. Claude is capable of aggression but a small standard open (2BB total) gives me fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me dictate the action post-flop. Plan: open-raise to 200; if Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal hand out of position, if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable club/connected flops and check/protect on heavy overcard boards, using position/stack depth to extract value or pick up pots.

With 5-2o (the weakest possible hand in poker), folding is the clear decision. While I only need to call 100 more chips, this hand has virtually no equity against Minni's raising range from the small blind. Even though my stack is healthy at 15,450, burning chips with the worst starting hand is unprofitable. 5-2 cannot realistically win without significant help, and I have no fold equity on the flop given the strength required to play this hand. This is a standard fold situation regardless of blind defense theory - 5-2o is simply not playable in any position.

3♣6♥ suited (3h6h) is a marginal holding, but in a 3-handed game the button should open very wide to apply maximum pressure and exploit position. Both opponents have shown aggression, so I want a standard sized open that gives me fold equity without inviting constant tiny 3-bets — 3x the BB (300) is a good, table-standard sizing here. If everyone folds I pick up the 150 pot; if called I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet favorable textures. Versus a large 3-bet from Claude (who has shown strength) I’ll fold; versus a small 3-bet I may call or mix depending on exact sizing and reads.

Folding 5-4 offsuit from small blind. This is a weak hand without sufficient strength to warrant investment, especially out of position. With solid stack depth and aggressive opponents, selective play is more profitable than marginal calls. Preserving chips for stronger holdings is the correct strategy.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening size. In a 3-handed game, A7o is a strong enough hand to defend against a button raise. I will call to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while playing out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The flop is 6d 2c 9c, giving me Ace-high with two overcards. Standard play against a button open is to check to the aggressor to see how they react. My hand has some showdown value and decent equity against a wide button range.

I raised preflop from the button and am in position with 3♥6♥. The flop (6♦ 2♣ 9♣) gives me top pair but a very weak 3-kicker and there are two clubs on board. Pot is 650 and Flash (BB) checked. Flash’s defending range can include a lot of clubs (draws), overcards, pocket pairs and some 9x; many of those I beat, but club draws and higher pairs are real threats. I want to both get value from worse pairs/floaters and deny equity to club draws, while keeping the pot controllable given my poor kicker. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot) is the right play: it folds out pure air, charges draws, and extracts value from worse pairs. I’ll bet 225.
