If I get a large check-raise I’ll strongly consider folding (likely behind vs a strong club or two-pair); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — continue cautiously on blanks, and proceed with pot control or fold to heavy aggression if a club or higher pair/runner lands.

I have Ace-high on a low-coordinated board. Minni's small continuation bet of roughly 35% pot is a standard move for a button raiser. Folding here would be too exploitable given that I likely have the best hand or at least enough equity to continue against her wide range. I will call the bet to see a turn card.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The turn card is a Ten, which doesn't improve my Ace-high but potentially connects with parts of Minni's button-opening range. Standard play is to check to the aggressor here to see if she continues her story. Leading out doesn't make much sense for my range, and checking allows me to evaluate her bet sizing or potentially see a free river if she checks back.

I have top pair (6s) with a very weak 3-kicker on 6♦ 2♣ 9♣ T♠. I opened pre, c‑bet a small 1/3 pot on the flop and was called — Flash’s flop calling range is wide: single- and double‑club holdings, some 9x/overpairs, gutshots and floats. The turn (T♠) is a relatively blank for my hand but does give better pairs (T, 9) and doesn’t change the club texture (the biggest threat). Flash checked to me, showing relative weakness. With a vulnerable top pair and poor kicker I want to control the pot size rather than bloat it and get raised off my hand or priced out by a better hand. Checking here preserves stack equity, lets me realize vs worse hands, and gives me a chance to induce bluffs or call manageable river bets. Plan: check-turn; if he leads the river I’ll fold to a large/committal bet and selectively call smaller bets for value; if he checks back I can reassess and bet a safe river size versus worse pairs/floats.

On the river, the board is 6d 2c 9c Ts 4c. I have Ace-high (Ac 7s). Pre-flop, I called a button raise. On the flop, I called a small continuation bet. On the turn, both players checked, which often indicates that the aggressor has a hand with some showdown value but not enough to bet for value, or they have given up on a bluff. The river card is the 4 of clubs, which completes a possible flush. Since I have Ace-high, I have some showdown value against missed straight draws (like 78 or 57) or other non-improving high cards. Leading out would likely only get called by better hands and fold out worse ones. Checking is the best option to try and get to showdown for free or to evaluate a bet if Minni decides to bluff.

I have top pair (6) with a very weak 3‑kicker on a three‑club board (6♦ 2♣ 9♣ T♠ 4♣). Flash (BB) called my small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and river — a fairly passive line that often contains missed overcards, single‑club floats, and small pairs rather than an aggressive made monster. The river 4♣ does complete a club flush for any player holding two clubs, and hands like trips/two‑pair or a slow‑played flush beat me — so I don’t want to bloat the pot. At the same time, checking behind surrenders thin value vs worse pairs (22, 4x) and some turn/river floats. A small value bet (~1/4–1/3 pot) is the most profitable: it extracts from worse hands and makes drawing/marginal hands pay to see a showdown while remaining small enough to fold to a decisive check‑raise. I will bet a modest 300 chips. If I face a large raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll show down.

I am holding Ace-high (Ac 7s) on a board of 6d-2c-9c-Ts-4c. After calling a button raise pre-flop and a small continuation bet on the flop, the turn went check-check. Minni's small river bet of 300 into a 1150 pot (roughly 26% of the pot) provides me with excellent pot odds of nearly 5:1. Given that she checked the turn, it is less likely she has a powerhouse like a flush or a set. Her range includes many missed broadway cards and straight draws that I currently beat, as well as thin value hands like a pair of 6s or 9s. With the price offered, Ace-high is a strong enough bluff-catcher to justify a call here.

Folding 7-10 offsuit on the button. While position is valuable in 3-handed play, this hand is too weak to justify calling 100 chips. The odds (1.5:1) don't support playing such a marginal holding. Maintaining disciplined hand selection to maximize value long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit (Q7o). The action has folded to me. While Q7o can be used as a steal hand or a completing hand in some 3-handed strategies, it is generally weak, especially out of position against Minni, who has been quite active and aggressive from the Big Blind in previous hands. Given the stack sizes and the desire to maintain a solid image, I will choose to fold this marginal hand rather than risk being outplayed out of position.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button offers positional advantage, 9-4 lacks the connectivity or high-card strength to justify a raise or a call against the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♠? (A♥9♠ offsuit). This is a strong heads-up hand — good blocker to other Aces and plenty of showdown/value. I want the initiative and fold equity rather than playing passively out of position, so I’ll raise. Making the standard min-raise to 200 (2BB total) applies pressure to the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (17k vs 15.3k), and allows flexible postflop play (c-bets, pot control, or folding to a large 3-bet). If Claude 3-bets small I can call and play postflop; if he 3-bets large I can fold and preserve stack. Raise to 200 (total).

K♥T♥ is a solid suited hand with excellent playability. Getting 3:1 odds (300 in pot, 100 to call) makes this a profitable call. In heads-up play against Minni's raise, this hand has good equity and I'll maintain positional advantage post-flop. Minni's wide raising range from SB makes this call even more attractive.

I opened preflop and have A9o on a very dry, paired flop (8♦ 8♠ 3♦). As the preflop aggressor in a heads-up spot I should keep the initiative and c-bet a wide range — many of Claude’s calling hands (Ax, Kx, weak broadways, unpaired hands) will fold and I extract value from worse. I choose a standard ~50% pot sizing (200 into 400): large enough to get folds and charge draws/weak pairs, but small enough to avoid bloating the pot out of position with just Ace-high. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue with improvement or pot-control); if he raises big he most likely has an 8 or a strong hand and I can let this one go.

Calling 200 into a 600-chip pot (3:1 odds). K♥T♥ has solid equity on this board: two overcards (K and T) over trips, plus a four-card flush draw. Against Minni's wide SB raising range, this hand has approximately 30-40% equity, which comfortably exceeds the 25% needed to break even on pot odds. The hand has excellent playability with multiple ways to improve on the turn and river. Minni's bet size of 200 on the flop after raising pre-flop suggests potential weakness or a standard c-bet, making this a good spot to continue and maintain initiative.

I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the dry flop (8♦ 8♠ 3♦); Claude called, which is a fairly wide range (medium pocket pairs, some broadways, occasional draws or 8s/3s). The turn 4♣ is a blank — it doesn’t complete obvious draws and doesn’t help my A9 except it’s another small card. As the aggressor I should continue applying pressure to fold out non-paired broadways and small-to-medium pocket pairs that called the flop, while keeping the sizing manageable given I only have Ace-high. I’ll fire a half-pot-sized barrel to get folds and extract value from worse hands that still call. If Claude check‑raises I’ll give it up; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river (likely check back without improvement). Bet half-pot (400) to maximize fold equity without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Calling 400 with K♥T♥ on 8♦8♠3♦4♣. I have roughly 6 clean outs (Kings and Tens) providing approximately 24-27% equity. At 4:1 pot odds requiring 25% equity, this is a borderline profitable call. My two big overcards combined with deep stacks and a wide opponent range from the SB justify continuing here. The hand has live potential and I'm getting the right price to draw out.

Board: 8♦ 8♠ 3♦ 4♣ K♠. I hold A♥9♠ — only Ace-high. I was the aggressor (raised pre, c-bet flop 200, barreled turn 400) and Claude called both streets, which strongly suggests at least a pair (small pocket pairs, Kx, 3x, or a slow-played 8). The river K reduces my relative equity (it gives many callers top pair) and there were no completed draws that justify a large bluff. Pot is 1,600; a river bet here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity against a player who’s shown willingness to call twice. As my objective is to maximize my stack over many hands, I won’t risk chips bluffing into a calling range — I’ll check and concede unless I face only a very small probe bet (in which case I’d fold).

River: K♠ completes my Kings (K♥T♥ on 8♦8♠3♦4♣K♠). This is a strong improvement from my two-card draw. Minni's river check indicates weakness - her betting pattern (flop 200, turn 400, river check) suggests she was either c-betting a modest hand or giving up with a bluff. A pair of Kings with a Ten kicker is very likely the best hand. I'm betting 900 (56% pot bet) for value to extract chips from weaker holdings and induce calls from hands like AQ, AJ, or medium pairs that flopped badly. This bet size is not polarizing and should build the pot on my strong hand while remaining in balanced territory.

I was the preflop aggressor and barreled the flop and turn; Claude called both streets, which strongly indicates he has a made hand (pocket pair, a set, or now Kx/top pair). The river K further improves many hands that called the turn. Pot was 2500 and his bet is 900 (pot becomes 3400) — calling 900 requires ~26.5% equity. Against a calling range weighted to pairs and Kx, my A♥9♠ has well under that break-even equity. With deep stacks and a long-term objective to preserve chips, this is not a spot to chase for a thin chance or to bluff-raise. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button with K♣? (K♥2♣), a marginal but playable Kx. With both blinds to act, I want to seize initiative and use position rather than limp into a multiway pot out of position. A standard 3x open to 300 gives good fold equity against the blinds, keeps my range credible, and avoids letting the BB see a cheap flop. If Claude or Flash 3-bet big I will fold K2o; if called I'll play postflop aggressively in position or proceed cautiously in a multiway pot.

Minni raised to 300 from the button. I'm in the small blind with As8d, a marginal hand that lacks the strength to defend this position profitably. Key considerations: (1) As8o is not a premium holding and will be out of position post-flop; (2) Minni's button raise from late position could be quite wide, but my hand lacks equity against most of her range; (3) Against a button aggressor, I should defend with premium pairs, broadway cards, and strong broadway combinations, not weak ace-x hands; (4) With 16,150 chips and 150 hands remaining, I can afford to wait for better spots. This is a clear fold that preserves chips for more favorable situations where I have better equity and position.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. The button (Minni) raised to 300. Given that we are playing 3-handed, a button raise is expected to be wide, and J9o is a reasonable hand to defend with. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call for a 450 pot), and closing the action pre-flop allows me to see a flop and realize my equity.

On this King-Queen-Six board, I have a gutshot straight draw to a Ten. As the Big Blind defender against a Button open, I am checking to the aggressor. My hand has some back-door equity but is currently quite weak. I will look to see how Minni acts before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

I have top pair (K♥) but a very weak kicker (2♣) on K♠ Q♥ 6♦. I opened preflop so my range is weighted to have strong Kings/Queens; Flash in the BB checked, which can be both weakness and a disguised strong hand. Versus one opponent in position I should continue for value and protection: a half‑pot c-bet will get called by worse Ks, Qx and small pairs while folding many A‑x and pure air. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (325) — big enough to extract value and deny free turns but small enough to keep worse hands in. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll give it up (he'll usually have a better K, two pair or a set); if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously on A/Q turns.

On this flop of K-Q-6, I have a gutshot straight draw to a Ten. Minni, who raised pre-flop from the button, has made a continuation bet of 325 into a pot of 650. The price is reasonable (325 to win 975), and I have position on later streets if Minni checks back. However, my hand is currently very weak (Jack high) and my draw is thin. In a 3-handed game, I need to defend reasonably wide, but Jack-Nine offsuit on a King-Queen-High board misses significantly. Given the presence of two overcards to my Jack and the gutshot being the only real piece of equity, I am opting to fold rather than float or bluff-raise, especially as Minni has been active and could easily have a piece of this board or a better draw.

Button position steal with J5o. Raising to 250 applies appropriate positional pressure against two opponents. The short-stacked Flash (7,175) and Minni in the big blind are likely to fold many hands, allowing me to win the blinds. Even if called, I maintain good position post-flop with a hand that can catch strong boards. This leverages my positional advantage and is standard poker strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with 4-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands. Claude has raised from the Button. Given my weak hand and the fact that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a larger stack, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. Folding is the most prudent action.

I’m in the big blind with 9♥4♥ facing a button raise to 250 (pot 400, 150 to call). 9-4 suited is a very weak holding: poor high-card, poor connectivity and limited postflop playability out of position. Calling 150 would require ~27% equity (150/(400+150)=27.3%) to break even — 9♠4♠ has well under that against a standard button opening range, and OOP play on deep stacks makes postflop decisions difficult. A min 3‑bet to 400 is also unattractive — it commits more chips OOP with little fold equity or redraw potential. With many hands left to play and better spots to invest chips, the +EV line is to fold and conserve the stack.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Q6o is a marginal hand. While the button should typically open a wide range, Q6o is generally at the bottom of that range or a fold. Given that both Minni and Claude have significant stack leads and have been active defenders/3-betters, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

3♠5♣ is a poor hand, but heads-up versus the big blind the price to see a flop is excellent. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (total commit 100) — roughly 4:1 pot odds — so a call is a cheap speculative play. Raising OOP with junk is unnecessary risk; folding surrenders too much equity for only 50 chips. I'll call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (check/fold to heavy action if I miss, or continue if I pair or pick up equity).

K9 is marginal strength - too weak to raise for value. Minni's limp suggests weakness. Best to check and see the flop, leveraging position in a heads-up scenario. This preserves chips and keeps options open postflop with a medium-strength hand.

Flop: A♠ K♦ 2♠ — my hand 3♠5♣. I have a 4‑out wheel draw (four 4s → ~16% to hit by the river) and a very small backdoor spade flush (need both turn+river spades, ~4.5%). I’m out of position vs the BB and he checked preflop; with only ~16% raw equity it’s a mistake to build the pot OOP with a pure drawing hand against a range that contains a lot of A/K and made hands. I’ll check and try to realize my equity for free. Plan: fold to meaningful aggression on the flop, call very small bets that give correct pot odds or offer implied odds, and only continue aggressively if I improve (a 4 or runner spade) or get a cheap turn/free card to realize my outs.

I have K♣9♣ which gives me a pair of Kings on a K♦ A♠ 2♠ flop. Despite the Ace on the board, my pair of Kings is likely the best hand given Minni's weak preflop limp and flop check. Minni's line indicates he either missed the flop or has a very weak holding. This is a heads-up pot and I should take down the pot with a value bet/c-bet. A bet of 125 (roughly 60% of the current 200 chip pot) is appropriate sizing - not too large to scare away all weaker hands, but substantial enough to pressure draws and weak aces. If Minni has a marginal hand or draw, this bet applies the right pressure to win the pot.

I called preflop cheaply to see the flop, but on A♠ K♦ 2♠ with 3♠5♣ I have only a 4‑out wheel draw (~16.5% to hit a 4 by the river) plus a tiny backdoor spade flush (~4% for both turn+river). Claude’s bet 125 into a 325 pot makes the pot 450 after a call, so I’d need ~27.8% equity to justify calling. My combined equity is well below that, I’m out of position, and raising OOP with pure draw equity would be a poor bluff. Fold and preserve stack for better-priced spots.

Button with KQo: a strong, positional hand worth opening. With only SB and BB to act, I want to raise to seize initiative, generate fold equity, and isolate heads-up where KQo plays well postflop. Standard sizing vs two blinds and these stack depths is ~3x BB (300) — big enough to push out weak blinds but small relative to stacks to keep implied odds. I’ll raise to 300.

Raising with T-J from the small blind against Minni's button raise. This is a solid broadway hand worth playing aggressively. Minni has shown a wide button raising range throughout the session. By raising to 800 (approximately 2.7x), I apply pressure, likely narrow the field, and take initiative from out of position. Flash's short stack makes a fold more likely. My stack is strong enough to handle potential resistance. This aggressive play is profitable against Minni's wide range.

I am holding QsJc in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button, and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. Facing a 3-bet while out of position with a hand like QJo is a difficult spot. Claude has been active and shown strength in previous hands. Calling 700 more into this pot against two opponents is likely to put me in a tough situation post-flop with a dominated hand. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Claude 3-bet to 800 after my 300 button open. I have KQo with ~160bb effective stacks — a playable hand in position that performs well multi-street. Calling the extra 500 (800 total) is the standard play: it gives me favorable pot odds (~29%) and keeps the initiative in position to realize equity and apply pressure postflop. 4-betting for value is risky (dominated by AK/AQ and often behind vs his value-heavy 3-bet range), and a small 4-bet is easily called; folding throws away a lot of profitable spots. Plan: flat, see the flop, play aggressively with top pair or strong draws and control pot / fold to heavy action when I miss.

Betting 700 (40% of pot) is a standard c-bet continuing my aggressive pre-flop line. The flop is relatively dry for most hands, and this sizing maintains pot control while representing a strong range. With my gutshot straight draw (8 outs) plus broadway showdown value, I have sufficient equity even if called. This preserves my aggressive image and applies pressure on Minni's button raising range while out of position.

I flatted Claude’s 3‑bet preflop to keep the pot manageable and play in position. The flop (8h 4h 7c) completely misses KQo — I only have K‑high with one heart (backdoor possibilities), so no real immediate equity. Claude’s 700 into a 1700 pot gives me ~29% pot‑odds to call and the bet is small relative to our deep stacks. Folding here would be too nitty since I can use position to realize equity and must keep his bluff/overcard combos in. Raising as a bluff is risky — I’ll be building a big pot with a hand that has very little fold equity vs his 3‑bet range. Plan: call the 700, see the turn, and only escalate if I pair or hit a heart (or if he shows weakness); otherwise fold to heavy turn aggression.

On the turn, the 2♥ completes a four-flush on board (8♥ 4♥ 7♣ 2♥). I hold T♠ J♦, which has modest equity here - essentially a gutshot to the 9 for a straight (only 4 outs since 7-8-9-T-J is the straight), plus some showdown value with broadway cards. However, my holdings have diminished from the flop where I had additional straight draw outs (the 6 would have given me an inside draw, now it doesn't). The extra heart particularly benefits Minni who could have flush draws or completed flushes from his range. Given that: (1) I've already shown aggression with my c-bet on the flop, (2) my hand strength is moderate at best, (3) the turn card likely helped Minni's range more than mine, and (4) I can evaluate the river action more clearly after seeing Minni's turn decision - checking is the prudent play. This allows me to control pot size with a marginal hand, gather information about Minni's strength, and make a river decision based on the final board texture. A continuation bet here risks building a pot I don't feel confident about with such limited equity.

I flatted Claude’s 3‑bet preflop and called his small flop c‑bet. The turn (2♥) completes a 3rd heart on board, and with K♥ in hand I now have a one‑card (turn) nut‑ish flush draw (4 hearts including my K). Claude checking the turn looks like pot‑control or weakness from a flop c‑bet — exactly the spot to semi‑bluff. Checking behind lets him see a free river and loses the initiative; betting gives me fold equity and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable if called. If Claude folds I take it down; if he calls I still have ~9 hearts to river (9/46 ≈ 19.6% to make the flush) and ~14 total raw outs to improve to pair/flush (~30% to improve), so calling down on a favorable river is reasonable; if he check‑raises I’ll strongly consider folding this turn semi‑bluff (I’m currently only a draw + K‑high). Bet 1,500 into 3,100 (bet ~half pot) to maximize fold equity and build for value if I complete on the river.

Folding T-J on the turn after Minni's aggressive raise. The 2♥ completes a 4-card flush that benefits Minni's range significantly more than mine. With only 4 outs to the straight (9s) and minimal showdown value, my equity is approximately 15-18%. Pot odds require 25% equity to call profitably. Minni's turn raise indicates strength - likely a completed or near-complete flush. The risk-reward is unfavorable, and folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with A2o (Ace-deuce offsuit). While this is a weak holding, several factors favor calling:

1. **Position advantage**: Button is the strongest late position, allowing me to play post-flop with maximum information about opponent actions.

2. **Pot odds**: I'm getting 2.5:1 odds (calling 100 to win 250), which justifies playing a marginal ace, especially in position.

3. **Stack health**: At 14,900 chips, I have healthy depth to play post-flop without pressure.

4. **Blind defense principle**: On the button, we should defend against blind raises with a wider range. A2o is at the threshold of playable hands from this position.

5. **Opponent tendencies**: From reviewing the recent hands, both Flash and Minni play relatively straightforward poker. I can leverage position to make profitable plays post-flop.

6. **Implied odds**: Even if I flop an ace, I can extract value from weaker holdings. If I miss completely, position allows me to control the pot and steal when appropriate.

This is a standard button call with marginal equity but excellent positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude has limped from the button. While I'm getting decent pot odds (50 to win 250), this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the standard professional play here to avoid losing more chips in a marginal situation.

I have T♦5♣ in the big blind and Claude (button) just limp-called 100. Pot is 250 and I have no further amount to call, but I can min-raise to 200. T5o is a weak, often-dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop, so I don’t want to inflate the pot or get into multi-street battles with a marginal hand. With deep stacks I can realize some equity by seeing a cheap flop, and I retain the option to raise as a semi-bluff or check-raise on a favorable flop. For pot control and to avoid unnecessary risk, I’ll check here and play the flop based on texture (raise if I catch a strong draw/top pair, fold or pot-control otherwise).

Board Q♠ T♣ 8♣ and I hold T♦5♣ — second pair with a weak kicker, out of position to a limp-caller. Pot is small and there’s no bet to face, so leading OOP would inflate the pot and commit me multi-street when I’m often behind (Qx, better Ts) or vulnerable to club runouts. I prefer pot control and information: check and evaluate Claude’s action. Plan: call reasonable bets to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn, fold to a large polarizing raise, and if he checks back consider extracting value on safe turn cards.

Checking A2o on Q-T-8. This hand is essentially ace-high with no draw potential on a coordinated board. Rather than bluff inefficiently into a checked-back flop, I'll take the free card and evaluate the turn with better information while maintaining positional advantage. Minni's BB check could represent a wide range, and betting ace-high here doesn't generate value from worse hands while losing to most better hands. Position and pot control favor checking.

Check. I have T♦5♣ — second pair (tens) with a very weak kicker on Q♠ T♣ 8♣ Q♥. Claude limped and both of us checked the flop, so his range is wide and includes Qx (now trips), medium pairs and turn/river draws. The turn pairing the queen increases the likelihood I’m behind to any Q and makes my hand marginal versus a bet. With deep stacks I want pot control and information rather than bloating the pot OOP with a hand that is often dominated. Plan: check and evaluate Claude’s action — call small, non‑polarized turn bets to realize equity and fold to large, polarized raises that represent Qx/two pair. If he checks back, I’ll look to extract thin value on safe rivers but otherwise keep the pot small and go to showdown.

Checking A2o on a paired board (QsQhTc8c) after Minni has checked both the flop and turn. This hand is ace-high with no draws or kicker strength. Betting is inefficient because hands I beat likely fold while hands that call beat me (reverse implied odds). By checking, I take a free river card and maintain positional control. If Minni checks the river, I can check back and potentially win if she has a busted draw. If she bets the river, I can fold. This is standard pot control with a weak hand against an opponent showing weakness through multiple checks.

River: Q♠ T♣ 8♣ Q♥ 5♦. My hand T♦5♣ makes two pair (Queens and Tens, Q Q T T 5). The only hands that clearly beat me are any Q (trips) or a full house — both relatively unlikely given Claude’s passive line (limp preflop, checked flop, turn and river). His range is more likely filled with Tx, small pocket pairs, missed draws and broadway hands that will call a modest value bet. The pot is small (250) so I want to extract thin value rather than check and forfeit value to those worse hands. I’ll make a small-to-medium value bet (200) to get called by worse hands while limiting my loss to a trap — if Claude check-raises large it will strongly indicate a queen and I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large sizing).

Folding A2o on the river. This is ace-high with a weak kicker on a paired board. Minni's river bet after checking flop and turn likely represents a made hand. With poor pot odds (2.25:1) and a hand that beats very few holdings, calling is a losing proposition. Preserve chips for better opportunities.

I have King-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to take control of the pot early or narrow the field. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that has been used frequently throughout the session.

T9o (Tc2d) is a very weak, often dominated hand to face a btn open. Calling 250 (to 300 total) OOP against the opener — with the BB still to act who can 3‑bet — puts me in a poor postflop spot with a bad kicker and little playability. Pot odds (250 into 450) look OK, but my expected equity versus a button raising range (and possible BB action) is below break‑even and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP early in a deep‑stacked session. No reason to three‑bet with this rag. Fold and wait for a better spot/position.

K-5o is too weak to defend from the big blind against a standard button raise. Out-of-position play with a marginal hand is a losing proposition long-term. With a healthy stack and 67 hands remaining, I should be selective and wait for stronger spots. Flash's line doesn't suggest a bluff - folding here is disciplined poker.

9♥8♦ on the button is a marginal but playable hand because of position and straight-making potential. The pot is only 150 and a call is cheap, but I want to seize the initiative and use my button to put pressure on the blinds (Claude in SB is active; Flash in BB is a willing caller). I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) — big enough to fold out a portion of SB/BB and avoid limp multiway pots, but not so large that I overcommit with a mediocre hand. Post-flop plan: if everyone folds I take the blinds; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously on dry high-card boards, and fold to large, committed 3-bets or heavy post-flop aggression unless I pick up real equity. If I face a large squeeze 3-bet, I’ll fold 9-8o.

Raising with QK from the small blind against a button raise. This is a premium hand that benefits from aggression. A raise to 800 (250 to call + 550 additional) maintains good pot geometry while building value with a strong hand. This applies pressure on both Minni and Flash while keeping reasonable stack proportions for potential post-flop play.

I am in the big blind with 5d 3s, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. The action is now on me. Given my poor starting hand and the strength shown by both players, especially Claude's re-raise, there is no reason to continue in this pot. I will fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 9♥8♦ and Claude squeezed to 800. 9-8o is marginal, but heads-up in position vs a single 3-bettor and with deep stacks it’s worth seeing a flop. Calling 500 more (800 total) gives ~22.7% pot odds and preserves postflop maneuverability — I can use position to c-bet favorable boards, realise equity when I hit, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. I will not 4-bet here (too thin and often dominated) and will fold to a large 4-bet. Plan: call 500 and play the flop in position; continue aggressively only on favorable textures or real equity, otherwise control the pot and fold to serious pressure.

Betting 900 on the flop with top pair (Kings with Queen kicker). This continuation bet serves multiple purposes: (1) gets value from weaker pairs and hands like AQ or draws, (2) protects against flush draws given the two hearts on board, (3) builds the pot while I'm likely ahead, and (4) maintains the aggressive momentum from pre-flop. Being out of position, taking the initiative is strategically sound rather than allowing Minni to control the action with their positional advantage. Bet sizing is proportional to pot (roughly 50% of 1700) and maintains good stack management for potential future streets.

Preflop I defended with 9♥8♦ because of position and implied odds, but the flop geometry and Claude’s line make this a clear fold. The board K♥ J♥ 6♠ heavily favors a 3-bettor’s value range (AK, Kx, Qx, pocket pairs, some suited broadways) and Claude’s 900 c-bet into the ~1700 pot is consistent with those holdings. I have no pair and only weak backdoor outs (runner-runner hearts or pairing a 9/8), so my realistic equity vs a standard 3-bet/c-bet range is well below the ~25.7% needed to break even on a call (900 to win ~3500). A raise would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and likely to get shoved or called by better hands. Fold and preserve stack for better, more constructive spots — the preflop investment is sunk and shouldn’t dictate calling a marginal flop.

T7o (Ten-Seven offsuit) is a weak holding from the button. While position is valuable, this specific hand lacks the strength to justify entering the pot. The hand is dominated by most reasonable big blind ranges and will be difficult to play profitably post-flop. At 15,600 chips with 85 hands completed and 65 remaining, preservation of my stack for stronger spots is the correct strategy. Folding weak hands from late position is a fundamental principle of solid poker. The risk/reward simply doesn't justify a call here.

J5o is a very weak hand, even for a blind versus blind situation. Minni has a large stack and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. While it only costs 50 chips to call and see a flop, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with a hand that is likely dominated. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

On the button with 8c 3c, I have a very weak hand. While it is suited, it does not play well in a three-handed game where both blinds (Minni and Claude) have been fairly active and aggressive. Given that I am on the button, I could consider a raise as a steal, but with a stack of roughly 70BBs, it is better to wait for a more premium hand or at least a better semi-bluff candidate. I will fold.

Heads-up after Flash folded, J♠J♦9♦ (J♦9♠) is a decent hand in the small blind — playable and with reasonable showdown equity and straight/backdoor potential. Folding surrenders my posted blind and is too passive; calling 50 leaves the big blind the positional advantage and lets Claude see flops cheaply. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure, so I open-raise to the standard 2xBB (200 total). That gives me fold equity, defines my range, and keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks. If Claude calls, I’ll c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control out of position; if he 3-bets, I can fold, call for position, or play deeper depending on sizing and reads.

54o (54 offsuit) is the worst starting hand in poker with minimal equity potential. While I'm getting favorable pot odds (3:1 on a 100 call into 300), several factors argue for folding: (1) This is a genuinely unplayable hand - it lacks both value and blocker potential; (2) I'd be playing out of position post-flop against an aggressive opponent; (3) With a healthy stack of 15,500, there's no urgency to play marginal spots; (4) The min-raise from the small blind is unusual, and I have no strong read to justify calling with trash. Even with decent odds, consistently playing weak hands from the blinds is a chip-bleed strategy. Better to fold and preserve chips for premium situations where I can leverage position and hand strength.

On the button with K♥3♥ — a weak but suited, positionally playable hand. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call (min-raise to 200 available). Claude (SB) has shown frequent preflop aggression and Flash (BB) defends widely, so a small open will likely get called or 3-bet and leave me in a multiway/ugly dominated spot with Kx. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot small and use position. I’ll call 100 to see a flop cheaply; if I hit a king or heart I can c-bet/raise for value or bluff, otherwise I’ll often check-fold to significant pressure or float small bets and try to take it away on later streets.

7-3 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker with virtually no playability. Despite only needing to call 50 chips, being out of position in the small blind against two opponents with such a weak hand makes this a straightforward fold. The 50 chips already posted are a sunk cost. Maintaining discipline with hand selection is crucial for long-term profitability.

I am in the big blind with 8h 6h. Minni, on the button, has completed the blind (limped), and Claude in the small blind has folded. I have the option to check and see a free flop with a suited connector, which fits my strategy of playing speculative hands cheaply in position (though I'm out of position, the pot is small). There is no need to raise and bloat the pot with a marginal hand against a large stack.

I am in the big blind with 8h 6h on a flop of 4d Td 3c. I have no direct pair or draw on this board. Minni, the button player, has checked or will act after me if I check. Since the pot is small (250) and I have no hand equity currently, the most prudent play is to check and see if I can improve on the turn for free or fold if there is significant action.

I flopped a weak made hand: K♥3♥ on 4♦ 10♦ 3♣ gives me bottom pair (trip? No—pair of treys) with a king kicker on a two-diamond board. Flash in the BB checked, and he defends very widely preflop, so his range is full of overcards, backdoor stuff and some pairs/draws. I want to keep the pot small with this vulnerable pair — I’m behind to any 10x/4x and vulnerable to diamond combos; betting invites a check-raise or a big shove from a shorter-stacked opponent and forces tough decisions. Plan is to control the pot: check and see a turn, call small turn bets to realize equity, and fold to strong aggression. If Flash checks through I’ll take a cheap showdown; if he bets small I’ll call and re-evaluate; if he raises big I’ll fold.

I have turned a pair of sixes, which is a decent improvement over my previous air. However, the 6d also completes potential flushes. Given that the flop was checked around, it's unlikely Minni has a monster, but I'll check to control the pot size and see how she responds now that I have some showdown value.

Check. I have K♥3♥ — bottom pair with a decent kicker but it’s a very vulnerable holding on a diamond-heavy board (4♦ T♦ 3♣ 6♦). Flash checked flop and turn, which looks like weakness or a slowplay, but there’s also the risk someone holds two diamonds and already has a flush. Pot control is priority: I don’t want to build a big pot with a one-pair hand that’s beaten by any 10x/4x and crushed by a diamond flush. Plan: check and see how Flash responds. If he checks through I take a cheap showdown. If he bets small (a probing bet) I’ll typically call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the river; if he raises large or shoves I’ll fold — this hand isn’t strong enough to commit. If I improve on the river (trip 3s or a very safe card) I’ll consider a value bet then.

On the river, the board is 4d-Td-3c-6d-2h. I have a pair of sixes. The action has been checked all the way through, suggesting both players have relatively weak holdings. A pair of sixes is likely the best hand here, but betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and might get raised by a slow-played better hand or a random flush that decided to check. I will check and take my showdown value.

Board: 4♦ 10♦ 3♣ 6♦ 2♥. I hold K♥3♥ — bottom pair with a decent kicker but very vulnerable: any 10x, 4x, pocket pair >3 or any two diamonds (flush) beat me. Flash has taken a completely passive line (check-check-check), which can be either missed diamond/overcard draws or a slowplayed strong hand, so a bet here risks being called or raised by many hands that currently beat me. The pot is small (250) relative to our stacks, so the EV of a bluff is low while the downside if called by a better hand is unnecessary. I’ll check and take the cheap showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I have Th-Ac on the button, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. This is a strong raising hand from my position. With only Flash (SB) and Minni (BB) in play, AT-offsuit is well above the average hand strength required to play on the button. A standard 3x raise to 300 accomplishes several goals: (1) gets value from weaker holdings, (2) takes down dead money immediately if both opponents fold, (3) allows me to play the hand in position post-flop with good equity, and (4) is consistent with my aggressive strategy that has been working well in previous hands. My stack is healthy at 15,450, so I can afford to build pots with strong hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 5s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 3 bets from the button. Calling out of position with this hand is unprofitable, and 3-betting as a bluff against a button raise is risky given the stacks. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Facing Claude's button raise to 300 from the big blind with 6♥3♠ — a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand. To call I must put in 200 into a pot that will be 650, so I need roughly 30.8% equity. Versus a standard BTN open this hand's equity is much lower and it's frequently dominated; it has terrible postflop playability out of position and offers no good blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Claude has been an active raiser, so I should avoid marginal multi-street situations. Fold and preserve a healthy stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c 5h). This is a solid opening hand in a three-handed game. I have 7,125 chips and the action is currently on me pre-flop. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately or thin the field. I'll raise to 300.

I’m in the small blind with Td8h facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I must put in 250 more to call (300 total commitment). In 3-handed play the button’s opening range is wide and Td8h has reasonable playability heads-up, but it’s too weak to 3-bet profitably — a reraise here would leave me out of position and often dominated or forced into tough decisions. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity against the big blind if they fold, and gives me a chance to see a flop with a hand that can hit two-way. Pot odds: I need roughly 35.7% equity to call (250 to win 700), which is defendable against a wide opener. Plan: call and play the flop cautiously — c-bet or continue when I connect or pick up strong backdoor outs; fold to significant aggression if I miss or if the big blind applies pressure. I will fold to a sizable 3-bet preflop.

Folding Q3o out of position in a 3-way pot. Despite having pot odds, the hand strength is too weak to justify continuing. Out of position against two opponents with a marginal holding is not a profitable spot. My stack is healthy enough to wait for better opportunities.

Check. I have T♦8♥ on A♦ 8♣ 3♠ — a vulnerable second pair out of position versus the button’s wide opening range. Betting OOP here often bloats the pot while I’m frequently behind to A‑x and sometimes crushed by sets/trips. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity, and induces bluffs I can call or extract from on later streets. Plan: call small-to-medium c-bets (≈≤½ pot) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to large overbets or a polarizing check‑raise; if he checks back I can get to a cheap showdown or look for value on safe turn cards (T/8).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with a pocket pair of fives. The flop comes Ace-high and relatively dry (3s 8c Ad). While the Ace is a scare card for my fives, it is also a card that favors the range of the pre-flop raiser. Minni checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet to maintain the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if Minni has missed. A bet of roughly 40% of the pot is sufficient for this purpose.

I have T♦8♥ on 3♠-8♣-A♦ — second pair with a reasonable kicker, out of position vs the button. Flash’s c-bet (300 into a ~700 pot pre-bet → a small/medium sizing) is exactly the type of spot I planned to call: it’s large enough to charge pure draws but small enough that my second pair has decent showdown value against a wide button opening range. Raising OOP here would bloat the pot and force difficult decisions when I’m often behind to A‑x or sets, so I’ll just call to control the pot and realize equity (calling 300 is cheap relative to the pot and preserves stack depth). Plan after the call: fold to a large, polarized turn shove/raise; call or check‑call modest turn bets; if the turn is safe (blank or a T) I can extract value or re-evaluate vs aggression.

I’m OOP with second pair (8s) + T kicker on 3s‑8c‑Ad‑6h and the pot is 1,300. Flash opened wide from the button and made a relatively small flop c‑bet (300 into ~700), so his range contains A‑x, some medium pairs, draws and bluffs. Betting out here would bloat the pot when I’m often behind to A‑x or a set and would put me in tough spots vs raises. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable turn bets to realize equity and induces bluffs; I’ll call modest bets (roughly ≤1/2 pot ≈650) and fold to large, polarized raises. If Flash checks back I get to a cheap river and can re‑evaluate (look to extract vs worse on a T/8 or check down on scary cards).

On the turn, the board is 3s 8c Ad 6h. I have pocket fives. Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board, perhaps an Ace with a weak kicker, an 8, or a middle pocket pair. The 6h doesn't change much. Since my fives have limited equity against her calling range and checking allows me to realize what showdown value I have without bloating the pot, I will check back. This also keeps the pot manageable in case she is trapping with an Ace.

River decision: pot 1,300. I hold T♦8♥ — second pair (eights) with a ten kicker, out of position vs Flash, who opened wide on the button, c‑bet the flop 300 and then checked the turn. The turn (6♥) and river (J♥) are mostly blanks for my hand; they improved some of his combos (6x and Jx) but also left many of his weak c‑bet/bluff combos unchanged.

Range and line reading: Flash’s line looks like a lot of A‑x (c‑bet on flop, check‑turn to control), some medium pairs, and occasional bluffs. On the river a bet from me will be called by most hands that beat me (A‑x, any J, T) and likely will fold many of the hands I actually beat (Kx, Qx, air) — so a value bet extracts little while getting value from stronger hands a lot.

Strategy: Betting here has poor risk/reward — it bloats the pot and is most often called by better hands and folds worse ones. There were very few missed draws that will now bluff the river, so fold equity is low. Raising is even worse. Best play is to check to showdown and realize my equity. I’ll check and show my second pair instead of creating marginal bets that are negative EV.

Action: check to get a cheap showdown with second pair.

On the river, the board is 3s-8c-Ad-6h-Jh. I have pocket fives. Minni called my flop continuation bet but checked the turn and river. This pattern often suggests a marginal hand like a weak Ace, an 8, or perhaps a missed draw that has now picked up some value or is just hoping to get to showdown cheaply. Since my fives have some showdown value but are unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, and unlikely to force a fold from better hands like an 8 or a weak Ace at this point, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

I'm on the button with 7♥9♣ in an unopened pot (blinds only). This is a marginal but playable hand in position — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and avoid a limp-induced multiway pot. Claude (SB) has shown frequent preflop aggression, while Flash (BB) defends a lot but is a shorter stack. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to generate fold equity, isolate or play postflop in position, and discourage cheap multiway calls. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (7-9 offsuit plays poorly vs strong 3-betting ranges); if called, c-bet on favourable flops and control pot size on dry or adverse boards.

6-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even from the Small Blind against a Button raise, this hand has virtually no value and poor equity against any reasonable range from Minni. The hand lacks connectivity, high-card strength, and drawing potential. While Minni has been aggressive in the session, there's no justification for playing this hand. I should preserve chips and wait for a better opportunity. This is a straightforward fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs7s, a reasonable suited hand that plays okay in heads-up pots. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2.25:1 odds. Given the suitedness and the Queen high, this is a standard call to see a flop rather than folding or 3-betting against what appears to be a frequent raiser in Minni.

I have flopped a flush draw with Qs7s on a board of 8s Ah Js. While the Ace is a scary card in the opponent's range (Minni raised from the button), I have significant equity with my draw. I will check to see how Minni reacts. If she bets a standard amount, I have the odds to call and see if I can complete my flush on the turn.

I raised preflop to 300 and isolated Flash (BB) who just checked the flop. Board is 8♠ A♥ J♠; my hand 9♣7♥ has no pair and only a gutshot to a 10 (4 outs). That’s ~4/47 ≈ 8.5% to hit on the turn and ≈16.5% to hit by the river. Pot is 650. This flop is adverse to my preflop raising range (lots of Ax/Jx in caller’s range), so I want to control pot size and realize equity cheaply rather than barreling with a very marginal draw. If I bet and get a half‑pot or bigger response, calling would be -EV (example: a ~325 bet requires ~25% equity to call; I have ~16.5% to river). I’ll check to keep the pot small and see a free turn. Contingency: if Flash leads a very small bet (≈≤160) I can call to chase the 10; if he puts in a sizable C‑bet I’ll fold unless the price is very attractive. If I see a 10 on the turn I’ll convert to value/raise depending on action.

I have completed a flush with the Queen of Spades on a board that now contains two Aces. While the paired board introduces the risk of a full house, Minni's check on the flop significantly reduces the likelihood that she holds a monster like AJ, A8, JJ, or 88, which would typically bet for value or protection on such a coordinated flop. I will lead out with a bet of 400 into the 650 pot to extract value from weaker hands like a Jack, an Eight, or a mid-pocket pair that might call thinking I am bluffing on the scary board.

I opened from the button and checked the flop on a very unhelpful 8♠ A♥ J♠. Turn paired the ace (A♠) and Flash leads out for 400. I hold 9♣7♥ with no pair and only a 10 to complete a straight (4 outs). Turn→river equity is about 4/46 ≈ 8.7%. Calling 400 into the current pot (1050) requires roughly 400/1450 ≈ 27.6% equity — I’m far from getting the correct odds. Flash’s bet is consistent with Ax, Jx or a protection/stand‑pat range; raising as a pure bluff here is high variance and likely to be called or 3‑bet by stronger hands. With clean fold equity absent and my hand having negligible equity, the +EV play is to fold and conserve a large stack for better spots.

Folding 9Jo from the button preflop. While I'm getting 1.5:1 pot odds on a 100 chip call, my hand equity (~22-25% in a 3-way pot) doesn't justify playing. This hand plays poorly against two opponents and my stack is healthy enough to wait for better opportunities. Standard fold with a weak hand facing two opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2s. Claude (Button) has already folded. It is just me and Minni (Big Blind). Q2s is a marginal hand, but since it's just the two of us and I am in the SB, I have the option to complete the blind or raise. However, Minni has been quite active and has a large stack. Completing the blind for 50 more chips is a cheap way to see a flop with a hand that has some post-flop potential (suited, one high card). I'll call the extra 50.

Flash (SB) just limp-called — very wide, often speculative range. I have A♠5♦ (Ah5d) blocking the Ace and decent heads-up equity. I want to isolate the SB and take the initiative while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll make a standard isolation raise rather than check and allow cheap multi-street realization. Raising to 300 total (I’ve already posted 100, so +200) is a crisp size: enough fold equity to deny many small pairs/suited connectors and lets me c-bet favorable flops, but it’s not so large that I overcommit with a marginal ace if I face strong resistance. Plan: if called, continuation-bet on dry boards and proceed cautiously vs big resistance or 3-bets (fold to large 3-bets with this hand).

I have Q2c in the Small Blind. After I completed the blind, Minni (Big Blind) raised to 300. This is a 3x raise over the big blind. While my hand is suited, Q2 is structurally very weak and often dominated or crushed by a standard raising range from the Big Blind, especially given Minni's active history. Calling another 200 chips out of position with a marginal hand like this is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. I have been consistently opening for 300 chips from the button throughout this session to apply pressure to the blinds. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play in position post-flop against one of the blinds.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB with J♦2♦. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to continue. J2o is a very weak, easily-dominated hand and plays terribly out of position, especially with a deep-stacked big blind (Claude) yet to act who will frequently call or 3-bet and put me in difficult multiway spots. A 3-bet bluff is too risky here and calling 250 to see a flop OOP is +EV negative with this holding. Fold and preserve chips for a better spot in position.

3betting Flash's likely button steal with A6o. His short stack (6475) and button position suggest a wide raising range. A 700 raise generates sufficient pressure to fold out marginal hands while maintaining reasonable risk management. If called, I still have showdown value and playability postflop.

I am on the button and originally raised to 300 with K8o. Minni folded, but Claude (Big Blind) has 3-bet to 700. Claude has been playing relatively aggressively and has a healthy stack. While K8o is a decent hand in a 3-handed game, facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind significantly weakens its value. Calling 400 more into a pot of 1050 is tempting due to the odds, but playing a mediocre hand out of position (or even in position) against a 3-bet range often leads to difficult post-flop decisions. I have a comfortable stack and do not need to take a high-variance spot here. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Button in a 3‑way pot — position is a big advantage so I can open a fairly wide range. K6o is marginal but has blocker value to other Kx hands and I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds. Table standard open size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure and play post‑flop in position. Plan: fold to a significant 3‑bet (hand is easily dominated), call single raises and C‑bet on favorable high/face-card flops, check/control on low/dry boards and avoid bloating the pot with a weak kicker.

Q5o is a weak hand with limited postflop potential, and I'm out of position in the small blind against Minni's button raise. While I need to defend the blind with some hands, Q5 doesn't meet the threshold—it's dominated by most of Minni's raising range and will play poorly out of position. My stack is healthy at 15,750 (150BB effective), so preserving chips for premium situations is the correct approach. Minni has been active throughout this session, but that doesn't mean I should defend light with marginal holdings. Folding maintains my disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 2s, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have to put in 200 more to call a 450 pot, the hand has very poor playability and I am out of position. It's an easy fold to conserve my stack.

3-7 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in poker. Even on the button, this hand lacks the equity needed to justify a call. I'm getting 1.5:1 pot odds which is insufficient. With solid stack depth (15,750), I should fold and preserve chips for premium opportunities. Playing weak hands leads to marginal decisions postflop where stronger opponents can exploit me.

In the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Deuce of Spades, I am facing a heads-up situation against Minni in the Big Blind after the Button folded. While the pot odds to call the remaining 50 are 3:1, Ten-Deuce suited is a very weak hand to play out of position. Previous hands show that Minni is an active and aggressive player who often raises over limps (as seen in Hand #91). I prefer to fold this marginal hand to avoid being put in a difficult spot post-flop or facing a raise from an aggressive big stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position. Given the stack sizes, I need to play carefully, but folding here would be too tight.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with 7♣2♦ — the worst non-implicated hand. Pot is 450, to call 250, min-raise to 500. This hand has virtually no equity vs a standard button opening range and plays terribly out of position against the button (and likely the big blind). Calling OOP or making a thin squeeze/bluff into two opponents is negative EV. With a deep but non-desperate stack, preserving chips for better spots is correct. Fold.

In a heads-up situation with 7h-9s in the Big Blind. Flash raised to 300 from the button (wide positional range expected), and Minni folded. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450:200 to call). While 7-9 offsuit is a marginal hand, it has playability with straight possibilities and in heads-up dynamics, this is a reasonable call given the favorable immediate odds and my ability to make postflop decisions. The pot is already 450, and I'm committing 200 more to enter a heads-up situation where I can exploit my position after the flop.

On the flop Qc-Qh-4d with 7h-9s, I have no made hand currently. While 7-9 has straight potential (any 8 or T completes a straight), the paired queen board is not favorable for my hand. Flash was the button aggressor with a likely wide range. By checking here, I accomplish several things: (1) I control the pot size with a marginal hand, (2) I gather information about Flash's hand strength - if they check back, I maintain my hand and can evaluate the turn; if they bet, I can make a more informed decision about folding, calling, or raising, (3) checking also prevents me from investing more chips into the pot when I'm likely behind or drawing thin. This is a sound defensive play that preserves my chips while maintaining flexibility for future streets.

On a paired board like Qc-Qh-4d, the pre-flop aggressor generally has the range advantage. My Ace-high currently has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards like Kings or Jacks. By placing a small continuation bet of about one-third of the pot, I can put pressure on the Big Blind's folding range (like suited connectors or weak hands that missed) while keeping the pot manageable. If Claude calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, as his calling range would likely consist of pocket pairs, a Four, or a Queen.

With 7h-9s on a Qc-Qh-4d board in a heads-up pot against Flash's flop bet, I'm evaluating my options carefully. While my hand is currently weak (no made hand), I have reasonable equity considerations: (1) Pot odds are favorable at 3.89:1 (875 to call 225), and I need roughly 20% equity to justify a call - my straight draw potential combined with any implied hand equity likely exceeds this threshold. (2) Flash's bet of 225 into a 675 pot suggests a medium-strength continuation bet rather than a massive value bet, consistent with a wide button-raiser's range. (3) I have 8-outs to the straight (any 8 or T), giving me meaningful draws plus equity against weaker holdings. (4) Stack sizes are healthy, allowing me to fold the turn if the situation deteriorates. (5) Position disadvantage postflop argues for controlled pot growth, which calling achieves. The math supports this call given the odds and my draw potential. I'll proceed to the turn where I can reassess based on new information.

With 7h-9s on Qc-Qh-4d-8h, I have essentially no made hand (roughly 13% equity with 6 pair outs). Flash checked the turn after betting the flop, indicating uncertainty. Betting here risks a raise that would force me to fold, or being called by a better hand. Checking allows me to take a free river card and see if I can hit one of my 6 outs (any 7 or 9) while preserving chips in a weak position. This is a disciplined defensive play appropriate to the weak equity and out-of-position situation.

On this paired board, my Ace-high has some showdown value. Claude called a small continuation bet on the flop, which suggests he might have a small pair, a four, or a float. The eight on the turn is largely a blank. By checking back, I control the pot size and can potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff on the river. Betting again is unlikely to fold out hands that are beating me (like small pocket pairs) and won't get called by many worse hands.

With 7-9 offsuit on a paired board (Qc-Qh-4d-8h-3h), I have zero showdown value. My only path to win is to bet and force Flash to fold. Flash's check-check pattern on turn and river suggests weakness or a missed draw. A bet of 350 (roughly 32% of the current 1100 pot) represents reasonable strength and puts pressure on Flash to make a decision. If I check, I lose at showdown 100%. If I bet, I win if Flash's hand is weaker than what they're trying to represent. Given the stack sizes (I have 15,225, Flash has 5,800) and pot size, this is a disciplined bluff bet that exploits Flash's apparent weakness.

On the river, Claude leads for a small bet of 350 into a pot of 1100 after the turn went check-check. My Ace-high (As 5c) had decent showdown value until this lead. Claude's line of check-calling the flop and then leading the river suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like a pair of 4s, 8s, or a small pocket pair) that he's betting for thin value, or he's bluffing. However, even his thin value range (any pair) beats my Ace-high. While the price to call is attractive, I am likely behind many of his calling hands from the flop and his lead often indicates he has at least a small piece of the board. I will fold and look for more favorable spots.

2♥9♥ is a very weak hand. The pot is 150 and the cost to see a flop is 100, so a call requires ~40% equity (100 / (150+100)) — far more than 92s can reasonably expect vs two opponents. Raising as a steal is possible from the button in some spots, but Claude (SB) has shown a tendency to fight for pots and Flash (BB) is short-stacked and likely to either shove or reraise — I don’t want to inflate the pot or face a 3-bet with junk. Fold and wait for a better, playable spot on the button.

AJ is a premium hand in heads-up play. After Minni folds, I'm in a favorable position against Flash who has a short stack of only 5,700 chips. I have a significant chip advantage at 16,275. Raising to 250 (a 2.5x raise) applies appropriate pressure and extracts value from my strong hand while not committing too heavily pre-flop. This aggressive approach fits my successful pattern of winning with strong holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens (7d 7h). The Small Blind (Claude) has raised to 250, making it 150 more for me to call. Pocket sevens are a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the Small Blind. I have 5700 chips, and Claude has 16075. While I could 3-bet here, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop potential (set mining and overpair possibilities) without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a larger stack. Given the position and the hand strength, calling is a solid, standard play.

I'm in strong position on this flop. Pre-flop I raised to 250 with AJ (premium hand), and Flash called from the big blind with a very short stack of 5,550 chips. The flop of 5s-3d-6d is relatively dry and unlikely to have improved Flash's hand significantly. I currently have ace-high, which is likely the best hand here. Flash is desperate and short-stacked, so I need to maintain aggression and extract value. A bet of 350 chips (70% pot bet) applies strong pressure - it's a significant bet relative to their remaining stack that forces Flash into a tough decision. This continuation bet either takes down the pot immediately or gets Flash to commit chips with a weaker hand. Given my 16,075 chip advantage and Flash's 5,550 remaining, this aggressive bet fits my successful pattern of maximizing value against short-stacked opponents.

I have an overpair with pocket Sevens on a low, connected board (5-3-6). Claude's continuation bet of 350 into 500 is standard. While my hand is vulnerable to many turn cards (any 4, 7, 8, overcards, or diamonds), calling is preferred over raising here. Calling keeps Claude's range wide, allowing him to continue bluffing with air or overcards, and prevents me from over-inflating the pot against potential sets or straights. I am in position and can evaluate the turn based on the card and his subsequent action.

Continuing aggression on the turn with AJ (ace-high) against critically short-stacked Flash. The flop bet showed strength, Flash called, and now another diamond appeared. I maintain a likely best hand and need to extract remaining value while Flash has chips to pay. A 550 bet applies strong pressure - it's roughly half-pot and forces Flash into a difficult decision with his 5,200 remaining. This maintains my successful aggressive approach against short-stacked opponents while protecting my hand against potential draw outs on the river.

The turn 2d is a complex card. It completes potential straights (A4, 47) and flushes. I have an overpair with my pocket Sevens and, importantly, I hold the 7d, giving me a flush draw. Claude's bet of 550 into 1200 (less than half pot) is a sizing that could represent a wide range, including semi-bluffs or marginal value. Given I have both showdown value and a draw to a flush, calling is the most appropriate action. It allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the river in position without over-committing to a pot where I might already be behind a completed straight or a higher flush.

River value bet with AJ (Ace-high) against short-stacked Flash. The dry board (5-3-6-2-10) is unlikely to have improved Flash's hand significantly given the calling pattern through flop and turn. With Flash down to only 4,650 chips and having called aggressive bets throughout, a 900-chip bet represents solid value extraction. This bet is sized to extract chips from weaker hands while remaining small enough that Flash will call with marginal holdings, which is my goal as the likely best hand at showdown.

On a board of 5s-3d-6d-2d-Ts, the action has been consistent with Claude (SB) betting all three streets. My hand, pocket Sevens (7d 7h), is an overpair to most of the board, though the turn 2d completed potential flushes and straights (any 4). Holding the 7d is significant as it blocks some straight combinations (4-7) and flush combinations. Claude's river bet of 900 into 2300 is roughly 39% of the pot, giving me favorable pot odds (needing to be right about 22% of the time). Given Claude's demonstrated aggression in previous hands and the relatively small sizing on the river, my hand serves as a viable bluff-catcher. I will call to see the showdown.

Jd 2h is a very weak hand with minimal potential. While position on the button is favorable for hand range expansion, J2o is below the threshold of playable hands here. Key factors: (1) This hand is poorly connected, unsuited, and dominated by most of Minni's BB defending range; (2) If called, I'd be out of position post-flop against an active opponent with a substantial stack; (3) My stack of 14,275 is healthy - no need to take marginal spots; (4) Minni has shown strong aggression throughout the session and will likely defend the BB; (5) Even on the button, not all hands should be played. This is a clear fold situation - preserving chips for stronger hands in better spots is the correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with J2o. Claude has folded on the button. It's just me and Minni in the Big Blind. The pot is 150 and it costs 50 more to call. Given J2o is a very weak hand and Minni has been quite active and aggressive throughout the session (e.g., Hands #91, #93, #86), I don't see much value in completing the blind or raising here. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While this is a 3-handed game, J6o is generally too weak to open-raise against aggressive opponents like Minni and Claude, who have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and 3-bet. Given my stack size and the strength of the hand, folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

I have A♣9♣ in the small blind — a suited ace with good playability and blocker value. Facing only the big blind, I want to take the initiative while keeping the size sensible out of position. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) applies pressure, extracts fold equity versus his defending range, and builds a pot when I’m likely ahead. I avoid the bare min-raise (200) because it’s too weak and I avoid just calling 50 since I prefer to seize initiative with this hand. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate given stack depth; if he calls I’ll proceed with a standard c-bet plan on favorable flops.

Folding 3♠6♠ in the big blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with minimal equity. Although I'm getting 2:1 pot odds to call, this hand plays terribly post-flop and I'll likely face continued aggression. With a healthy 14,175 chip stack, there's no reason to play marginal hands. Preserving chips for premium hands and better spots is the disciplined approach.

I’m on the button with pocket fives (5c5s) and deep stacks (I have ~180 BB, effective vs Flash ~77 BB). Versus the blinds this is a standard spot to open — I want to take the initiative, get heads-up where my small pair has good implied odds, and avoid a limp multiway pot that reduces set-mining value. A 3xBB open to 300 gives good fold equity and isolates or keeps the pot a manageable size in position. Postflop I’ll c-bet dry boards to take it down, slow-play sets, and re-evaluate if I face a large 3-bet (fold to very large 3-bets, call small/medium 3-bets to set-mine).

Folding 6-10 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak, uncoordinated hand that lacks both high card strength and premium connectivity. While I'm only risking an additional 250 chips to call, the hand has poor playability postflop against Minni's wide raising range from the button. Flash hasn't acted from the big blind yet, creating the risk of a 3-bet squeeze. With a healthy stack at 14125 and plenty of hands remaining (99 of 150), there's no reason to play a marginal hand in early position. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 3x the big blind (300). Claude has folded from the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, an Ace in the Big Blind is generally a strong enough hand to defend against a Button open. Calling 200 more into a pot that will be 650 gives me decent odds. While A3o isn't a premium hand, folding it heads-up to a button raise is too tight. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position thereafter.

I have Ace-high on a low, somewhat connected board (4-8-6). As the Big Blind defending against a Button open, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. I do not have a strong enough hand or draw to lead out into the raiser. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 5s on a dry rainbow flop 4h‑8d‑6s. This is a relatively uncoordinated board with no two‑card flush draws and very few meaningful straight combos, so my medium pocket pair is good versus a large portion of the checking range (overcards, floats, weak A/K/Qx). I opened preflop and have position, so taking the initiative with a continuation bet both gets value from worse hands and denies equity to random overcards/floaters.

Sizing: the pot is 650. I want a small-to-medium bet to extract value from worse hands and keep the pot manageable if called. Betting ~250 (~35–40% pot) achieves that: it folds out pure air and gets called by worse hands (A/K/Q/J/T, some singletons and weak pairs) while retaining flexibility going to the turn. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if called I re-evaluate on the turn — continue barreling on blanks, pot-control or fold to significant strength (big check-raise/overpair) — and set-mine/slow-play only if I improve to trips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. On a flop of 4-8-6 rainbow, I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, who bet 250 into a 650 pot. This is a relatively small continuation bet (approx. 38% pot). While my hand is only Ace-high and lacks significant draw potential, the pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 1150) are favorable enough to peel one card. In a three-handed game, folding every non-pair to a single small c-bet on a low board is too predictable. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn, likely folding to further aggression unless I improve to an Ace.

On the turn, the board has developed to 4h-8d-6s-2s. I currently hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Having called a small bet on the flop, my standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and see how they respond. If Minni checks back, I get a free look at the river to potentially hit my Ace or my straight. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing against my drawing odds, but for now, checking is the most appropriate action.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry flop (4h‑8d‑6s) to 250 and was called; pot is 1,150. The turn 2s is a relatively benign card for my range — it gives a few 2x combos that now beat me but doesn't complete straights or flushes for single‑spade holdings. I have a vulnerable middle pair (55) but I have position and a chance to fold out overcards and weaker holdings that called the flop. I’ll continue barreling with a small/medium bet (~35% pot) to extract value and keep the pot manageable; I will fold to a significant check‑raise. If called I’ll re‑evaluate the river and pot‑control or barreL again depending on the card.

On the turn, the board is 4h-8d-6s-2s and the pot is 1550. Minni has bet 400, which is approximately 25% of the total pot including her bet (400 / 1950 ≈ 20.5%). I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). My estimated equity, considering the 4 outs for the straight and 3 potential outs for the Ace, is around 15%. While this is slightly below the direct pot odds required for a break-even call (20.5%), the price is very favorable, and Minni's aggressive history suggests I may have implied odds if I hit my straight on the river. Given the small sizing of the bet, I will call once more and evaluate the river.

I have reached the river with Ace-high after calling two streets. The board is 4-8-6-2-Q. The river Queen is a potential scare card, but my hand has no showdown value against any pair. In this position, I will check to the aggressor. If Minni checks back, I might win against a busted draw, but I am prepared to fold to most bets as Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand here.

Pot is 1,950 and it’s checked to me on the river. I hold a vulnerable medium pair (5♣5♠) on 4♥‑8♦‑6♠‑2♠‑Q♥. The queen is a scary card because it gives Qx many ways to beat me, but Flash checked the river — if he had a Q he’s more likely to bet for thin value than check. He’s been calling small barrels, so his range contains a lot of unpaired overcards and weak pairs that will call a small value bet.

I want to extract thin value from Ax/Kx/Jx and worse pairs/floaters while keeping the pot manageable if I’m behind. A small river bet (~35% pot) achieves that: it gets called by worse hands and folds pure air, and it’s easy to fold to a sizable river raise (which would indicate a made hand or set). I’ll bet 700 chips (≈36% pot). If called I go to showdown; if check‑raised I’ll fold to strong aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The board is 4h-8d-6s-2s-Qh. Throughout the hand, Minni (on the Button) has bet on every street. I called the flop and turn bets with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, but I failed to improve on the river. The river Queen is a card that frequently connects with a Button's opening range. While Minni is an aggressive player, a triple barrel (betting flop, turn, and river) typically signifies a made hand, even if it's just a middle pair or a weak Queen. I need to be right more than 20% of the time to justify a call here, but Ace-high has no showdown value against any pair or a better Ace. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Folding J-2o (Jack-Deuce of hearts) on the button. While button position is favorable and I only need to call 100 chips into a 150 chip pot, J-2 is one of the weakest hands in poker with poor kicker problems. Even in late position with reasonable pot odds, this hand doesn't have sufficient equity to justify a call against two active opponents, especially when considering the aggressive tendencies I've observed from both Flash and Minni. The risk of running into stronger holdings outweighs the positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with KQo, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Claude has folded the button. I have 6,800 chips and only need to put in 50 more to complete the blind or raise. Since there are only two of us left in the hand and KQo performs very well against the Big Blind's range, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a post-flop advantage.

I’m in the BB with JJ facing a 3bb open from the SB. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200; effective stacks ~65bb (opponent 6550). JJ is a strong heads‑up hand but I’m out of position postflop, so a flat call gives the SB too much initiative and good odds to see flops. Folding is too tight; a tiny 3‑bet (min to 500) is too weak and keeps many equity hands in. I want value and fold equity to isolate a wide SB opening range (Axs, Kx, small pairs, suited connectors). I’ll 3‑bet to a standard, constructive size — raise to 1000 total. That’s big enough to take the initiative and earn folds, but small enough to keep postflop maneuvering with ~65bb effective.
