If Flash calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (target C‑bets on favorable textures, check back dangerous Ace/King heavy boards). If he 4‑bets large/shoves, I’ll reevaluate (likely fold JJ to a polarized shove depending on sizing).

I raised from the Small Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Minni responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the Big Blind. While being out of position is a disadvantage, KQo has sufficient equity against a typical 3-betting range in this spot, and I am getting decent pot odds (calling 700 to win a total pot of 2000). I will call to see the flop and evaluate the situation further.

On a board of Th-6h-Tc, I have King-Queen high. This is a paired board that is generally good for the pre-flop aggressor's range (Minni), which likely contains overpairs (JJ, QQ, KK, AA) and strong Ace-highs. However, I have two overcards. Being out of position as the caller of a 3-bet, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the aggressor and see how they act. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands and doesn't provide much information.

Flop: Th 6h Tc. I hold Js Jd — two pair (Jacks & Tens). After my preflop 3‑bet I’m usually ahead of Flash’s calling range except for the small number of Ten/6 combinations that make trips/full houses. Flash checked, so this is a good spot to extract value and deny equity to overcards/backdoor draws while defining his range. I’ll bet for value and protection: pot is 2000, so I size to ~60% to keep worse one‑pair and some floats in but not fold out all of his calling hands. If he calls I plan to proceed cautiously on a Ten/6/Ace turn and re‑evaluate; if he check‑raises big or shoves I’ll re‑assess (those lines polarize to very strong hands and I may fold to an extreme shove).

On a paired board of Th-6h-Tc, I am facing a 60% pot bet (1200 into 2000) from the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. My KQo is currently just King-high with no heart for a flush draw and no backdoor straight potential. Given that Minni 3-bet pre-flop, her range is heavy on overpairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ), strong Tens (AT), and flush draws. Calling here out of position with only overcards is risky, as I am likely behind and will struggle to realize my equity on future streets if I don't improve immediately. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from this position to either steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with A♠2♣. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more (300 total) to continue. With deep stacks and a wide button opening range, A2o is a defendable hand — it blocks many A‑combos and can make top pair or realizable equity postflop. 3‑betting with A2o is suboptimal (hand is often dominated and a min‑raise to 500 is too weak/too committal); folding is too tight vs a wide button open. I want to keep the pot size manageable and preserve postflop maneuverability, so I’ll call the 250 (300 total committed) and evaluate the flop. If it’s heads‑up I can play aggressively with A‑high flops; if Claude (BB) calls and it’s multi‑way I’ll proceed cautiously and look to realize equity rather than bloat the pot without a made hand.

Calling with K9o in the big blind. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds with two opponents already in the pot. While K9o is marginal, the odds justify a call and I can evaluate postflop. Position will be bad (out of position), but the cheap entry allows me to see if the flop is favorable before committing further chips.

Flop is 6♥-4♥-8♣, I have A♠2♣ (ace-high) and I'm first to act in a three-way pot (pot=900). Preflop I defended to keep the pot manageable — same plan now. This is a fairly dry board (main threat is heart flush draws); I'm not beating many of the hands that called preflop (pairs, 8x/6x, heart draws), and a c-bet multiway is rarely going to fold out both opponents. Claude has shown willingness to raise/pressure postflop, so betting here risks a check‑raise and bloating the pot with only A‑high. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity, and lets opponents show their hand/strength. Plan: check; call small continuation bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈300) to see a turn and re-evaluate; fold to large bets or strong check‑raises unless the sizing/pattern indicates a bluff or gives correct pot odds to continue. If checked around, consider a delayed bet on a blank turn or value bet if I pair my Ace.

I'm holding K9o on a 6h-4h-8c flop in the big blind, out of position in a three-way pot. This is a weak holding with just one overcard and no draw. Minni has checked to me, signaling weakness or a marginal hand. Checking is the correct play here because: (1) I have no made hand and checking realizes my hand's equity cheaply; (2) I'm out of position, which makes bluffing risky and inefficient; (3) In a multiway pot, bluffing success rates are lower; (4) If Flash checks behind, I get a free card to potentially hit my king on the turn; (5) If Flash bets, I can make a better-informed decision knowing his likely hand strength. Protecting my chips with a marginal hand is more important than trying to win a pot with poor equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and both opponents have checked to me on an 8-high board. Although I only have Jack-high, I have two overcards. A continuation bet of half the pot is a standard play here to capitalize on the initiative, represent a strong range, and fold out hands that have equity against mine.

I fold. Preflop I defended to keep the pot small and my plan was to check/call small continuation bets (~≤1/3 pot ≈300) and fold to larger sizing or strong aggression. Flash's c‑bet of 450 into 900 (~50% pot) in a three‑way pot is larger than my calling threshold — calling 450 for the current pot gives roughly 3:1 odds (needs ≈25% equity). Versus the button's wide but still pair/draw‑heavy range, and with Claude still in the hand, A♠2♣ has well under that equity and very limited improvement (no heart, only a few aces left). Raising as a bluff out of position is thin and likely to be called or check‑raised. Preserve stack and fold to this sizing; wait for a better spot or a cleaner heads‑up opportunity.

Calling Flash's flop bet. With K9o and 4:1 pot odds in a heads-up situation, calling is justified. I have one overcard to a dry board and can evaluate the turn. My marginal hand deserves a cheap look given the odds, and I can fold to aggression on the turn if needed. Folding would be too passive, and raising is too aggressive with this hand strength.

With K9o on a 6h-4h-8c-7s board heads-up against Flash, checking is the correct play. I have just one overcard with no flush or straight draws, making this a weak hand that doesn't warrant betting from out of position. My flop line established weakness through two checks, and now on the turn, revealing this weakness early allows me to gather more information about Flash's hand strength. If Flash checks, I get a free look at the river. If Flash bets the turn, I can reassess and make a fold/call decision with better information. The pot is already healthy at 1,800, and protecting my marginal holding by checking represents solid fundamentals - no need to blow chips betting weak hands without showdown value. The turn card (7s) didn't improve my hand, so continuing cautiously is prudent.

The turn card (7s) is quite coordinated and likely connects with Claude's flop-calling range (which often includes middle pairs, straight draws, or low connectors). While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and two overcards, I don't want to get check-raised off my equity. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and potentially realize my equity or bluff on a better scare card, while also controlling the size of the pot with a marginal hand.

River decision with K9o on 6h-4h-8c-7s-6d board. I have a weak pair of 6s with K kicker. Flash's flop aggression followed by turn check indicates either a missed draw or marginal hand. Betting would only get called by better hands or vulnerable to river raises. Checking to showdown is the prudent play - it avoids burning additional chips with a hand that has limited showdown value against Flash's likely range. Protecting chips with marginal holdings is fundamental poker strategy.

On a board of 6h-4h-8c-7s-6d, I am holding Jack-high (Jc Th). After my flop continuation bet was called by Claude and we both checked the turn, the river has paired the board. Claude has checked for a third time. While my Jack-high has very little showdown value, Claude's line (check-call flop, check turn, check river) often indicates a marginal made hand (like a 7, 8, or a small pocket pair) that is in 'check-call' mode or a missed draw. Given that my stack is significantly smaller than my opponents and Claude has shown a tendency to call down in previous hands, I will check behind to see the showdown rather than risking a bluff against a range that may not fold a pair on this board.

I'm on the button with 9♠7♦ in a 3-handed spot. With no prior raise and both blinds posted (pot 150), this is a good position to open wider — 79o is marginal but playable in position and has good steal potential. I want meaningful fold equity against both blinds, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing (300) rather than the tiny minimum; that reduces multi-way flats and makes SB pay to defend. If Claude (SB) 3-bets big I’ll fold 79o out of position; if called I’ll use position postflop: c-bet appropriate dry boards, check/control on coordinated/overcards and exploit positional advantage. The raise size keeps the pot manageable while applying pressure and preserving stack flexibility.

I'm in the small blind with 2c 6d, facing a 300 raise from Minni. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker—a weak, unconnected hand with very limited potential. Even though I'm getting reasonable pot odds (need to call 250 to win 450), several factors make folding the correct play: (1) I'm out of position, (2) the Big Blind (Flash) still has to act and could re-raise, (3) my hand has virtually no equity even if I do get to the flop, and (4) there's minimal upside. With a healthy stack of 15,125 chips and 49 hands remaining, I should preserve capital and wait for better opportunities. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. QT is a solid hand to defend with against a wide button opening range, and the pot odds (2.25:1) make this a standard call to see if I can hit top pair or a straight draw.

I have a gutshot straight draw on an Ace-high board. As the defender from the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard procedure on this texture. I will see how Minni reacts to the flop before deciding whether to continue with my draw.

I raised preflop and now have middle pair (9s) on A♣ 9♣ 8♥ with position and the initiative. Flash checked, so his range is wide (missed connectors, weaker pairs, some A-x slowplays). Betting accomplishes three things: (1) extracts value from worse hands and draws, (2) charges overcards and backdoor draws, and (3) keeps pot control since I can fold to a large check-raise. I’ll make a standard half‑pot c-bet (325) — large enough to deny free cards and get value from worse, yet small enough to keep the pot manageable versus this short-stacked opponent. Plan: fold to a sizable check-raise; if called, re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously on A/overcards, continue vs blanks).

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) on an Ace-high board. The pre-flop aggressor, Minni, has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot. With only four outs to a straight and no other significant draws or pair potential, the pot odds of 3:1 are not sufficient to justify a call. Additionally, being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity or bluff effectively on later streets if I miss. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Raising to 300 from the button with K-7o. This applies pressure to the short-stacked Flash, puts Minni in a position to make a decision, and allows me to win the pot immediately or get to a favorable flop. The positional advantage combined with Flash's short stack makes this an ideal blind-stealing situation. If Minni or Flash come back with significant aggression, I can fold and reassess.

I have pocket nines in the small blind, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Claude raised from the button, and given the wide range typical of button opens, my hand is likely ahead. With a stack of 4750 (47.5 big blinds), I want to play aggressively to protect my equity and build a pot. I will 3-bet to 1000, which is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and the big blind while maintaining leverage.

Button opened to 300 and the SB 3-bet to 1,000. I’m in the BB with J♦4♦ — a weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position. To call 900 into a 1,400 pot I need ~39% equity, which J4s won’t have vs a BTN open + SB 3-bet range. Implied odds are limited by the SB’s short stack and 4-betting or calling here is a speculative leak. Fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

Folding K7o to Flash's 3x reraise. While my initial raise applied position pressure, Flash's aggressive reraise over my 300 bet indicates strength or desperation. K7o is simply too weak to call 700 more in a heads-up situation where Flash's range (likely premium pairs, AK, AQ, broadway combinations) dominates my hand. My equity is only around 40-42%, making this a marginal or slightly negative spot. With a healthy stack, I can afford to fold and wait for better opportunities. No need to take marginal spots when chip preservation and position selection are superior strategies.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While being on the button allows for a wider opening range, J7o is generally too weak to open, especially with two aggressive and deep-stacked opponents in the blinds. I currently have a shorter stack (52 BB) compared to the others, so I should look for better spots to commit chips.

Heads-up from the small blind (I’m effectively on the button postflop) with A♣2♥. This is a strong enough hand heads-up to take the initiative — I want to play postflop in position and force Claude to defend a wide range. Table tendency shows many standard 3x opens; raising to 300 is a standard sizing that gives fold equity, isolates the BB, and keeps the pot manageable. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing); if he calls I can c-bet missed boards or get value when I pair my Ace. Action: raise to 300.

AJ is a premium starting hand deserving aggressive play. With 2:1 odds and heads-up play after Flash folded, I'm re-raising to 700 to build the pot, show strength, and control the action. Minni's loose raising tendencies make this an excellent spot to leverage my hand quality. Deep stacks allow me to apply pressure while maintaining flexibility post-flop.

I opened to 300 from the small blind with A♣2♥ to play postflop in position. Claude's 3-bet to 700 is a relatively small sizing and likely contains a fairly wide range. Calling 400 into the 1000 pot (~28.6% break-even) is correct here — A2o has reasonable equity heads-up and I prefer to realize that equity in position rather than folding or thinly 4-betting. With deep stacks I can maneuver postflop (c-bet good textures, check/back when behind, fold to very heavy aggression). I’ll call and re-evaluate on the flop.

Board is 2♣ K♥ J♠; I hold A♣2♥ — bottom pair with the nut kicker to worst-pocket-pair scenarios but dominated by any K/J and by many 3‑bettor value hands. Pot is 1400 and I’m in position with deep stacks. I want to keep the pot controlled and let Claude c-bet a wide 3‑bet range so I can call reasonable bets and realise equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and call small-to-medium c‑bets (~≤1/3–1/2 pot); fold to very large overbets or a check‑raise that polarizes to sets/two‑pair. Checking retains folding equity vs heavy aggression while extracting value from bluffs/draws and weaker holdings.

Betting 750 on the flop with AJ on a 2c-Kh-Js board. This represents strong play with a strong hand. My pair of jacks with ace kicker is well-positioned on this flop against Minni's likely range after calling my pre-flop raise. The bet charges draws, builds the pot, and maintains the aggressive momentum I've established. Minni's check suggests weakness or a marginal hand, making this an ideal spot to apply pressure. The bet size is approximately 54% of the pot, which is standard for a continuation bet while keeping the pot manageable.

Call 750. Pot is 2,150 and I only need ~26% equity to justify a call (750/(2150+750)=~25.9%). I have A♣2♥ on 2♣ K♥ J♠ — bottom pair with the best possible kicker against many of Claude’s 3-bet continuation combos. Claude’s small 3-bet preflop and then a ~53% pot c-bet on the flop represents a fairly wide range (broadway hands, some pocket pairs, suited Aces and bluffs). In position I prefer to realize equity and keep the pot manageable rather than folding or bloating it with a thin raise. Plan after the call: re-evaluate on the turn — if I improve (A or another 2) I’ll go for value; if the turn is a blank and Claude fires a large bet or a polarized check‑raise, I’ll give up; if he checks or makes another small/medium bet I’ll call to realize equity and use position to control the pot.

Board is 2♣ K♥ J♠ K♠ and I hold A♣2♥ — I have a small pair (pair of deuces) with the nut ace kicker, which is far behind any Kx, any Jx (two-pair), and many pocket pairs. Claude 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range is wide but contains lots of Kx/Jx and value hands that now dominate me. He checked the turn, which is consistent with pot‑control with a king, a medium pocket pair, or with missed broadway/Ax hands planning a river stab — it doesn’t polarize in my favor. Leading out here would bloat the pot with a very marginal hand and get called or raised by better hands while folding out only thinner holdings; checking keeps the pot manageable and uses position to make a decision on the river. Plan: check back the turn, call small/medium river bets to realize equity, but fold to large overbets or polarizing river aggression.

Continuation betting the turn with AJ on a 2c-Kh-Js-Ks board. Minni's double-check pattern (flop and turn) indicates weakness, likely a marginal pair or weak King. My pair of Jacks with ace kicker is well-positioned. The duplicated King on the turn doesn't significantly diminish my hand strength relative to Minni's most likely holdings. A 1,600 bet (55% of pot) maintains aggression, extracts value from weaker pairs, and keeps the pot manageable. This continues the aggressive line I established pre-flop and on the flop while positioning me well for the river decision.

I have A♣2♥ — bottom pair with the nut Ace kicker — facing 1,600 into a 4,500 pot on the turn. Calling requires ~26% equity (1,600 / (4,500+1,600)), but Claude’s line (3‑bet preflop, flop c‑bet, turn barrel after a King pairs the board) is heavily weighted toward Kx, Jx and strong pocket pairs that dominate me. My clean outs are tiny (two remaining 2s to trips ≈4.3%, three Aces ≈6.5% but an Ace often ties or still loses versus AK/KK), so my realistic equity is well below the break‑even. Raising is a thin bluff with poor blockers and would commit too many chips against a player who shows value‑heavy lines. Fold and preserve the stack — I’ll wait for a better spot in position.

I’m on the button with Js8c in a three-handed pot (pot 150, to call 100). This is a classic steal spot — Js8o isn't premium but it plays well in position and can be opened widely vs the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) — big enough to apply pressure to the blinds and deny correct calling odds to the BB, but small relative to my deep stack if faced with aggression. Reads: Claude (SB) is an active/aggro player who will defend or 3-bet sometimes; Flash (BB) is the shorter stack and will often fold or call. Plan: if both fold I pick up the pot; if called I’ll use position to evaluate and c-bet favorable flops; I will fold to a large 3-bet or shove from SB, and only continue vs small cold-calls/flat calls.

Q7 offsuit is too weak to call from small blind against a button raise. I'm out of position postflop, which makes this hand even more difficult to play. With a healthy stack and many hands remaining, I should fold and wait for better opportunities where I have either hand strength or positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with A2o. Minni raised from the Button to 300, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. A2o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a wide Button opening range. While it doesn't play great post-flop, the pot odds (2.25:1) and the fact that I have an Ace blocker make a call the most reasonable play here.

I have Ace-high on a paired board (J-J-5). Minni raised pre-flop from the button and I called from the big blind. Usually, a continuation bet is expected from the pre-flop aggressor here. Checking is the standard play for the big blind to see how the button reacts to this board. If Minni checks back, my Ace-high has good showdown value; if she bets, I can decide based on the sizing.

I flopped trips (Js in hand + Jc Jh on board) in a heads-up pot vs the BB who checked. This is a very strong made hand and we want to extract value from any Jx, pocket pairs (5s/set, smaller pairs), and weaker showdown hands. The board is fairly dry (no real draws), so a straightforward value c-bet is best — big enough to build the pot but not so big that we fold out every worse hand. I’ll bet about half the pot to target calls from top-pair/jack combos and medium pairs while keeping weaker hands and bluffs interested. If he check-raises I’ll re-evaluate (likely call or 3-bet given trips); if he folds we take it down now.

On a paired board of J-J-5, Minni is very likely to continuation bet with her entire button opening range. My Ace-high (As2d) still has significant showdown value and beats all of her complete air (like 89s, 67s, etc.) and some of her high-card bluffs. I am getting 3:1 odds to call. Folding immediately to a single C-bet on this texture is too tight, especially since the Ace can be an out if I am behind a small pocket pair. I will call to see the turn and evaluate her aggression level.

On the turn, the 6c is largely a blank. My Ace-high (As2d) still has showdown value against a wide button opening range. I called the flop continuation bet because the board was paired (J-J-5), making it likely Minni would bet with much of her range. Checking is the standard play here to see if she chooses to double barrel or check back to realize equity. Leading into her doesn't serve much of a purpose for my hand strength.

I have trip Jacks (Js with Jc Jh on board) in position in a heads-up pot. Pot is 1,300 and Flash (BB, stack 4,575) checked the turn (6c) after calling my flop bet — his calling range is wide (Jx, medium pairs, some 5x, weaker Ax, and speculative hands). The turn 6c is largely a blank — it doesn't add many straights and only slightly helps 66-type hands; the primary hands that already beat me are pocket 5s (full house) and very rarely slow-played monsters. My goal is to extract value from Jx, worse pocket pairs, and 5x while keeping worse hands and marginal J-catchers in the pot. A half-pot to ~2/3-pot turn bet gives good value without folding out most of his calling range, and his short stack means he can still call. I’ll bet ~650 (about half the pot). If he check-raises I’ll re-evaluate (likely call or commit given the strength and his tendency to call), but I won’t thin-value by checking back.

On the turn, the 6c is mostly a blank, but Minni has followed up her flop continuation bet with a second barrel of half the pot. While I called the flop with Ace-high (As2d) due to the paired board texture, calling a second street of aggression with no draw and only Ace-high is much riskier. Minni's button range is wide, but it contains many hands that have now improved or are already beating me (any pocket pair, any 5 or 6, any Jack). Furthermore, I do not hold the Ace of clubs, so I have no backdoor flush potential. Calling here often leads to a difficult river decision if she continues her aggression. Given the board and her betting pattern, I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I'm on the button with 4♦2♦, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While position is favorable, this hand is not worth playing in a 3-handed scenario where both opponents have demonstrated aggressive tendencies throughout the session. Flash (SB: 4,525) and Minni (BB: 19,100) are both active players who frequently raise and bet. Even from the button, 4-2 lacks both high card value and meaningful connectivity. The pot is small (150 chips), and the risk-reward doesn't justify a call or raise here. I should preserve my stack (16,225) and wait for a premium hand in position where I can play profitably against these aggressive opponents.

In Hand #106, I am in the Small Blind with 6s7c, which is a marginal hand. Claude has already folded from the Button. It's just me and Minni, the Big Blind. The cost to complete the bet is 50 chips into a 150-chip pot. Minni has been quite aggressive, frequently raising and re-raising pre-flop. While 6-7 offsuit can be played in some heads-up scenarios, completing here out of position against an aggressive Big Blind is often asking for trouble post-flop. However, given the pot odds (3-to-1), I could consider a call or a raise, but with 150 hands to play and a stack that has dwindled, I should look for better spots to commit chips. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being outplayed out of position.

I am on the button with 3s 9d. This is a very weak hand (offsuit gapper, low card strength). The pot is small (150) and while I only need to call 100 to see more action, standard professional play dictates folding such weak hands from any position to avoid getting into difficult post-flop situations with no equity or fold equity.

I’m heads-up against the big blind (Flash folded) in the small blind with 8♠2♠. I’ve already posted 50 and the to-call is only another 50; calling 50 into a 150 pot gives ~25% break-even equity. 8-2 suited is weak but has some heads-up equity and potential to realize implied odds; raising to 200 would unnecessarily inflate a pot out of position with a marginal holding. I’ll defend the blind by calling the 50 (total committed 100) and proceed cautiously on the flop—fold to heavy aggression unless I connect or pick up a good draw.

Checking with K♦2♦ against a limper from the small blind. This is a weak hand that benefits from seeing a cheap flop. Raising would overcommit chips to a marginal holding. By checking, I can either hit a king on the flop or fold to aggression if the board misses. This conservative approach is appropriate given the hand's weakness and my out-of-position situation.

Pot is 200, no bet to call. I hold 8♠2♠ on 4♠-3♥-7♣: no pair, only a very weak backdoor flush (need both turn and river spades — ~4.4% to complete) and tiny backdoor straight potential. I’m out of position vs one opponent who checked preflop. With almost no realized equity and no fold equity worth risking chips OOP, the correct line is pot control: check and see a turn card. If Claude bets, I’ll fold to meaningful pressure — only consider continuing to the turn with an extremely small bet that gives proper pot odds (unlikely here). If I pick up a strong draw on the turn (e.g. spade that changes dynamics to a 4‑card flush), I can reevaluate. For now: check and conserve stack.

Checking K♦2♦ on a 4♠3♥7♣ board after Minni's weak SB check. With only king-high and potential draws (gutshot, backdoor flush), this marginal hand benefits from seeing another card cheaply. Betting would overcommit to weak equity, while checking keeps the pot small and maintains flexibility for the turn. This conservative approach is standard when out of position with weak hands.

Check. I have 8♠2♠ and missed the board — after the turn the backdoor flush is dead and I have no pair or realistic straight draw, so virtually no showdown equity OOP. Pot is small (200); best play is pot control and conserve chips: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude checks back I’ll see the river and re-evaluate, only firing a bluff on a very favorable river card or clear weakness.

With K♦2♦ on a 4♠3♥7♣ - 10♣ board, I have king-high which is a marginal hand. Minni has checked both the flop and turn, indicating either weakness or a slow-played hand, but more likely weakness given the board texture. Flash folded pre-flop. With no strong equity and being out of position, checking allows me to see the river cheaply without overcommitting chips to a weak holding. If I had a strong hand, aggressive betting would be warranted, but here I benefit from seeing another card at no cost. The backdoor flush draw and gutshot provide minimal equity, so river cards that don't give me a pair or flush draw will likely be a check-fold situation. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size and maintain flexibility on the river.

I have nothing on the river (8♠2♠ missed the backdoor flush and any pair/straight). Pot is tiny (200) and both of us have checked every street — Claude has shown passive behavior this hand, which is often weakness or a marginal holding. Betting a small-to-medium bluff here (~1/2 pot) is low-risk and can pick up the pot against missed hands and overcards. A 100 chip bet requires Claude to fold >33% of his checking range to be profitable (break-even fold freq = 100/(200+100) ≈ 33%). I’m not invested in this pot and will not bloat it; if Claude raises I’ll give up. Bet 100 to try and steal the pot.

Folding K♦2♦ on the river. With only king-high against an opponent who just bet after being passive throughout the hand, my hand is too weak to justify calling. While pot odds (3:1) suggest marginal callability, Minni's river bet after checking the flop and turn indicates she likely improved (pair or better with the 6♥, or completed a draw). King-high has poor equity in this spot. This maintains consistency with my conservative approach throughout the hand and avoids overcommitting chips with a marginal holding.

7♠8♥ offsuit is a marginal but playable hand on the Button — good enough to see a flop because I have positional advantage. The pot is 150 and the cost to see the flop is only 100 (pot odds ~40%), so a call is cheap to realize equity and keep my options postflop. A min-raise here is possible as a steal, but Claude (SB) is capable of defending/3-betting and Flash (BB, ~44bb) will often call; I’d rather avoid getting bloated in a multiway pot out of position. Plan: call, see the flop, and use my position to c-bet or fold to heavy resistance depending on texture/equity.

Holding Q6o in the small blind with a fairly deep stack (16,075). Minni limped from the button for 100, Flash is in the big blind with a short stack (4,425). I need to call only 50 more to complete the small blind and see the flop. In this 3-handed game, Q6 is a reasonable holding, especially with position post-flop. Minni's limp is unusual given his aggressive tendencies in the session - he typically raises. Completing the SB for a small additional investment with position advantage is mathematically sound. I can apply pressure on the flop with my range and position. Folding Q6 when I've already committed 50 and can see a flop with positional advantage post-flop doesn't make sense.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Diamonds and Deuce of Diamonds (Jd 2d). The Button (Minni) limped and the Small Blind (Claude) also limped. I have a suited hand, although the kicker is weak. The pot is currently 300 chips and it is checked to me. There is no reason to raise here with a weak hand like J2s out of position against two players who have already shown interest, even if only by limping. I will check to see a free flop and evaluate my hand then.

Q6 is a complete whiff on a 5-2-J board with no pair, draws, or showdown value. I'm out of position as small blind and should avoid building a pot with a weak hand. Checking allows me to see what Minni and Flash do while keeping the pot manageable. If they check through, I can potentially bluff later or reassess on the turn. This is a passive play but correct given the weakness of my holding and disadvantaged position.

On the flop of Jc 5s 2c, I have hit top two pair (Jacks and Deuces). The pot is small (300) with three players involved. Claude has checked to me. While the board has some straight and flush draw potential (clubs), my hand is very strong in a limped pot. I want to build the pot and protect my hand. I will bet 200, which is two-thirds of the pot, to get value from weaker Jacks, draws, or middle pairs like fives.

Preflop I called 7♠8♥ on the button to use position. On the flop (5♠ 2♣ J♣) I have no pair and essentially no clean draw — only very remote backdoor straight possibilities and I don’t hold a club while there are two clubs on board (others can have flush draws). Flash (BB) bets 200 into a 500 pot. Calling 200 here means I must win ~22% of the time to break even (200 / (500 + 2*200) ≈ 22%). Against a realistic c-bet range (made Jx, pocket pairs, club draws, A‑high) my true equity is likely at or below that number and will drop further in a multiway pot if Claude (SB) wakes up. Raising as a bluff is thin — I have poor blockers and will often get called by hands that dominate or have good equity. With no constructive reason to build the pot and plenty of better spots behind, I fold and preserve my stack.

Fold Q6o on a 5-2-J board. This hand has no equity against Flash's flop raise - no pair, no draws, and insufficient equity (~20-25%) for pot odds of 2.5:1. Being out of position with a weak hand against an aggressive short stack makes this a straightforward fold. With a healthy stack (16,025), I can wait for better spots.

Holding 4♠A♠ on the button against Flash (SB: 4,675) and Minni (BB: 19,150). While I'm in position, A-4 suited is a marginal hand with a weak kicker. My stack is healthy at 16,025, and I should be selective about which marginal hands to enter pots with. Flash is short-stacked and likely to be desperate, but Minni has a substantial stack (19,150). Getting involved with a weak hand here doesn't offer good risk-reward when I can wait for better spots. Folding preserves chips for premium hands where I have a clearer advantage.

I'm in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo). Since the Button (Claude) has folded, I'm in a head-up situation with the Big Blind (Minni). QTo is a strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately or define the hand against Minni, who has been playing relatively loose-aggressive.

Flash opened to 300 from the small blind; I’m in the big blind with 9♠5♠. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives me good immediate pot odds and lets me realize the hand’s equity — 9-5 suited has backdoor flush/straight potential and plays well heads-up. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to 3‑bet this marginal holding; a flat call keeps his wide SB opening range and allows me to see a flop cheaply. Plan: continue when I hit a spade/pair or a strong draw; fold or proceed cautiously to aggression if I miss and face strong pressure.

On this 8c-7c-5h flop, I have two overcards and a backdoor straight draw. While I haven't hit a pair, this board texture is quite coordinated and favors the defender's (Minni's) range, as it includes many straight draws and two-pair combinations. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet to test Minni's strength and potentially win the pot right here. A half-pot bet is appropriate to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

Board is 8♣ 7♣ 5♥ and I hold 9♠5♠ — bottom pair with a weak kicker, a gutshot to a 6, and only backdoor spade possibilities. Flash (SB) opened preflop and is making a fairly small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot). Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)=25%), and against a typical SB c-bet range (lots of overcards, some clubs, some pairs) my hand has enough equity and beats a lot of air. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair and give the SB easy spots to shove or bluff‑raise. I’ll call to realize equity and reevaluate on the turn — if I improve to two pair/trips/straight or pick up a spade I can play aggressively; if I miss and face strong pressure I’ll fold or pot‑control.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades gives me top pair with a decent kicker. While the board is highly coordinated with potential straights and a flush draw, I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against those draws. Many of Minni's calling range on the flop (like middle pairs or straight draws) will likely still call a bet here. I will bet 750, which is approximately 62.5% of the pot, to maintain initiative and extract value.

Call. I have bottom pair (5♠) with a 9 kicker in a 3‑way pot but effectively heads‑up vs the SB. Flash’s line (SB open, small flop c‑bet, another barrel on the Q turn) contains a lot of thin value (Qx, 8x, 7x, 5x) and plenty of bluffs/overcards. The pot is 1,950 and calling 750 gives ~27.8% pot odds. My raw river outs to two‑pair/trips are only ~5 cards (~11%), but I’m already ahead of many unpaired hands and bluffs, so my overall equity vs his barreling range is comparable to the odds. Raising would polarize and commit too much out of position with a marginal hand; folding gives up too much. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot under control — on the river I’ll extract value if I improve or he checks, and fold to a large shove if I miss and he applies heavy pressure.

On the river, the board has become extremely coordinated with the Six of Diamonds (6d), creating a four-card straight (5-6-7-8). Any 9 or 4 in Minni's hand now completes a straight. Given that Minni has called two streets on a board that heavily favored a defender's range (containing many middle cards and straight draws), it is highly likely she has a piece of this board. While I have top pair with Queens, my kicker is only a Ten, and I am beat by any straight, set, or two-pair combination (like 87, 76, 65). Betting here offers little value as most hands that call will beat me, and I risk being raised off the best hand if she is bluffing. Checking is the safest way to reach showdown or evaluate a bet from her based on the pot odds.

I hold 9♠5♠ — bottom pair (5s) with a weak kicker on a board 8♣-7♣-5♥-Q♠-6♦. Flash’s line (SB open, small flop c-bet, a turn barrel on the Q, then checking the river) is full of thin value hands (Qx, 8x, 7x, some 5x) and some bluffs. My hand is behind most top pairs/two-pair/sets and beaten by any completed straight (a 9 or a 4), but it beats missed overcards and pure bluffs. With the river checked I don’t want to simply give up value from worse hands that will check behind; at the same time I don’t want to bloat the pot and invite a raise from straights/full houses. I’ll make a small value-targeted bet (~1/3 pot) to extract calls from Qx/8x/7x and thin callers while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash reacts with a large raise or shove I will fold — this is a fold-to-raise spot given how vulnerable my one pair is to straights and bigger pairs.

On the river, the board has completed a four-card straight (5-6-7-8), meaning any 9 or 4 in Minni's hand makes a straight. Additionally, the Six of Diamonds (6d) creates several two-pair possibilities for a defender's range (like 7-6 or 6-5). While I have top pair with Queens, my kicker is only a Ten, and Minni has called both the flop and the turn on a very coordinated board, which is a strong signal that she has a piece of it. Her bet of 900 into a 2700 pot (1/3 pot) often represents a value bet or a 'blocking' bet with a made hand. Given the high probability that she has a straight, two pair, or a better Queen (like QJ or QK), my top pair has become a weak bluff-catcher. I do not believe she is bluffing frequently enough here to justify a call, especially since almost all hands she would value bet have me beaten.
On the button with Ace-Nine offsuit (As 9h), I have a strong enough hand to open for a raise, especially in a three-handed game. Opening to 300 is standard and puts pressure on the blinds.

Call to 300. J5s is a marginal but playable suited hand from the small blind and I only need to add 250 to see a flop — good pot odds to defend against a wide button open. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with this hand (isolating a short-stacked button or bloating a pot out of position versus Claude), and folding here surrenders too much equity for the price. Plan: if we see a flop and miss, keep the pot controlled and fold to heavy aggression; continue with top-pair, strong draws, or nut-flush/turn improvement and exploit any weaknesses postflop.

I'm in the Big Blind with 6c-2s, the worst hand in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button, Minni called 250 from the small blind. While I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds (700 to call 200), this is a clear fold.

Key factors:
1. 6-2 offsuit is the weakest possible starting hand
2. I'm out of position (Big Blind) facing two opponents
3. Both opponents have shown strength through betting/calling
4. My position disadvantage compounds the weakness of my hand
5. Even decent pot odds cannot overcome the fundamental weakness of playing 6-2 out of position against two players

This is a straightforward fold. Playing marginal or weak hands out of position is a common leak, and folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities. With 40 hands remaining and a healthy chip lead in this session, there's no need to take marginal risks.

Flop 6♦‑4♥‑7♥ with J♠5♠ — I completely missed: no pair, no heart draw, only a very unlikely backdoor spade. I’m out of position to the pre‑flop raiser (Flash) and the pot is already 700 while he has ~3k behind. With a marginal hand I want to keep the pot small and follow my preflop plan: check for pot control and induce bluffs or free cards. I will fold to heavy aggression or a shove, but will consider calling a small, non‑committal c‑bet to see a cheap turn if the price is right. Checking preserves my stack and keeps marginal hands in my range rather than bloating an OOP pot with little equity.

On a low, connected board like 6-4-7 with a heart draw, Minni's calling range from the small blind is very likely to have connected or picked up a draw. My Ace-Nine offsuit has some showdown value but doesn't want to build a large pot or face a check-raise. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate the next card.

Turn gives me second pair (J♠5♠ on 6♦‑4♥‑7♥‑5♦). I was OOP and checked the flop for pot control; this pair is weak but has showdown value versus Flash’s wide button raising range. With the pot only 700 and Flash ~3k behind, a small value/protection bet is best — it extracts from worse (A/K/Qx and other non‑pairs), charges any two‑card heart or straight draws, and keeps the pot manageable if he shows aggression. I size to roughly half‑pot (350) to get value and fold equity. If Flash raises big or shoves, that line is heavily polarized to hands that beat me (6/7/4, straights, big pairs), so I’ll fold to a large raise/all‑in and re‑evaluate on a simple call; if he calls I’ll reassess on the river.

The turn card (5d) is extremely dangerous as it completes a four-card straight on the board (4-5-6-7). Any 8 or 3 in Minni's range now has a straight. While I have an Ace and a Nine, the Nine only helps if an 8 appears on the river to give me a 9-high straight, and even then, I could be losing to a 10-9 or 9-8. Minni's lead of 350 into 700 indicates she likely has a piece of this highly connected board or is capitalizing on the fact that I checked the flop. Given I have no made hand and very poor equity against a range that connects so well with this board, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Qc3h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and a bad kicker that will frequently be dominated. Here I face two players to act (aggressive SB Claude and a ~30bb BB who can readily jam); calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to breakeven (100/(150+100)=40%), which Q3 rarely has versus their defending ranges. Raising to attempt a steal invites a shove or a 3-bet and puts me in marginal spots out of position postflop. With deep stacks and lots of hands left, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold this one.

Despite holding 7-3o (the weakest hand in poker), the pot odds of 4:1 make this a mathematically profitable call. In heads-up play, 7-3 has sufficient equity (~32%) to justify calling. Additionally, Flash's short stack (2,975) creates additional value, and my small blind position provides a postflop advantage. The risk/reward favors seeing a flop here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 2s, the weakest starting hand. Claude, the Small Blind, has merely completed. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can improve my hand for free.

With 7-3 offsuit on a flop of 8c-Jh-8h, I have absolutely zero equity - no pair, no draw, and the worst two-card hand in poker. Flash checked from the big blind, indicating weakness or uncertainty. In this situation, the mathematically correct play is to take the free card rather than bluff with no hand equity. My stack (15,825) is healthy, Flash's stack (2,975) is short, and pot control is essential. Checking allows me to see the turn at no additional cost, where I might hit a 7 or 3, or evaluate Flash's turn action. Betting or raising with 7-3o would be poor bankroll management and would only add chips to the pot with a hand that has no value. I'm content to check this down and either fold to a bet or get to showdown if we both check.

I have 7-high on a board featuring a pair of eights and a Jack. My hand has no showdown value and no draw. Since Claude has checked, I will check back to see the turn for free, as betting with the weakest possible hand here is unlikely to be productive.

With 7-3o and zero equity on a paired board (8c-Jh-8h-6c), checking is the mathematically optimal play. I have no hand strength and no draws - betting would only waste chips. Both opponents have checked, indicating weakness or caution. Checking lets me see the river for free and lets Flash determine the action with their hand. This is standard pot control with a hopeless hand. My short-term goal is to minimize losses when holding the worst hand in poker.

I have 7-high with no value or draws on a board featuring a pair of eights and a Jack. Claude, the only other player in the hand, has checked the turn after completing the small blind pre-flop and checking the flop. While I have no hand, there is no reason to bet into a small pot with the worst possible hand. I will check and see the river for free.

With 7-3 offsuit on a final board of 8c-Jh-8h-6c-2d, I have zero hand value - no pair, no draw, and the worst two-card combination possible. Both opponents have checked all the way through (pre-flop through turn), and I'm now on the river with nothing to call. 

Checking is the only rational action here. While my hand cannot win at showdown against any opponent holding a single card higher than my 7 kicker, betting would be throwing away chips with no hand strength whatsoever. Flash has demonstrated weakness by checking throughout, making a bluff impossible to justify when they can simply call with garbage and beat my 7-high.

The mathematically correct play is to minimize losses on a hopeless hand by taking the free showdown. This hand exemplifies the importance of tight position-based bankroll management - I got in cheaply from the small blind with poor odds, and now I must simply accept the loss and move to the next hand.

Check to the showdown.

The hand has been checked down to the river on a board of 8c Jh 8h 6c 2d. I hold 7d 2s, which gives me a pair of Eights (on the board) with a Jack-Seven-Six kicker. Since Claude has checked every street after completing from the Small Blind, he likely has a very weak hand, but even Ace-high or a Nine-kicker would beat me. There is no value in betting this hand as a bluff in a tiny 200-chip pot, and I have minimal showdown value. Checking is the most sensible action to reach showdown for free.

5-3 offsuit is the weakest hand in poker. Despite favorable button position and low cost to call (100 into 150 pot), this hand has poor equity against any reasonable range. It cannot make strong straights, pairs are weak with poor kicker, and it loses to nearly all holdings. With a decent 158 BB stack and 43 hands remaining, I should fold weak hands and preserve chips for better spots. Folding is standard GTO strategy even from the button.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5s, a speculative suited hand that can play well in some post-flop scenarios. Claude on the button has already folded, leaving only me and Minni in the Big Blind. I only need to put in 50 more chips to call and see a flop. Given my stack size of 3125 and the pot odds being 3:1, calling to see a flop is a reasonable play to try and hit a strong draw or two pair, especially since I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is too tight, and raising is a bit risky against Minni who has been active.

I’m in the big blind with 8♦3♥ — a very weak offsuit hand. There’s no raise to me (pot 200, to call 0) and the small blind (Flash, ~30BB) has already completed. No reason to inflate the pot preflop with junk: I’ll check and see a flop. Plan to fold to significant aggression or a large C-bet on most flops, only continue if I hit or the texture and opponent’s actions give a clear opportunity to bluff/float.

I have 8-high on a board of Jack-Ten-Deuce. I have no draws and very little showdown value. Given the board texture, it's likely Minni has a piece of it or a better high card. Checking is the most sensible play to see what she does without committing more chips to a weak hand.

I have 8♦3♥ — complete air on J♦ T♣ 2♥ with no meaningful backdoors. Flash (SB, ~30BB) has already checked; pot is small (200) and there’s no reason to bluff into a single caller with this hand. I’ll keep the pot small and evaluate further streets — check now and fold to any significant bet. If Flash checks through I can reassess on the turn, but I won’t bloat the pot with junk.

On the turn, the board is Jd-Tc-2h-6h. I have 8-high with no draws and no showdown value. Both Minni and I checked the flop, suggesting a weak range for both of us. However, with absolutely no equity, there is no reason to lead out with a bet. I will check and hope to see a cheap river or simply fold if Minni bets, as my hand has no potential to improve to a winner here.

I’m holding 8♦3♥ on J♦ T♣ 2♥ 6♥ — basically complete air. Flash (SB, ~30BB) has checked both flop and turn; pot is only 200 and there are no realistic outs (flush is impossible with one card to come and I don’t have a straight or pair). No reason to bloat the pot or bluff into a single passive opponent. I’ll check and conserve chips, folding to any meaningful river bet and only considering a tiny bluff if the river bricks and Flash checks again.

I have 8-high on a board of J-T-2-6-T. I have checked the flop and turn, as has Minni. My hand has almost zero showdown value; I only beat absolute air like 7-high or lower. However, in a small pot of 200 chips that has been checked down, there is a high likelihood that Minni is holding at least an Ace-high or a King-high, both of which beat me. While a bluff could potentially take the pot, Minni has shown a willingness to call small river bets in the past. Given the risk-to-reward ratio in such a small pot, I will check and hope for a check-back, or fold if she bets, rather than committing more chips with no equity.

I hold 8d3h — complete air on Jd Tc 2h 6h Td. The SB (Flash, ~30BB) has been passive (checked flop and turn) and the pot is tiny (200). Bluffing here has poor fold equity against a passive calling range (Tx, Jx, small pairs) that will often call, so I shouldn't bloat the pot with junk. I’ll check to see showdown and fold to any reasonable river bet.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sevens (7d 7s). This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or thin the field. A standard raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here to apply pressure on the blinds.

I have A♠3♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 with the big blind still to act. A3s is worth defending: it has nut-flush potential, an Ace blocker, and good post‑flop playability. Calling 250 more (300 total) keeps the pot controlled and avoids isolating against Claude (BB, deep and capable of 3-bets/squeezes) or inducing a shove from Flash (≈29bb) when my kicker can be dominated. Pot odds are reasonable (if BB folds I need ~36% equity to break even), and if we see a favourable flop we can realize equity or take a free card; if not I can fold to heavy action. For these reasons I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate.

Folding 6-4 offsuit in the big blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker and not worth calling 200 into 700 out of position against two opponents. With a healthy stack of 15,725, I can afford to wait for premium hands. Pot odds don't justify playing a dominated hand heads-up against better ranges.

I will check. I have A♠3♠ and missed this T♦ J♣ T♠ flop — only Ace‑high plus a very weak backdoor spade draw and an Ace blocker. The button opened preflop and this board heavily favors his continuing range (any T, any J, many broadways and medium pairs), so I am typically behind to his value hands. I’m out of position, and a lead/bluff here lacks sufficient fold equity and showdown backup — betting will frequently be called by Jx/Tx/mids and will bloat a pot I’m likely losing. Checking controls the pot, lets me call small c‑bets to realize my backdoor/overcard equity (and exploit bluffs), and preserves the option to fold to big pressure. Plan: check; call small-to-medium c‑bets to see a turn, fold to large bets/shove unless I improve.

On a board of Td-Jc-Ts, my pocket sevens are vulnerable. The board contains a pair of tens and a jack, which hit a large portion of a small blind's calling range. Since I am in position and the board is quite coordinated for high cards, checking back is a safe way to control the pot and see how the turn develops. I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff here, as it still has some showdown value against missed high cards, but it isn't strong enough to value bet against a range that likely contains many Jacks or Tens.

I have A♠3♠ and missed this T♦ J♣ T♠ 5♥ runout — only Ace‑high plus a remote backdoor spade. Both players checked the flop which signals weakness from the preflop raiser but also removes a lot of fold equity for a bluff. Out of position with very little equity, betting here mostly bloats a pot I’m likely behind or gets called by better hands; folding to a shove is also unnecessary when I can control the pot. I’ll check to keep the pot small, realize my (very limited) equity, and reserve the option to call a small turn/river bet or fold to substantial pressure. Re‑evaluate after the river and only continue if the price is favorable or I improve.

On a board of Td-Jc-Ts-5h, my pocket sevens (7d 7s) have decent showdown value but are vulnerable to any Jack, Ten, or higher pocket pair in Minni's range. Minni has checked to me twice now. While she could be slow-playing a monster, it's more likely she has a weak pair or a drawing hand like KQ. By checking back on the turn, I control the size of the pot and ensure I get to see a river card for free. There is little value in betting here, as better hands (Jacks or Tens) won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call, except perhaps for straight draws which I'd rather not face a check-raise from.

I missed again — A♠3♠ is only Ace‑high on T♦ J♣ T♠ 5♥ Q♥. Both players checked flop and turn, and the river Q improves a lot of hands in Flash’s likely continuing range (Qx, Jx, Tx, pocket pairs). I do block some Ax combos, but not nearly enough to turn this into a profitable bluff: a bet will be called by most Queens/Jacks/Tens and small pairs and will only fold the very worst Kx or low missed hands, so the fold equity needed isn’t there. I’m out of position, the pot is small (700) relative to stacks, and betting just bloats a pot I’m unlikely to win — checking and taking my showdown is the highest EV line. I’ll check and show/cut my losses rather than bluff at a river where I have minimal fold equity.

On a board of Td-Jc-Ts-5h-Qh, my pocket sevens (7d 7s) technically form two pair: Tens and Sevens with a Queen kicker (T-T-7-7-Q). While this hand has showdown value, it is vulnerable to any Jack, Queen, or Ten in Minni's range, as well as any pocket pair higher than sevens. Minni has checked through the flop, turn, and river. Given the coordinated nature of the board, it is possible she is holding a marginal hand like a Jack or a Queen and is playing it cautiously, or she has completely missed. Betting here would likely only result in being called by hands that beat me (Jacks, Queens, or better) and folding out hands I already beat (Ace-high, etc.). Therefore, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value.

On the button with 2♣4♣ — a weak, speculative suited connector that plays best in position. The cost to see a flop is only 100 into a 150 pot, so I’ll take the cheap flop with position and retain flexibility. Raising to try a steal risks a shove from the short-stacked BB or a 3-bet from Claude out of the SB, which would put me in a tough spot with a dominated low hand. Call 100, look to continue only with a pair, good draw, or favorable board texture; fold to large aggression otherwise.

I'm holding 6s-2h (6-2 offsuit), which is statistically the worst starting hand in poker. Despite only needing to call 50 chips with decent pot odds (5:1), this hand is fundamentally unplayable. The weak kicker makes it nearly impossible to win the pot profitably even if I hit a pair. With Flash short-stacked (3,475 chips) and Minni having called from the Button, I should fold and wait for a better opportunity. My tight image and chip position allow me to be selective with hand selection.

I am in the Big Blind and the Button has limped. There has been no raise, so I have the option to check and see the flop for free. Jack-Five offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't benefit from raising into a large stack out of position. I will check and see if I can improve on the flop.

I have Jack-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. The flop is Ace-Six-Seven with two hearts. This board does not connect with my hand at all, as I have no pair, no draw, and only a weak Jack high. Minni limped from the button pre-flop. I will check to see if I can get a free turn card, but I intend to fold if Minni bets.

I have 2♣4♣ on A♣6♥7♥ — no pair, only a very weak backdoor club flush (three clubs including the A♣) and runner-runner straight possibilities. Flash is checked in the BB with a short stack (≈3.5k) and will be able to shove over a c-bet, which I don’t want to face with essentially zero showdown value. Pot is only 250; this is a classic spot to pot-control and see a free turn in position rather than bluff into an Ace-heavy range. I’ll check and preserve my stack, plan to fold to significant aggression if I don’t improve, or extract/continue if I pick up a real draw or pair on the turn.

The turn card is the King of Spades. I still have nothing but Jack high with a weak kicker. The pot is small (250 chips) and I have checked the flop. Minni also checked the flop. Since there is still no betting action and I have no hand or draw, there is no reason to bet. I will check and hope to see a free river card.

Board: A♣ 6♥ 7♥ K♠. My hand 2♣4♣ has no pair. The backdoor club draw that existed on the flop is dead after the K♠ turn (only one card to come), so I have essentially zero showdown equity and no real draw. Pot is tiny (250) and the only active opponent (Flash, ~3.5k) has checked flop and turn — either weak or pot‑controlling. With no fold equity vs a short stack that can shove and no value to extract, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and see the river. If Flash bets a reasonable amount on the river I’ll fold; if he checks through I’ll re-evaluate the river card for a potential small bluff only on very safe runouts or bet for value if I improve.

The board has several high cards (Ace, King) and a pair of Sevens. Throughout the hand, there has been no betting action from either myself or Minni. My Jack-high has very little showdown value, but in a limped pot where everyone has checked three streets, there is a small chance it could win against a totally missed draw or a lower high card. Betting into this board is unlikely to represent enough strength to fold out a better hand, and checking allows me to see the showdown for free or fold if Minni decides to bet.

Board A♣ 6♥ 7♥ K♠ 7♣ and I hold 2♣4♣ — no pair, no made draw, essentially zero showdown value. Pot is tiny (250) and Flash checked flop, turn and just checked the river, which signals weakness or straightforward pot control. A river bluff here is low EV: he can profitably call small with A‑high or any small pair, and if he jams I face unnecessary risk with nothing. I have a large stack advantage and should preserve chips for better spots, so the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and concede the pot unless he puts in a bet (in which case I fold).

Raising with A9 suited from the button is standard. This is a premium hand in late position that plays well both in isolation and heads-up. A 3x BB raise (300 chips) is appropriate sizing - strong enough to build the pot and maintain aggression while not overcommitting. From the button, I have positional advantage and can apply pressure to both blinds. Flash's short stack makes this a favorable raising spot, and even if Minni calls, I'll have position and a strong hand to work with post-flop.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) in the Small Blind. This is a premium starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. With a strong hand like Jacks, I want to build a pot and potentially isolate Claude or even win the blinds and his raise immediately. A 3-bet is the standard and most effective play here to extract value and prevent the Big Blind (Minni) from seeing a cheap flop. I'll raise to 1000.

Claude opened to 300 and Flash (short stack ~27 BB) 3‑bet to 1000. I have K♦T♣ in the big blind. Calling 900 (1000 total) gives ~39% pot odds to see a flop (pot 1400 -> 2300 after my call) and is the most +EV line here: KTo has reasonable equity vs a polarized short‑stack 3‑bet and plays well heads‑up if Claude folds. 4‑betting with KTo is too thin and folding surrenders good pot odds and a chance to exploit the short stack post‑flop. Plan: call to 1000, evaluate flop texture and opponent actions — proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression, look to realize equity or target the short stack when in position.

Re-raising with A9 suited from the button to 2,100. This applies pressure against Flash's short stack while maintaining aggression with a premium hand in position. Flash's re-raise from the small blind could be light, making this an ideal spot to apply pressure. Even if called, I retain positional advantage with a strong hand that plays well post-flop. The sizing is appropriately aggressive without overcommitting given the stack sizes and pot dynamics.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket Jacks (Jc Jd). The action pre-flop has been intense: Claude opened to 300, I 3-bet to 1000, Minni cold-called the 1000 from the Big Blind, and now Claude has 4-bet to 2100. My stack is relatively short at 2725 total. Calling the 1100 more would leave me with only 625 chips in a pot that will be over 5200. With pocket Jacks in a 3-way 4-bet pot, my equity is often high enough against their ranges, especially since Claude's 4-bet size is quite small (a 'min-click' back). However, given my stack size, I have no maneuverability post-flop. The correct move with JJ and a short stack in this configuration is to commit the rest of my chips now to maximize my equity and realize it fully by seeing all five cards. I am effectively pot-committed.

I planned to call the 3-bet earlier to see a flop, but the action just escalated: Claude 4-bet to 2,100 and Flash shoved/5-bet to 3,200. Pot is 6,300 and it costs me 2,200 more to call (I would have to commit 3,200 total), so I need ~25.9% equity to break even. K♣T♦ multiway against a 4-bet/5-bet sequence is frequently dominated (Claude's 4-bet looks value-heavy; Flash's 5-bet polarised/committed), and my equity vs two strong ranges is well below the required threshold. Raising would only bloat the pot with two strong opponents and commit a lot more. Fold and preserve a healthy stack — look for a better, clearer spot (heads-up or versus weaker sizing) to invest chips.

Calling Flash's all-in re-raise with A9 suited. The pot odds of nearly 10:1 are too good to pass up, and A9 suited plays well heads-up against Flash's short-stack range. Whether Flash has a premium pair, broadway cards, or a light desperation shove, I have sufficient equity to justify the call. The risk-reward is favorable given the pot size relative to my stack size.

I am in a three-way pot that was heavily actioned pre-flop, and I have committed nearly all of my stack already. My remaining stack is 525 chips, while the pot is 7400. This is an extremely low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of approximately 0.07. With pocket Jacks on a Queen-high board, I still have a very strong hand relative to many parts of Claude's range, such as AK or smaller pairs. Regardless of whether I am ahead or behind, the mathematical reality is that I am pot-committed; I cannot fold for 525 chips into a 7400 pot. Betting my remaining chips is the only logical move to realize my equity and put the pressure on Claude, though he is almost certainly calling with his entire range given the pot odds.

On the flop with A♠9♠ against a 6d-Ts-Qh board. I'm facing a 525 bet into a 7925 pot, giving me approximately 15:1 pot odds to call.

My hand has solid equity with multiple improvement avenues:
- Flush draw (9 spades remaining)
- Pair outs (3 nines, 3 additional aces = 6 outs)
- Gutshot straight draw (4 kings for the wheel)
- Overcard aces

With conservative equity calculations against Flash's likely all-in range (broadway cards, small pairs, or semi-bluffs given the 3200 all-in pre-flop), I'm likely holding 25-35% equity, which is well above the 6.7% break-even threshold required by the 15:1 pot odds.

The call is mathematically sound - excellent pot odds combined with a hand that has multiple ways to win. This is a straightforward profitable call that maintains my aggressive position in the hand.

I am on the button with 9c 6h. This is a very weak hand in a three-handed game. While I have the positional advantage, calling or raising with 96o into two aggressive opponents like Minni and Claude (who have been involved in several large pots) is generally not profitable. Folding here is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up from the small blind with Jc2s — a very weak hand out of position. Pot is 150 and it only costs me an extra 50 to see a flop (total committed 100), giving ~4:1 pot odds to try and hit. Raising to 200 would invite a sizable 3-bet from Claude (he shows frequent big 3-bets) and force awkward out-of-position decisions with junk. Folding surrenders only 50 but is too tight given the cheap price to see a flop. Plan: defend cheaply — call the 50 (total 100). If Claude 3-bets substantially preflop I will fold; if we see the flop we’ll proceed cautiously and avoid bloating the pot without improvement.

Checking the big blind with 5d8s (a weak, gapped hand) to see a flop cheaply. I'm out of position against Minni and can control the pot size. If I get a favorable flop, I can continue; if not, I can fold to any aggression and save chips. Checking costs nothing and gives me maximum flexibility with a marginal holding.

I defended preflop cheaply with Jc2s as planned, but on the A‑5‑3 rainbow flop I have no pair and only a backdoor club. I’m out of position versus Claude (who shows frequent aggression), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand or lead into a likely caller/raiser. Check to control the pot, gather information, and either take a free turn or fold to a credible bet. If Claude fires a very small probe (tiny cbet) I may call to see a cheap turn, but I will fold to larger continuation bets — saving chips for better spots.

I'm holding 5d8s and have flopped a pair of fives on a board of 5c-3h-Ah. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness. My hand is marginal—a pair of fives with an eight kicker is vulnerable on this Ace-heavy board. Key factors support checking: (1) Minni's check suggests a missed hand or weak holding, (2) I'm out of position and checking keeps the pot small and controlled, (3) seeing another card cheaply is valuable with this marginal strength, (4) if Minni bets, I'll have good information to decide whether to fold or call, and (5) if the turn improves or stays blank, I can reassess. This is conservative but correct poker with a weak-to-marginal hand in a 3-way pot where position matters.

I have Jc2s on A-5-3-9 rainbow with no pair and effectively no draw. Pot is only 200 and I’m out of position vs Claude, who can be aggressive; there’s no point bloating the pot with pure air. My river outs are slim (three Jacks + three Deuces ≈ 6 outs, ~13%), so bluffing the turn has poor risk/reward and is likely to be raised off. I’ll check to control the pot and see a free river, then re-evaluate: fold to a sizable bet on the turn or river without improvement, call small probes if they’re cheap, and look for a river to either bluff or get thin value. Action: check.

Checking the turn with a marginal pair of fives. Minni's back-to-back checks suggest weakness. The Ace-heavy board makes my hand vulnerable, so pot control is critical. Checking allows me to see the river for free and maintain flexibility. If Minni bets the river, I'll have better information to make a call/fold decision. This conservative line preserves my marginal holding without overcommitting chips.

River pairs my 2s — I now have a small pair (twos) with a Jack kicker on a 5♣-3♥-A♥-9♦-2♦ board. Pot is tiny (200) and Claude has been passive the entire hand (checked flop, turn and river). There is very little thin‑value to gain by betting: most hands that would call a river bet (A, 9, two pair, better) beat me, and bluffs I might fold out are unlikely given his passive line. Betting risks getting raised off a better hand and is negative EV. With a deep stack relative to the pot I should conserve chips and take the showdown for the small pot. Action: check to show down.

Minni has checked all four streets (preflop, flop, turn, river), indicating weakness or a missed draw. I hold a pair of fives with an eight kicker—marginal but likely ahead of most unimproved hands. The pot is only 100 chips with no bets placed. Betting accomplishes nothing: if Minni folds, I win 100 chips; if she calls, I'm likely beaten. Checking allows me to reach showdown safely with my marginal hand, which is the most profitable line. This maximizes the equity of my holding without overextending in a small pot against a weak-play opponent.

Js2h is a weak, non‑connective offsuit hand, but I’m on the button facing only the blinds. Open‑raising here is standard exploitative play: pressure the blinds, leverage position postflop, and avoid playing a bloated multiway pot out of position. Table shows many players fold or call wide from the blinds, so a 3xBB-ish open is appropriate. I’ll raise to 300 to steal the blinds and take initiative. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (relative to stacks, e.g. >700–800), consider calling a small 3‑bet to use position, and proceed cautiously postflop — c‑bet on dry boards where a jack or overcard helps the story, otherwise check and abandon if I miss.

6d-Jc is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. I'm in the small blind with 250 to call into a 450 pot after Minni's raise from the button. While pot odds are reasonable at 1.8:1, hand strength is the limiting factor here. Jack-six offsuit is below the threshold for playing out of position against a raise, especially when I'll have to act first post-flop with no positional advantage. My stack is healthy at 12000, so there's no reason to play weak hands. Folding is the mathematically sound play in this spot.

In Hand #117, I am in the big blind with 8s 5h. Minni, who is on the button and has the largest stack, has raised to 300. This is a 3x open. Looking at my cards, 85o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player who has shown aggression throughout the session. Given that I have a stack of 8350 and need to conserve chips to maximize my standing over the remaining 33 hands, calling here is not profitable. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play with this hand against a button open.

Playing 2-K offsuit from the button. While this is a weak hand in isolation, several factors make calling reasonable here: (1) I have positional advantage as the button and will act last post-flop, (2) The cost to call is minimal (100 chips) compared to pot odds and my stack size (12,000), (3) Looking at recent hands, the table is fairly aggressive, and weak hands can win with position and fold equity, (4) With Minni's large stack and the aggressive tendencies shown, I can make profitable decisions post-flop with my position edge. The small blind cost justifies seeing the flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh 5h. Claude, the button, has limp-called for 100. This is a speculative hand but has decent playability being suited. To call costs only 50 chips into a pot of 250, giving me 5-to-1 odds to see a flop. Raising from the SB into two opponents (one who limped and one in the BB) with Q5 suited is a bit too loose, especially given the stack sizes and Minni's aggressive tendencies in previous hands. Calling the 50 is the most cost-effective way to realize my equity and see if I flop a strong draw or top pair.

Three-way limp pot; I'm BB with pocket deuces (2♣2♦). Pot is 300 and both opponents are deep (Claude ~11.9k, Flash ~8.25k). This hand is a classic set-mining candidate — its primary value comes from flopping a set (~11.8% on the flop) and extracting big implied odds. Raising to 200 (only 100 more) won’t accomplish much here: it’s unlikely to fold out many limpers, and I’d be bloating a pot out of position with a very vulnerable pair. I’ll check to see a cheap flop and preserve stack flexibility. Plan: if I hit a set, bet/raise for value and commit; if I miss, evaluate the flop texture and opponent action — on dry, low boards I may try a small continuation bet or check-call a small bet, but against heavy action or high-card boards I’ll concede and fold to significant pressure. Checking maximizes EV by realizing equity cheaply and keeping my options open.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-high and no draws on an Ace-high board. In a three-way limped pot, the most prudent action is to check and see how the other players respond. Leading out with a bet here would be a weak bluff into two opponents, one of whom (Minni) has shown significant aggression in previous hands.

Three-way limp pot and I’m the big blind with pocket deuces — this flop (A♥ 8♦ 4♦) is exactly the type of texture where my hand loses a lot of equity. I’m an underpair to the Ace and there’s a two-diamond flush possibility; both opponents checked so I’m not getting information that they’re weak enough to fold to a small stab. Betting into two players here bloats the pot with a hand that has very little showdown value unless it improves to a set. Pot-control is the priority: check and see a turn cheaply, only continue if I pick up a deuce (set) or a very safe blank and both opponents give clear weakness. If either opponent C-bets, I’ll fold to significant pressure (call only very small sizing vs obvious drawing/passive lines), and if checked through I’ll reevaluate turn for possible delayed bluff or check-fold line. Overall: avoid building a big pot with an underpair in a multiway spot and preserve stack for true value spots.

With K2 offsuit on a board of 4d 8d Ah, I have no made hand and no real draw. Both Flash and Minni checked to me, indicating weakness. While I have positional advantage on the button, betting here with zero equity is not profitable—I'd be making a position play on a hand that has virtually no showdown value and risks getting called by hands that have pair equity. The optimal play is to take a free card and see the turn. If the board improves (hit a King or a favorable card), I'll be in position to make a profitable decision post-turn. This conservative approach preserves chips while maintaining my positional advantage for later streets.

The turn card is a King, adding another high card to the board. I still have no pair and no draw. In a three-way pot with only 300 chips, there is no reason to bluff here when I have zero equity. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

Three-way checked pot; I have pocket deuces (2♦2♣) on A♥ 8♦ 4♦ K♠. I missed a set and with one card to come I cannot make a diamond flush anymore, so this hand has very little equity versus possible A/K highs or any pair above twos. Both opponents have shown passivity through the flop and turn. Betting into two checked players will rarely fold out better overcards and only bloats the pot with a marginal hand — with stacks deep I should avoid creating a big pot without real equity. Pot-control is the priority: check the turn, see the river for a free card, and only continue vs very small bets. If checked to me on a harmless river I can consider a small delayed bluff; otherwise I'll check/call very small or fold to meaningful pressure.

Checking on the turn with K2o on 4d 8d Ah Ks. While I improved to a pair of Kings, the Ace on the flop makes this a marginal holding in a 3-way pot. Both opponents checked, but in multiway pots, checked Aces are common. Betting here doesn't make sense: the pot is small (300), and I'd be risking chips with a weak hand that's likely behind to an Ace. Checking takes a free river card, preserves chips, and keeps flexibility. If the river improves my situation or provides information through opponent action, I can react accordingly.

In a three-way limped pot where every player has checked the flop and the turn, the board is very heavy with an Ace and a King. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but it's also not a good candidate for a bluff as the pot is very small (300 chips) and someone could easily be checking a small pair or a weak Ace/King.
