I will check and hope to see a free showdown, folding to any bet.

I have pocket deuces in a three-way limp pot and missed my set. The final board A♥ 8♦ 4♦ K♠ 3♠ has no improvement for me and my hand has almost no value versus any Ace/King or pair — and there were checks on every street from both opponents. Betting as a bluff into two players with essentially zero fold equity is a negative-EV play; a small blocker bet might fold pure air sometimes, but against two passive players it will be called too frequently by Ax, Kx, or single-pair hands. Pot is only 300 and stacks are deep, so pot-control and preserving chips for real value spots is optimal. I’ll check to showdown and only pay a small bet if someone bluffs; otherwise I reveal and take the small pots I can when opponents have nothing.

With K2o making a pair of Kings on the river, but facing an Ace-heavy board and two opponents who both checked, betting is not profitable. The pot is only 300 chips, and betting risks chips on a marginal hand that's likely behind to any Ace. Both opponents' checking indicates weakness, so taking the showdown with my pair is the optimal line. Checking preserves chips while maintaining a reasonable chance to win the pot, and it protects against getting caught bluffing into a stronger hand.

I am on the button with 7d 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. With both the small and big blinds left to act and no prior action in the pot, this hand does not meet the requirements for an open or a steal attempt. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Tc2s is a very weak, easily dominated hand and is awful to play out of position. Facing only the big blind (Claude) I could call 50 or min-raise to 200, but both lead to difficult postflop spots against a player who has shown frequent aggression. With a deep stack and many hands remaining, disciplined fold is the +EV choice — preserve chips for better spots rather than playing T2o out of position.

On the button with 4♦5♣: a weak, speculative hand but massive positional advantage. Pot is only the blinds (150) and standard table open size has been ~300, so a 3x BB open is a credible steal and keeps my ranges balanced. I raise to 300 to apply pressure and either take the blinds or play a multiway pot in position; if Claude (SB) 3-bets I can fold — this hand is not worth getting tangled in a 3-bet pot out of position. If called, I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry ones.

I'm facing a raise from Minni (Button) with J6o from the small blind. While Minni is an aggressive player who raises frequently, J6o is simply too weak to play from out of position. Even with pot odds of roughly 1.8:1 (450 to call 250), this hand has poor equity against Minni's range and plays terribly postflop out of position. With 120 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium spots rather than play a speculative weak hand. Folding is the best option here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, Ten-Trey is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. I choose to fold and conserve my stack.

Raising to 300 total (2x raise over the 100 call) from the button with QsTs. This is a standard, non-aggressive raise that takes advantage of my position and decent hand strength. The raise is likely to win the blinds outright or build a pot where I have positional advantage post-flop. My stack allows for this play, and the hand has good potential against a wide range of opponent holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. While K5o is a marginally playable hand in some heads-up scenarios, playing it out of position against a Button raise in a 3-way game is generally a losing proposition, especially since Minni is still to act in the Big Blind and has shown a tendency to be active. I will fold to avoid a difficult post-flop situation.

Fold. 8♣3♠ is a very weak, heavily dominated hand versus a BTN open. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (calling would make the pot 650), so I need ~30.8% equity to break even preflop. Versus a standard button opening range my realized equity with 8-3o will be well below that and I’ll be out of position postflop. A 3-bet bluff is possible occasionally, but this hand has poor blockers and poor postflop playability, so I’d rather preserve my ~195BB stack for better spots.

I have a playable suited hand (Q8s) on the button. In a three-handed game, this is well within my opening range. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with the advantage of position.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with K♦3♦. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to continue. K3s is a weak, often-dominated holding versus a button’s opening range and plays poorly out of position — and the big blind still has to act, risking a multiway pot. 3-betting as a bluff here is thin with deep stacks and would put me in difficult postflop spots; calling leaves me OOP with a marginal hand and low realization of equity. Preserve the deep stack and fold this marginal spot.

I have pocket kings (KK), one of the two best starting hands in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and the small blind (Minni) folded, leaving this as heads-up play. With this premium pair in position against Flash's button raise (which has a wide range), I should 3-bet aggressively to build the pot and extract value. A raise to 800 is appropriate sizing - it's significant enough to apply real pressure while maintaining good pot odds if Flash calls. This also aligns with my aggressive playing pattern shown in earlier premium hands. I'm well-stacked at 12,250 chips and can afford to play for value here.

I opened to 300 from the button with Qc8c, a decent suited hand for 3-handed play. Claude 3-bet from the big blind to 800. While my hand has some playability and I have position, I'm facing a significant raise from a solid opponent (Claude). My hand is on the weaker side of a calling range against a 3-bet, and with a stack of 7800 (78 big blinds), I don't want to get involved in a bloated pot with a mediocre hand out of position relative to the 3-bet's strength. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with T6d (suited). Facing a standard min/open to 2x BB (To Call 100). With ~195bb stacks and only two opponents, this hand is marginal but playable in position — suited tens have decent implied odds and can make top-pair, straight and flush combos. The price is cheap: call 100 to potentially win ~250 (break-even ~40%), and being last to act postflop is a big advantage. Not strong enough to 3-bet; plan to call, see the flop, and proceed aggressively only if I hit (top pair, strong draw) or fold to significant resistance. If I face a large 3-bet preflop I will let it go. Execute a flat call to realize position and implied odds.

Holding 6h2d (Six-Deuce offsuit) in the small blind - this is the worst hand in poker. Despite getting 5:1 pot odds to call the 50 additional chips, this hand has almost no value. It plays terribly in position against two opponents, has minimal equity, and will struggle to win post-flop. Even though I'm in a strong position chip-wise (12,650), there's no reason to get involved with this holding. A standard fold from the SB with this garbage hand, consistent with tight-aggressive strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5c. Minni, on the button, has completed the blind (limped). Claude has folded from the Small Blind. The pot is small (250) and it's a 'check or raise' situation for me. 95o is a very weak hand, but since Minni just limped, there is no need to fold. Checking allows me to see a free flop and potentially realize some equity or find a bluffing opportunity later. There is no reason to bloat the pot with such a weak hand out of position.

I have bottom two pair (Nines and Fives) on a Queen-high board. In a limped pot, this is a very strong hand. I will check first to see if Minni (the button) bets, which would allow me to check-raise for value and protect against potential draws like J-T or 8-7. If she checks behind, I will definitely lead on the turn to extract value from one-pair hands or draws.

Flop checked to me in position on a dry Q-9-5 board with two diamonds in my hand (Td6d). I have no pair but I do have a two-card backdoor diamond draw and decent positional advantage. Flash’s check is non-committal — his range is wide and contains many missed overcards and small pairs that will fold to pressure. The pot is tiny (250), so this is an easy spot to take with a c-bet to win the pot now or realize equity cheaply. I’ll make a polarizing, medium-sized c-bet (~1/2–2/3 pot) to get folding equity while not committing too many chips if I’m raised; plan is to fold to a sizeable raise, and to continue only if I pick up equity (a diamond or a T) or get a favorable turn action. Bet 150 into 250 to take it down or see a cheap turn.

In this limped pot, I have flopped bottom two pair (Nines and Fives). This is a very strong hand given the pre-flop action. Minni has bet 150 into a pot of 250. To extract value from top-pair hands (Qx), middle-pair hands (9x), or straight draws (JT, T8, 87), I will check-raise. Raising now allows me to build the pot and protects my hand against various draws that could improve on the turn. A raise to 500 total is a standard sizing to put pressure on her range and get value.

I c‑bet 150 as a standard positional stab, but Flash’s check‑raise to 500 is a clear strength signal. The pot is ~900 and I must call 350, so I need roughly 350/(900+350) ≈ 28% equity to break even. T♦6♦ has only weak backdoor equity (runner‑runner diamond/straight) and just a few live outs to improve to a competitive hand; versus a check‑raise range that will include many Qx, sets (99/55) and strong made hands my real equity is well below 28%. Raising as a bluff here is too risky — Flash’s sizing and previous line indicate limited fold equity. With deep stacks I can preserve chips for better, higher EV spots, so folding is the correct play.

Raising to 350 (3.5x BB) from the button with 9-J offsuit is standard aggressive play. This hand has decent equity against a wide range of opponent holdings. Position advantage combined with stack sizes and opponent tendencies makes this the optimal play. The raise puts immediate pressure on the blinds, is large enough to accomplish its goal of gaining information and winning the pot, but small enough to not overcommit with a marginal hand if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 4s, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button to 350. There is no reason to call or raise with one of the worst starting hands in poker while out of position. I will fold.

Claude opened to 350 from the button and Flash folded. I’m in the big blind with 8♠8♣ — a medium pocket pair. Out of position, I prefer to take the initiative preflop rather than just set-mine; a 3-bet accomplishes three things: gets fold equity vs broadways, builds value when I’m ahead of much of his calling range, and defines the hand. The min-raise to 600 is too small to achieve that, so I’ll raise to a meaningful size but leave room to fold to a big 4-bet. I’ll 3-bet to 1200 total: big enough to apply pressure and extract value, but small relative to stacks so I’m not overcommitting. If Claude folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (look for sets, c-bet selectively and control pot), and I will fold to a large 4-bet (but re-evaluate small 4-bet sizing).

Folding 9-J offsuit to Minni's 3-bet. While the hand has some value, it lacks sufficient equity (~30-35%) to justify continuing. Against her 3-bet range (which includes premiums and strong broadways), I'm at or slightly below the break-even point on pot odds. Playing 9-J out of position postflop against an aggressive, deep-stacked opponent with marginal equity is unprofitable long-term.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game, Q3o is a very weak hand to open with, even from the button. My stack is around 80 BBs, and there is no need to play such a marginal hand into active opponents like Minni and Claude, who have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and 3-bet. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

Q2o from the small blind is a poor, easily-dominated hand and I’ll be playing out of position. It’s only 50 more to call into a 150 pot, so the price to see a flop is excellent. A min-raise to 200 would be a pure steal attempt against Claude, who has shown a willingness to defend and play postflop — I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with junk. I’ll complete the call (100 total committed) and evaluate the flop; continue only if I make a meaningful improvement or get a cheap showdown, otherwise fold to significant aggression.

With 3c-Js (a weak hand) in the big blind against Minni's small blind call, checking is the optimal play. Minni showed weakness by just calling rather than raising, so there's no need to invest additional chips with a marginal hand. I'm already committed to the small blind bet, so I can see the flop cheaply and reassess based on what hits. If the flop misses me and Minni bets, I can fold easily. If I hit a Jack or get a favorable board, I can proceed with the hand.

I improved to a meaningful hand — a pair of deuces (Qh2d on 6h-2s-3h). Pot is only 200 and I’m OOP vs Claude, who checked preflop and has a wide checking/defending range. A small value/protection bet (~1/2 pot = 100) is the best play: it charges worse hands (A/x, K/x, small pairs), deters free cards to backdoor hearts, and keeps the pot controllable out of position. If Claude check‑raises heavily I’ll fold — my pair is vulnerable. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

Calling with bottom pair and a Jack kicker against Minni's flop bet. The pot odds are favorable (3.5:1), and Minni's 100-chip raise into a 150-chip pot doesn't necessarily indicate a strong hand. Bottom pair has showdown value and my Jack kicker provides additional equity. The small investment relative to my 12,200 stack makes calling the natural choice to proceed and gather more information on the turn.

Turn paired the board (6h 2s 3h -> 6c). My Q2o improved to a single pair of deuces on the flop, but the turn pairing of sixes makes that holding much more vulnerable — any 6 (trip sixes), pocket 3s/22 (full houses/trips), or even A2/2x with a better kicker now crushes or dominates me. Claude called my flop stab, which is a wide range that can include these hands as well as simple A/K holdings and backdoor heart draws. Betting here bloats the pot out of position and exposes me to a check‑raise that I’d have to fold to; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize equity. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the river. If Claude leads a small bet I’ll call to see a cheap river; fold to a large bet/raise indicating strong made hands.

I have two pair (sixes and threes with Jack kicker) which is a moderately strong hand with good showdown value. Minni's turn check after her flop raise suggests weakness. With a medium-strength hand and 25 cards to come, checking is prudent to reach showdown safely while preserving my stack. I want to see the river cheaply and avoid getting trapped by a possible set of sixes. If the river brings action, I can reassess with complete board information.

I have Q2o and only a pair of deuces on a 6h-2s-3h-6c-8c board. I led the flop with a small bet and Claude called, then both of us checked the turn when the board paired. The turn pairing makes my hand very vulnerable: any 6 (A6/K6/etc.), any pocket 6/3/2, or combos like A2 will beat me. The river 8c changes almost nothing — it doesn’t complete a heart flush and mainly helps unlikely club draws. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position and will mainly get called by hands that already beat me (or fold out the few worse hands), so a river bluff or thin-value bet is low EV. Best play is pot control and see showdown: check and show my pair of deuces.

River check with two pair (6s and 3s) when opponent has shown clear weakness through their check-raise flop followed by turn/river checks. My hand has solid showdown value, and Minni's passive line indicates she cannot call a bet with worse holdings. Checking guarantees I win the pot while protecting against a potential check-raise. With 25 hands remaining in the session, there's no need to risk additional chips on marginal value in this spot.

I'm on the button with 3♠8♠ — a weak but suited hand with some backdoor potential. Versus only the blinds this is a good spot to open-raise and use position/fold equity rather than limp. I’ll make a standard open to 300 to apply pressure and narrow opponents' ranges; that sizing is big enough to force some folds but small enough to keep pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (my hand plays poorly vs aggression), but if called I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when I miss. With stacks deep I can exert pressure postflop, so an open-raise here maximizes expected value compared to just calling 100.

Folding K-7o from small blind. While pot odds are 1.8:1, the hand is too marginal to play out of position against a button raise. K-7 will struggle post-flop when playing out of position. Better to preserve chips for premium hands in better spots.

I am in the big blind with 3h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. Although I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, 3-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and is extremely difficult to play out of position against a player who has shown aggression throughout the game. With my current stack and the position, it's a clear fold to wait for better opportunities.

Raising with Js8s on the button from 100 to call. This is a standard button steal with a decent hand that has good equity if called. The position advantage combined with the decent hand makes this a +EV play. Standard raise of 3x BB to 300 total chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (Th 4s). Claude, the button, has raised to 300. Ten-Four is a very weak hand, and I am out of position against a player with a significant stack. Calling or raising with such a weak hand would be unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button; I’m in the BB with Qh5d. Pot is 450 and it’s 200 to me (I’ve already posted 100), so calling requires a total of 300. Q5o is marginal but defendable heads-up: calling 200 into a 450 pot gives ~31% break-even equity, and versus a BTN opening range I have reasonable equity and good implied odds with deep stacks. Claude has been an active opener, so I’ll defend rather than fold or bluff-3bet with a weak kicker. Plan: call to see a flop; proceed only with real connectivity (Q/5 or strong draws) and otherwise play cautiously OOP (check/fold to heavy aggression). If Claude 3-bets large preflop I’ll fold this hand. Action: call to 300 total.

Flop Jc Td 9c is very connected and heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range. With Qh5d I have an open‑ended straight draw (K or 8 — 8 outs, ~31–32% to the river) and three queens that can make a vulnerable top pair. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than firing or bluffing here. Plan: check and call standard continuation bets (small–medium, ~<=1/2 pot) to see the turn; fold to large overbets or a committed check‑raise unless the price is correct. If Claude checks back I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate — bet/extract when I hit the straight or a clean top pair, but otherwise play cautiously OOP. Action: check to keep pot manageable and realize my draw.

Betting with top pair (jacks with 8 kicker) on a coordinated board. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a draw. A bet of 400 (roughly 60% pot) is appropriate to: 1) Build pot when ahead, 2) Get value from drawing hands and weaker pairs, 3) Apply pressure and get information. The board has straight/flush draw possibilities, so I want to charge draws and get maximum value from my strong current hand.

Claude (preflop raiser) c-bets 400 into a 1050 pot on Jc Td 9c after I checked. With Qh5d I have an 8‑out straight draw (Ks or 8s) — roughly ~31% to hit by the river. Calling 400 to win a 1450 pot gives ~27.6% break-even odds, so this is a +EV call purely on equity. He’s betting small, his range is wide as the opener, and stacks are deep so I want to realize my draw equity rather than fold or overcommit with a semi-bluff. Plan: call to see the turn; if I miss and face large aggression I’ll fold, but if I hit the straight or a clean Q I’ll start extracting value or raising accordingly.

I’m out of position with Q♠5♦ on J♣ T♦ 9♣ 7♠. After calling Claude’s flop c‑bet I’ve got 8 clean outs to a straight (Ks or 8s) plus 3 queens to pair — 11 total outs ≈ 11/46 ≈ 23.9% to improve on the river (≈17.4% to hit the straight alone). Pot is 1,450 and there’s no bet to call right now, so betting as a bluff would need a large fold frequency to be +EV (a half‑pot shove would require >30% folds), and Claude has been an active/aggressive opener who’s unlikely to fold enough. I want to control the pot size and realize my draw equity cheaply rather than bloating the pot OOP. Plan: check and see the river for free; if Claude bets the river I’ll evaluate based on sizing and exact odds (call only when price justifies my ~17% straight or ~24% combined improvement chance), fold to large overbets; if checked through and I hit a clean straight or pair I’ll extract value.

Betting top pair (jacks with 8 kicker) on the turn. Minni checked after calling my flop bet, indicating weakness or a draw. The 7s doesn't significantly help most hands. A value bet of 700 (approximately 50% of the pot) is appropriate to: 1) Extract value from weaker pairs and draws, 2) Get information about Minni's hand strength, 3) Apply pressure and potentially win the pot. My position as the button gives me an advantage if Minni folds or checks the river. If called, I can re-assess on the river card.

Pot is 2,150 and it’s 700 to call, so I need ~24.6% equity to break even on a call (700 / (2,150+700) ≈ 24.6%). With Q♣5♦ I have 4 kings + 4 eights = 8 straight outs and 3 queens = 11 total outs → 11/46 ≈ 23.9% to improve on the river. That’s slightly below the price, and many of those outs are not clean (KQ would already have a straight, some Q/K rivers still lose). Claude’s line (open pre, c‑bet flop, small turn bet) is consistent with a lot of hands that connect strongly with J‑T‑9‑7 or that won’t fold to a raise. Raising as a bluff OOP into deep stacks is too thin. Given the marginal pot odds, blocked/dirty outs and opponent tendencies, folding is the +EV play here — preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While I have the opportunity to open the pot, this hand is significantly below the standard range for a button open, especially with active and aggressive players in the blinds who have shown a willingness to 3-bet or call and play post-flop. Given my current stack of 79 BBs, there is no strategic reason to attempt a low-frequency steal with such a weak hand.

Heads-up in the small blind with 4♠5♦ (weak, unsuited connector). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call (total commit 100), giving ~4:1 pot odds (break-even equity ≈20%). Versus a typical big-blind defending range I have enough speculative equity to see a flop cheaply, and with deep stacks I can realize draws or hit pairs. A min-raise to 200 would overcommit OOP with a very weak hand and folding surrenders too much given the price. Plan: call to see the flop; continue only if I pair, make a strong draw, or get a favourable, low-commitment texture — otherwise fold to significant aggression OOP.

Raising to 350 with AQo from the big blind. Minni's small blind call suggests weakness, and AQ is a premium hand that benefits from building the pot and applying pressure. Standard 3.5x raise for value and to isolate.

I limp-called preflop intending to see a cheap flop with 4♠5♦, but Claude’s raise to 350 changes the economics. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call, so I’d need ~36% equity (250/700) to break even. 4‑5 offsuit OOP has far less equity vs a normal BB raising range (pairs, broadways, suited Aces) and is frequently dominated. Raising is out of the question with this weak hand. With position disadvantage and the increased price, folding preserves stack for better spots — I fold.

I'm on the button with pocket deuces in a three-handed pot. This is a clear open-raise: we have position, deep stacks (188bb effective vs BB 78bb / SB 132bb), and 22 plays well as a set-mining hand while also having fold equity. A standard 3xBB open keeps the price reasonable to see a flop if called, looks natural at this table (we and others frequently open to 300), and often isolates to one opponent. Plan: raise to 300 to take the initiative; if we face a large 3-bet I'll fold (22 is too weak as a bluff-catcher), but if called I'll proceed postflop with position, looking to extract when we hit a set and control the pot otherwise.

Td7s is too weak to call from the small blind out of position against Minni's button raise. While I have pot odds of 1.8:1, this hand lacks both strength and playability post-flop from out of position. Position is critical in poker, and Td7s doesn't justify getting involved. Folding preserves my healthy 13,250 stack for better opportunities with premium holdings.

I am in the big blind with 9c8c, a very strong suited connector that plays well in 3-way or heads-up pots. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Claude has already folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. The pot odds (200 to win 450) and the playability of my hand make this a standard call to see a flop. Raising (3-betting) is also an option, but calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot controlled out of position against a player with a significant stack advantage.

I have an open-ended straight draw (9, 8 on a T, 7, 3 board). The pot is 650 and I am in the Big Blind. Minni (Button) raised pre-flop. I should check to the aggressor to see how they react to this board. If Minni bets, I have a strong enough draw to call or potentially check-raise depending on the size, but checking first is the standard play with a draw out of position.

I opened 22 preflop as a set-mining/initiative play. The flop (Th 3s 7h) missed me but is relatively dry with only two hearts as backdoor danger. Flash checked; in position I want to leverage initiative with a small C-bet to fold many overcards and pick up the pot, while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet ~1/3–35% pot (250) — large enough to deny equity and get value from worse high-card hands, but small enough to fold to a significant check-raise since I have very little showdown value without a set. If called I’ll proceed cautiously and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a board of Th 3s 7h with my 9c8c. The pot is currently 900 and it costs me 250 to call, giving me pot odds of 3.6:1. With an open-ended draw, I have 8 outs to make a straight, which gives me roughly a 32% chance to hit by the river. Given the price and the playability of my hand, a call is standard here to see a turn card. I will check-call to realize my equity and potentially hit my straight on the turn.

On the turn, the board is Th 3s 7h 2h. A third heart has arrived, completing potential flushes. I still hold an open-ended straight draw with 9c8c. As the player out of position, I will check to the aggressor (Minni) to see how she reacts to the flush-completing card. This allows me to control the pot and decide whether to call a bet based on the price offered, while also protecting my checking range.

Turn pairs my pocket deuces — I have trips (2c2d + 2h). Flash called my small flop c-bet, so his range is wide: Tx, weak pairs, some 7x/3x, and draws. The turn also brings a third heart, so there are now possible made flushes (two hearts in hand) and I need to charge/price those plus any straight/heart draws. I should extract value and protect vs draws while I have position. Betting ~2/3–3/4 pot is the right balance: big enough to charge Tx/7x/weak pairs and deny equity to pure draws, but not so big I fold out most callers. Pot ≈1,150, so I’ll bet 800. If he check-raises large I’ll re-evaluate (a jam likely represents a flush and deserves caution; a smaller raise I’ll treat as value or a bluff and can call or shove depending on sizing).

I am currently holding an open-ended straight draw with 9c8c on a board of Th 3s 7h 2h. The turn brought the 2h, completing a potential flush. Minni has bet 800 into a 1150 pot, which is a strong sizing (approx. 70% pot). My pot odds are roughly 3.4:1, requiring about 23% equity to call. With only 8 outs to a straight (some of which are the Jh and 6h, which complete flushes), my raw equity to hit on the river is only about 17.4%. Given the large bet size and the fact that even if I hit, I may lose to a flush, the price is not right to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

7-2 is the worst starting hand in poker and has minimal equity. Even on the button, position advantage cannot overcome the fundamental weakness of this hand. With a healthy stack and only 20 hands remaining, folding and waiting for premium hands is the correct long-term strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d4d. Claude has folded on the Button. The pot is currently 150 (100 from Minni's BB and 50 from my SB). It costs me only 50 more to see a flop. While 64s isn't a premium hand, getting 3-to-1 odds to call in a head-up situation against Minni is generally acceptable for a suited connector/gapper in a cash game context, especially since I'm trying to maintain my stack. I will complete the bet and see a flop.

Heads-up vs Flash (SB) who limped. I have A♦T♦ in the big blind — a strong, playable hand heads-up that I don’t want to play passively out of position on the flop (Flash is also the button/SB and will have positional advantage postflop if I check). Flash has shown a tendency to limp/call, so I should raise to isolate and take the initiative, denying favorable pot odds to speculative hands and getting value from worse aces/broadways. I’ll raise to a standard isolation size: 400 total (puts pressure on the limp, often folds out weak pairs/connectors, and keeps the pot manageable). Plan: if called, continuation-bet favorable A/T/T-high textures; if I face a large 3-bet, re-evaluate (likely fold to a very large 3-bet given stack dynamics), but proceed aggressively with position and initiative when appropriate.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d4d. I initially limped, and Minni raised to 400 from the Big Blind. It costs me another 300 to call into a 500 pot, giving me odds of about 2.6 to 1. While 64s has some playability, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader, Minni, who has been playing aggressively. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities, as calling 4x pre-flop out of position with a weak gapper is generally a losing play in the long run.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a weak hand and generally not worth playing, especially since both the small blind and big blind have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to be aggressive pre-flop. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Heads-up from the small blind with Td2c — a marginal hand but the price to see a flop is only 50 chips into a 150 pot, so the pot odds justify a call. Raising to 200 would be a thin bluff out-of-position against Claude, who has shown willingness to defend and raise; I don't want to inflate the pot with a weak holding. I’ll call the 50 more (100 total committed) to see a flop cheaply, then fold to heavy aggression on a dry miss or continue only with a pair, good kicker, or strong draw.

With 54o (worst starting hand), I should minimize losses and see the flop cheaply. Minni's weak call shows she doesn't have a strong hand. My position post-flop (acting last) gives me control and flexibility. Checking allows me to realize equity against her weak range and potentially win the pot if she checks through or when I can leverage position with a well-timed bet. This is pure damage control with a trash hand.

Flop: Jd 3d 7c with Td2c in the small blind. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond draw plus 3 outs to a ten. That is ~12.4% to pair a ten by the river and ~4.2% to make the backdoor diamond flush by river (roughly 16–17% combined equity) — not nearly strong enough to bluff or raise out of position. I called preflop to see a cheap flop and my plan was to continue only with a made hand or a strong draw. Claude checked preflop and there’s been no action on the flop, so I’ll check to realize equity for free. If Claude shows aggression on the turn I will fold to any meaningful bet (e.g. ~half‑pot or larger) and only call very small probes if the price plus implied odds justify it. No raise or lead here.

With 54o and no equity, I need to take down this pot immediately while Minni shows weakness via her flop check. I'm in position heads-up after Flash folded. Her pre-flop call followed by flop check indicates a marginal/weak hand. A min bet of 100 is a low-risk probe that applies pressure on an uncoordinated board (3d Jd 7c) where she likely missed. This wins the pot most of the time while keeping my risk minimal relative to my 13,150 stack. If she calls, I reassess, but the goal here is immediate fold equity exploitation.

I’ll fold. I checked the flop with T♦2♣ as planned — no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond draw. Claude’s bet is 100 into a 200 pot (pot now 300), so calling 100 gives ~3:1 pot odds and requires ~25% equity. My realistic equity (≈12.4% to pair a Ten by the river + ≈4.2% backdoor diamond flush ≈16–17% total) is well below that. I’m out of position, have no fold-equity or strong redraw, and raising as a bluff is a poor play here. Preserving a healthy 19,400 stack for better spots is the optimal play, so fold.

J♠9♣ on the button is a decent steal/opening hand — playable, has decent connectivity and good positional advantage versus the blinds. I have a deep stack (≈194 BB), so I want fold equity and the initiative. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to pressure the blinds and either take the pot preflop or play post-flop in position. Post-flop plan: if I get one caller I’ll continuation-bet on dry, high-card or uncoordinated boards and check/float or pot-control on wet/connected boards; if I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from Claude, who has shown aggression), I’ll fold J9o. If it becomes multiway, I’ll play more straightforwardly for value and avoid big bluffs.

Folding 6d7d from Small Blind against Minni's raise. While the hand has some potential with the flush draw and I have decent pot odds, the combination of poor position (out of position for postflop), weak hand strength preflop, and aggressive opponent makes this a fold. With 120 hands remaining and a healthy stack, I should be selective and avoid marginal situations from out of position.

I am in the big blind with Qs 2h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard 3x open. Queen-deuce offsuit is a very weak hand to defend against a button open, especially out of position. Minni has been quite active and has a significant stack advantage. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives me decent odds, but the hand is difficult to play post-flop and often dominated. Given my stack size of 7150, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips rather than defending a trash hand out of position.

4-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. From the button, while I have positional advantage, this hand lacks both high card strength and card connectivity. It cannot make a broadway straight, and has minimal potential to win in any scenario. With 13,300 chips and 150 hands remaining, there's no value in entering the pot with this hand. Even from late position, folding 4-2o is mathematically sound. Minni has a large stack at 19,450 and has been very aggressive throughout. A fold preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. After the Button folds, it is down to me and Minni in the Big Blind. A6o is a solid hand in a heads-up situation from the blinds. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and try to win the pot immediately or play a pot with a range advantage.

Heads-up in the big blind with Kc Jh vs a small-blind open to 300. KJo is strong enough heads-up to continue but I'm out of position, and Flash's open is likely wide. Calling keeps the pot controlled, gives correct pot odds (I'm calling 200 into ~400, break-even ~33%) and lets me realize equity against a wide range while avoiding unnecessary bloating of the pot out of position or getting isolate-4-bet. Plan: see the flop and play based on texture — value/raise strong top pairs, fold to heavy action on very unfavorable runouts or repop if I miss and face aggression. I call to 300 total.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a board of Ac-9h-5c. I was the pre-flop aggressor and I need to continue my initiative while extracting value from weaker hands like a 9, a 5, or various draws (specifically the club flush draw). Betting 300 into the 600 pot is a standard continuation bet size that serves both to build the pot and protect my hand.

Heads-up vs a wide SB opener who C-bets 300 into the ~600 preflop pot. I have Kc Jh — no made pair on Ac 9h 5c but decent showdown value and a backdoor club. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives me 25% break-even equity; vs a wide c-bet range my equity should be >= that. Raising OOP with no pair is high-variance and will bloat the pot against many Ax/pair hands that won't fold. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, raise/value-bet if I improve (pair K/J or pick up a strong club).

I have top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (6) is relatively weak. The turn 4d is largely a brick, although it does slightly connect with some straight draws like 67 or 78. Minni called pre-flop and on the flop, suggesting she likely has a piece of the board (a 9, a 5, or a weaker Ace) or a draw (flush or straight). By betting again, I maintain the initiative, extract value from her weaker pairs and draws, and protect my hand from giving a free card. I will bet 700, which is slightly over half the pot, to keep the pressure on her range.

Turn analysis: Flash has barreled flop and now the turn (bet 700 into a 1200 pot → pot is 1900). Calling 700 requires ~27% equity (700 / (1900+700) ≈ 26.9%). I hold Kc Jh with no pair and no realistic draw — after the 4♦ turn I cannot make a flush (needed two clubs on turn+river and turn missed), so my only clean outs on the river are three kings + three jacks = 6 outs → ~13% equity. That is well below the break-even threshold. Flash’s line (raise pre, c-bet flop, barrel turn) is very consistent with Ax/9x/5x or other value hands that will continue; raising OOP as a bluff here would be high-variance and unlikely to fold enough of his value range to be +EV. I therefore fold and conserve a large stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While it is a three-handed game, Jack-Seven offsuit is generally too weak to open from the button, especially since both opponents in the blinds have been relatively active and have larger stacks than me. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

7♦4♦ is a weak but suited, speculative hand. With Flash folded it’s effectively me vs Claude and the price to see a flop is tiny: I only need to put in 50 more to win a 200-chip pot (about 4:1 pot odds). Claude is a regular raiser/aggressive player, and min-raising to 200 here would commit an extra 150 into a spot where I’m likely out of position and could face 3-bets or large postflop pressure with a marginal holding. I’d rather see a flop cheaply and fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, or continue when I hit a pair/flush/strong draw. Therefore I’ll call the 50 (total committed this street = 100) to realize equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

With 6c-4c (bottom of the range), I'm facing a limp from Minni in the small blind. Since I've already posted the big blind and don't need to call any additional chips, I should take a free flop. This is a weak holding that won't win very often, but checking gives me equity to potentially hit the board while minimizing my investment in a weak hand. With only 16 hands remaining and a weak holding, defensive play is appropriate here. If Minni raises, I can reassess, but from the big blind facing a limp, a check is standard.

I missed the flop entirely with 7♦4♦ on Th 9c 3s — no pair, only backdoor diamond redraws and a small chance to pair by the river (~24%). Pot is tiny (200) and Claude checked his BB preflop, so there’s no clear weakness to exploit that justifies bloating the pot OOP with a pure bluff. With deep stacks I don’t want to face a raise and be forced to fold or play a large pot with a currently worthless hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity, and reevaluate on the turn — fold to significant aggression unless I improve or get a very favorable spot to bluff.

With 6c-4c on Th 9c 3s, I have nothing. Minni checked, giving me a free turn card. With only 16 hands remaining and a complete air hand, checking is standard defensive poker. I'll re-evaluate on the turn if another card comes.

The Qc on the turn doesn't help 7♦4♦ — I have no pair, no club/straight draw and only ~13% to make a pair by the river. The pot is tiny (200) and I'm out of position against Claude. He checked flop and turn, which reads more like weakness or pot control than a hand I can credibly bluff off later without strong blockers. I don't want to bloat the pot OOP with a pure bluff here, so I'll control the size and see a cheap river. Plan: check back the turn, fold to significant aggression unless I improve, and re-evaluate a river action (value-bet if I pair, consider a small river bluff only if the card and opponent action make it profitable).

With 6c-4c on Th 9c 3s Qc, I have no made hand and no real showdown value. Minni has checked flop and turn, indicating weakness, but with complete air I should minimize losses. Bluffing the river is marginal with 16 hands remaining and a decent stack. A small pot loss is acceptable; risking more chips on a bluff that requires Minni to fold most calling ranges is poor EV. Check and go to showdown is standard defensive poker here.

I hold 7♦4♦ and missed every street — no pair, no meaningful blockers or draws. Both checked flop and turn and the pot is tiny (200). The river paired queens, which actually makes a bluff less credible (opponent could now have trips or a strong Queen) and increases the chance of a caller. With deep stacks and no showdown value, betting here just risks extra chips for a low chance of success. I'll check back to avoid bloating the pot; if Claude bets I’ll fold to significant action.

I have 6c-4c on a final board of Th 9c 3s Qc Qh. This is complete air - no pair, no draw, no showdown value whatsoever. The board shows a pair of Queens, and I'm beat by any hand containing a Queen, pair, or even high cards higher than my six. 

Minni has checked three streets (flop, turn, river), indicating weakness or a marginal holding. However, they limped preflop from the SB and called the BB, so they have some hand in their range.

With 17 hands remaining and my stack at 13,200, this is not a spot to bluff. On a paired board where I have absolute nothing, bluffing is high risk and low reward. Minni's checking pattern could indicate weakness, but it could also indicate a hand like a pair of Nines, Tens, or even a Queen that doesn't want to get raised on the river.

The mathematically correct play is to check and go to showdown. I minimize my losses in this hand by not investing additional chips with complete air. This is straightforward defensive poker - when you have nothing and your opponent checks to you on the river, take the free showdown and move to the next hand.

Button with Td4o is a weak, marginal hand but the price to see a flop is cheap (100 into a 150 pot) and I have position on both blinds. Folding would be too nitty — calling preserves positional advantage and lets me realize equity cheaply. A min-raise to 200 to attempt a steal is thin: Claude (SB) has shown frequent aggression and Flash (BB) calls a fair amount, so raising risks getting called or 3-bet out of position with a poor hand. Plan: call 100, see the flop, and play postflop based on texture — fold to heavy action on dry boards, and continue/raise if I connect or pick up strong equity.

Call 50 to complete the small blind with K4o. Pot odds of 5:1 justify seeing a flop despite the weak hand. I have position on the big blind and decent stack size to play postflop. The King has showdown value and can win if I flop top pair or a strong draw.

I have a small pocket pair (3s 3d) in the Big Blind. Both the Button and the Small Blind have limped into the pot. While raising is an option to thin the field or take the pot down pre-flop, checking is the most prudent play here. Checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that plays best by hitting a set. Keeping the pot small out of position is a standard approach with low pocket pairs in this scenario.

Checking K4o on a J52 flop. This hand has minimal equity with only King high and a gutshot draw. I'm out of position facing two opponents. By checking, I keep the pot small and avoid putting chips into a marginal situation. If Flash bets and Minni folds, I can fold my weak hand. If the turn improves my King or completes a wheel draw, I can reevaluate. This is a prudent defensive check with a weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with a small pocket pair (33). The flop is Jc-5c-2d. I have an overpair to the bottom cards but a Jack is definitely in the range of my opponents who limped/called. The pot is currently very small (300). Claude has checked to me. While I could bet to try and take it down or get value from a drawing hand, checking is a safer way to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts on the button. Since I'm out of position against one player and the pot is small, checking to see a free turn is a reasonable line.

I called preflop to realize equity and keep position. On Jc-5c-2d with Td4c I’ve missed — I have no pair and only a very weak runner-runner club draw (need clubs on both turn and river) plus three outs to a Ten. Both opponents checked to me and the board is favorable for continuation bets from opponents (Claude has shown postflop aggression). Rather than firing a thin bluff that will often be called or raised, I’ll check for pot control and to induce bluffs. Plan: check now, fold to significant raise, and only barre l/raise if I improve (club/T or pick up strong equity) or get a clear fold on a later street.

Check K♦4♦ on the turn. Current hand (King-high) is weak, but I have ~16 outs including a flush draw and pair outs (approximately 35% equity). All players checked the flop, suggesting weakness. Being out of position, checking keeps the pot small and lets me see the river cheaply to complete my draw. Betting would be overextended with just King-high and could face aggression. This conservative approach respects my draw and pot control principles.

The turn is the 7 of clubs, bringing a third club to the board. I still have a weak pair of threes. Claude has checked for a second time. Given the passive nature of the hand so far (limped pre-flop, checked through on the flop), there is little reason to turn my hand into a bluff or bet for value here. I will check and see if Minni bets or if we can see a free river.

Board: Jc 5c 2d 7c. My Td4c: no made hand but a strong 4-card club draw plus two additional tens for a pair — 11 unique outs (~24% to improve on the river). Both opponents have checked flop and turn, showing weakness or pot control. Betting here is a thin bluff that gets called by many made hands (Jx, small pairs) or gets me raised by the few hands that already have me crushed. With position and decent equity to realize on the river, I prefer pot control and to induce bluffs. Plan: check the turn, fold to a significant raise, call a small probe bet only if the odds are correct, and if I hit the river play for value; if I miss I'll re-evaluate a river bluff only on a favorable card/line.

RIVER decision with K♦4♦ on 5♣2♦J♣7♣8♦. All three players (including myself) checked every street, indicating weakness. My hand is King-high, which is marginal but likely in showdown territory given the passive action throughout. 

The pot is only 300 with no bets committed on later streets. Betting here would be speculative - I risk additional chips trying to win a small pot with a weak hand. Given that:
1) All three players checked the flop, turn, and presumably the river is also checked by opponents
2) King-high has showdown value against a field that checked down
3) I'm out of position with a marginal holding
4) The pot is small, so there's minimal value in stealing

The correct play is to CHECK and go to showdown. This is consistent with the cautious, position-aware play I've demonstrated with this weak hand throughout the hand. King-high might very well be the best hand at this table given everyone's passivity.

The board has run out Jc-5c-2d-7c-8d in a limped pot that has been checked all the way to the river. My pocket threes have very marginal showdown value. There is no reason to bet, as I likely won't get called by worse hands, and better hands (like a 5, 7, 8, or any Jack) are unlikely to fold. By checking, I can reach showdown for free and hope my small pair is enough to win a small pot against missed high cards.

Board: 5c 2d Jc 7c 8d. My hand Td4c misses — no pair and I failed to make the flush. Both opponents checked flop, turn and river, showing either weakness or pot control; neither has given me a clear target to bluff. This is multiway and fold equity is low (both players must fold), while Flash in particular has shown a tendency to call down. The pot is tiny (300) relative to my stack, so a thin river bluff is unnecessary and likely marginal EV. I'll check to avoid throwing chips away — if either opponent now bets a small probe I'll re-evaluate (call only if sizing + reads strongly indicate a bluff; fold to meaningful pressure).

Playing AJ suited from the Button with position advantage. This is a premium hand that should be raised to build the pot, isolate the short stack (Flash), and define my hand strength. A 3x BB raise to 300 is standard aggression for this situation.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (Tc 8d). Claude, the button, has opened with a 3x raise to 300. T8o is generally too weak to defend from the Small Blind out of position against a standard open. While the Button range can be wide, T8o lacks the playability and high-card strength to call or 3-bet effectively here. Given my stack size and the desire to wait for better spots, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Claude opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind with 9c6h. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (total commitment 300), giving ~3.25:1 immediate pot odds (need ≈24% equity). Button opening range from the CO/button here is likely wide, and with deep stacks I can realize enough equity with 96o to justify a defend. I’ll call and keep the pot manageable — plan is to proceed cautiously OOP: continue with pairs, decent draws or two‑pair+; check‑fold to large c‑bets on dry boards and fold to heavy turn/river pressure if I don’t improve. I won’t 3‑bet a weak offsuit hand out of position and folding would be too tight given the pot odds and opponent’s likely wide range.

I’m first to act OOP with 9♣6♥ on J♠ T♥ 5♠ — no pair, no spade, no meaningful straight draw. I defended preflop for pot odds but the flop is fairly dry and unfavourable; my plan was to proceed cautiously OOP and check‑fold to a standard continuation bet. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to aggression (or realize equity for free if Claude checks back). Only an unusually tiny probe bet would merit a call; otherwise I’ll release this hand if he bets.

Betting AJ diamonds on a Js Th 5s flop. I flopped top pair with an excellent Ace kicker. Minni checked the flop, indicating weakness, so I should build the pot with a standard continuation bet of 400 (roughly 65% of the 650 pot). This value bet wins immediately against air/weaker pairs and extracts value from Minni's range while building a pot I'm likely ahead in.

Facing Claude’s c-bet (400) on J♠ T♥ 5♠ with 9♣6♥ OOP, I’m essentially drawing dead to a reasonable equity target. Calling 400 into a 1,050 pot requires ≈27.6% equity (400/(1050+400)). I have no spade and no meaningful straight draw — only 6 clean outs to a pair (≈24% to pair by the river) and many of those pairs still lose to Jx/Tx/overpairs. Claude has shown frequent aggression and this sizing is a standard C‑bet on a dry board; per my preflop plan I should check‑fold here rather than invest further OOP with air. Fold to preserve the stack for better spots.

I have a pocket pair of sixes on the button in a three-handed game. This is a strong opening hand. I will raise to the standard 3x big blind amount to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with a decent hand.

I’m in the small blind with J♠2♥ facing a button open to 300. This is a very weak, offsuit, dominated holding with poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop OOP gives poor equity vs a BTN open and carries big reverse implied odds (if I hit a Jack I’m often second‑best). Claude (BB) can still peel or 3‑bet, making a multiway pot even worse. A 3‑bet bluff isn’t attractive — I have no useful blockers and deep stacks mean tough spots if I get reraised. Preserve stack and fold this marginal hand.

Raising to 700 with AQ offsuit from big blind after button raiser and small blind fold. AQ is a premium hand worthy of aggression. Flash's 3x raise is standard positional play covering a wide range. A raise here applies pressure, gets value from weaker hands, maintains initiative in heads-up play, and is appropriately sized relative to stack depth. Looking to either take down the pot immediately or get value from a call with positional advantage.

Claude has 3-bet from the Big Blind after my button open. The raise is relatively small, requiring only 400 more to call into a pot that will be 1,450. With pocket sixes, I have a strong enough hand to continue, especially with the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially hit a set or navigate the hand based on his post-flop aggression.

Continuation bet with top pair, strong kicker on a coordinated flop. The Ad-8c-Ts board has broadway cards and potential draws that need to be pressured. A 750 bet (approximately 52% of the 1,450 pot) is standard sizing to extract value from Flash's likely range which could include weaker aces, pairs, broadway combinations, and draws. Given the ~7:1 effective stack ratio, this sizing maintains balanced pot odds and sets up for further action on later streets while protecting my hand equity.

Claude 3-bet from the Big Blind pre-flop and is now leading into me on an Ace-high board (Ad-8c-Ts). This board texture strongly favors his 3-betting range, which likely includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT), sets (AA, TT), and high pocket pairs (KK, QQ, JJ). My pocket sixes are now a weak underpair with only two outs to improve. Calling here would be chasing a very thin margin, as even if Claude is bluffing with high cards like KQ or KJ, he still has significant equity against my hand. Given the board and the action, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.

Kc Jd on the button in a 3-handed pot is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With deep stacks and two blinds to apply pressure to, I want to take the initiative and isolate — a standard 3x BB open is appropriate. Raising to 300 (total) gives good fold equity against the SB and BB, keeps postflop play in position, and leaves plenty of room to navigate if Claude 3-bets or Flash defends. I'll c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate sharply to large 4-bets.

6-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (450 to 250), the hand has minimal equity and poor playability postflop. With only 250 more to call, it's tempting, but 6-3o performs poorly against a button raiser's range and will likely result in a marginal or losing situation. Folding weak hands early in my decision tree is a fundamental part of solid poker strategy. I'll wait for a better opportunity with a stronger hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5h, a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a standard play, but even against a wide Button range, 95o performs poorly out of position. With my stack around 71 BB and only 12 hands remaining in the session, I should avoid defending with marginal hands that can lead to difficult post-flop situations. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

Folding 6-4 offsuit from the button. While position is valuable, this hand is too weak to play profitably. Early stage of blinds (100 to call) doesn't justify the marginal holdings. In late-stage tournament play (hand 139 of 150), stack preservation with weak hands is critical. Against Minni's aggression and Flash's short stack, a post-flop struggle with 6-4o is not worth the risk.

I am in the Small Blind holding 93o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Claude (the Button) has already folded. Although I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 93o has very poor playability and I will be out of position against Minni, who has been active and aggressive. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack for the remaining hands.

I am on the button with 6d 5d, a solid suited connector that plays well in position. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. My opponents have been aggressive with 3-bets lately, but I must maintain a balanced opening range. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take the blinds or play a pot in position.

I'm in the small blind with pocket deuces (2h2d) facing a button open to 300. With deep effective stacks and a single raiser, the +EV line is to set-mine rather than 3-bet or fold — I have ~11.8% to flop a set and want to keep the pot reasonably small and playability manageable out of position. A 3-bet would commit more chips and force difficult postflop spots for little extra value; folding is too tight. I'll call the extra 250 (total commitment 300) and re-evaluate on the flop; if I face a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve my stack.

I'm in the Big Blind with QhTd (Queen-Ten of different suits), a solid Broadway hand. Flash (button) raised to 300, and Minni (small blind) called 250. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 more.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: QT is a decent holding with two high cards - good playability on many flops
2. Pot odds: Calling 200 into a ~700 pot gives me 3.5:1 odds, which is reasonable
3. Position: Both opponents are in the pot already, so I'll have position postflop
4. Stacks: Flash is short (6,750), giving me leverage. Minni has 18,500
5. Stage: Hand 140/150 - getting close to the finish, but still need to accumulate chips

QT plays well in 3-way pots and has multiple ways to improve. The pot odds justify a call, and the hand has enough showdown value and equity to proceed. Folding strong hands in the BB against already-committed opponents is a mistake. I'll call to see the flop.

I missed my set. With 2h2d on a 3c Jc 9h flop in a 3-way pot I’m just an underpair with essentially no realistic redraw (a flush is impossible from here and straights are remote). I called preflop to set-mine, but since I didn’t hit I want pot control and to preserve stack depth for when/if I hit. Betting here would bloat the pot with a hand that is usually behind and has very little fold equity multiway. Plan: check and see a cheap turn. If faced with a small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 300) I may call once for set odds; I’ll fold to larger bets or any check‑raise. If I hit a deuce on a later street I’ll play aggressively for value; otherwise keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

Betting 500 on a dry 3-J-9 flop with top pair (Q-T). Minni's check indicates weakness, making this an ideal spot to take control and build the pot. This is roughly 55% pot-sized, which is standard for a continuation bet in position. The bet charges draws and gets value from weaker hands while narrowing the field. With 10 hands remaining, building the pot with strong hands is crucial for maximizing chip accumulation.

On the flop of Jc 9h 3c, I have 6d 5d, which is a complete miss. I was the pre-flop aggressor and both opponents called. Minni checked, and Claude (in the Big Blind) led out with a bet of 500 into a 900 pot (the actual pot is 1400 with current bets). This 'donk' bet from the Big Blind into two players usually indicates either a strong made hand (like a set or two pair) or a strong draw (like a flush draw or straight draw) that wants to define its value. Since I have no pair, no draw, and I'm facing a bet into two people, there is no reason to continue or bluff. I will fold.

Fold. I set‑mined preflop as planned and checked the flop to keep the pot small after missing. Claude’s bet of 500 into a 1,400 pot gives me ~26% pot‑odds to call (call 500 to win ~1,900). With pocket deuces I have only two outs to hit a set by river (~8.4% equity), far below the break‑even equity required. I have no backdoor flush and very little showdown value versus a c‑bet that likely contains Js/9s/overcards and club combinations. Raising OOP here is a losing line — I’d be bloating the pot with a virtually dead hand. Fold and preserve the 18.5k stack for better spots.

Button with Qc7d vs only the blinds. Pot is 150 and to call 100. Q7o is marginal but I have position and decent fold equity from the button. I want to take the initiative, reduce multi‑way chance, and pressure the blinds, so I’ll open-raise. Size to 300 (≈3xBB) gives meaningful fold equity while keeping pots manageable with a marginal hand. Postflop I’ll C‑bet favorable, use position to realize equity, and fold to heavy resistance or a large 3‑bet preflop.

Folding J9 offsuit from the small blind against Minni's raise. This is a marginal hand that lacks the strength needed to defend profitably from out of position. While Minni is active, J9o has insufficient equity to warrant a call or raise here, especially with limited hands remaining in the tournament. Preserving chips for better spots is the correct strategy.

I am in the big blind with 9h 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the button, and although button opening ranges are wide, 92o is not strong enough to defend, especially out of position. I will fold and conserve my stack.

Raising with AK from the button is textbook poker. This is one of the strongest hands preflop. Position is optimal, and standard raise sizing of 3x BB (300) applies pressure while building the pot. AK plays well both if blinds fold and if we get called. Flash's short stack (6,600) makes the dynamics favorable - if he shoves, I'm in good shape against a wide range. Minni must decide in the big blind with significant pressure. This is a clear raise.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

Claude opened to 300 on the button and I’m in the big blind with A♦6♥. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (i.e. putting 300 total in preflop). If I call the pot will be ~650, so my break-even equity on a call is ~30.8% (200/650). Versus a typical button open (a fairly wide range), A6o has roughly that equity or better and has good playability postflop (can make top pair, backdoor hearts, and uses the ace blocker). 3-betting with A6o is marginal — it’s a weak kicker ace and a tiny min-raise to 500 won’t buy much fold equity; a larger 3-bet bloats the pot out of position with a hand I don’t want to commit with often. Therefore I’ll defend by calling, see a flop, and proceed cautiously postflop — value-bet/top-pair hands, but fold or pot-control when dominated or facing heavy aggression.

I have A♦6♥ on 8♥-5♣-6♣ — a fragile but valuable one-pair hand (pair of sixes with the ace kicker). The pot is small relative to stacks and Claude’s button open is wide, so pot control is priority: I want to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot out of position versus hands that currently crush me (88/55/66 or 8x). Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his c-bet range while allowing me to call reasonable bets and reevaluate on the turn. Plan: check here; if Claude makes a small-to-medium c-bet I will call to see a turn (folding to a large raise unless I improve), and if he checks back I can use a safe turn to re-evaluate or target value on improvement (A or 6) or lead vs a passive line.

I'm playing AK on the button with position and initiative. Minni called from the big blind pre-flop and checked the flop. The board (8h-5c-6c) is relatively dry.

This is a textbook continuation bet situation. With AK, I have two overs and need to apply pressure while the initiative is mine. Minni's check from the big blind typically indicates weakness or a whiff, and I should take this opportunity to win the pot or build it when ahead.

A bet of 450 represents about 70% of the pot size, which is standard for a c-bet. This sizing is aggressive enough to win the pot immediately but not reckless given the stack depths and hand strength. If called, I maintain strong equity with my two overs and can continue playing the hand.

Position allows me to make decisions post-flop if called. This is exactly the type of spot where I should bet with my premium hand and strong position.

I have A♦6♥ on 8♥-5♣-6♣: second pair with the ace kicker. Claude opened wide on the button, and his 450 c-bet (~70% pot) is a standard protection/bluff-heavy sizing that includes lots of worse pairs, floats and club draws. Pot is 1100 and it costs 450 to call, so I need ~29% equity (450/1550) to break even — A6o versus a wide c-bet range meets that and has good post‑flop playability. My priorities are pot control and realizing equity out of position rather than bloating the pot with a marginal 3‑bet or folding and giving up. I’ll call the 450 to see a turn, then re-evaluate: if I improve (another 6 or an A) I can extract value; if Claude barrels large on a scary turn and I don’t improve I’ll fold to heavy aggression to protect my stack.

I hold Ad6h — second pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on 8h–5c–6c–3h. I improved on the turn but this is still a marginal, showdown-capable hand out of position: I have 5 clean outs to get to two‑pair/trips (3 Aces + 2 sixes), but no made flush/straight. Claude was the aggressor on the flop and is capable of holding top pair (8x), sets, or continued bluffs; I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. Pot control is the priority: check and see Claude’s action. If he checks back I take a cheap showdown; if he fires the river I’ll call reasonable bets for value/protection and fold to heavy pressure when I don’t improve.

Minni has checked both the flop and turn, indicating weakness or marginal hand strength. With AK, I have strong equity and maintain the initiative. The 3h turn is a blank. Continuing to apply pressure with a moderate bet sizing is the correct play - it wins the pot immediately against weak hands while maintaining strong equity if called. AK plays well as a value hand here against Minni's checking range. Turn bet of 700 is approximately 45% of current pot, standard for continuing aggression on a favorable board.

I will CALL 700. Pot is 2250 and calling 700 makes the pot 2950, so my break-even equity is only ~23.7% (700/2950). I hold A♦6♥ — second pair with the Ace kicker — which has decent showdown value against Claude’s wide button-raising/c-betting/turn-barrel range (lots of bluffs, floats and club draws) and I also block many Ace-high holdings. I have 5 clean outs to improve (3 Aces + 2 Sixes) and, more importantly, will beat a lot of his bluff/air that continues. Raising OOP with a marginal one-pair hand is unattractive (min-raise to 1400 will bloat the pot and get me in difficult spots vs sets/top pair), and folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to control the pot and reevaluate on the river — extract value if I improve, call small/medium river bets for showdown value, and fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve and the line indicates a stronger made hand.

Board: 8♥-5♣-6♣-3♥-Q♥. I hold A♦6♥ — a single pair (sixes) with the Ace kicker. Claude was the preflop raiser and has barreled flop and turn, so his range includes many hands that now beat me (Qx, 8x, sets, and — after the Q♥ river — two-heart combos that make a flush) as well as some missed club draws and bluffs. Betting out of position with a marginal one-pair hand is thin value and turns me into a target for raises; I want pot control and to realize my equity. Check here: it keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets me call reasonable sized river bets for showdown, and allows me to fold to large polarized river aggression that strongly represents a made hand.

River value bet with AK. Minni's passive checking line throughout the hand (flop check-call, turn check-call, river check) indicates a marginal holding—likely a medium pair, weak queen, or bricked draw. My AK is almost certainly ahead. A bet of 600 (approximately 20% of pot) is standard for extracting value from this type of weak range while maintaining aggression consistency. This sizing gets called by weaker hands and wins immediately against most of Minni's checking range. The line is profitable against both value-calling hands and occasional bluffing frequencies.

Claude was the preflop raiser and barreled flop and turn, so his range contains both strong value (sets, 8x, Qx now on the river) and a lot of missed/draw-style hands (club draws, A‑high floats, bluffs). He now makes a very small river stab — 600 into a 3550 pot — which is either a thin value bet or a probe/bluff. I hold A♦6♥: second pair with the best possible ace kicker for my pairing, plus a heart that slightly blocks some two‑heart flush combos. Pot odds are excellent: calling 600 into 3550 (pot becomes 4150) requires only ~14.5% equity. Versus a wide barreling range my hand has adequate showdown value and blocks some Ax combos, so folding would be too nitty. Raising OOP with a marginal one‑pair hand would bloat the pot and put me in difficult spots against stronger hands. I will call the 600 to see showdown and collect against missed draws/weak floats while folding to any large raise.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, 9 of hearts and 4 of clubs. This hand has very little equity and poor playability, even in a three-handed game. With only a few hands remaining, there is no reason to enter a pot with such a weak range, especially against the large stacks of Minni and Claude. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket kings are a premium — raise for value and to isolate the lone big blind. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x BB / standard opening size here) to build the pot, charge weaker hands, and keep post‑flop maneuvering room with deep stacks. If Claude 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on size (likely call or 4‑bet depending on his sizing); if he calls I’ll continue for value on favorable flops.

Calling 200 with Qc 6d from the big blind. Getting 2:1 pot odds with a marginal hand that has decent equity against Minni's wide SB raising range. Short stack (12,550) and already invested 100 BB makes the incremental call reasonable. Can evaluate further on the flop.

I have KK (KcKh) — a big overpair to this dry flop 2c Jh 3h and I’m in position (button). Claude called preflop so his range is wide (broadways, Axs, medium pairs, some Jx) and I’m ahead of most of it except Jx and unlikely sets. I want to extract value and protect against the two hearts on board and any turn cards that improve draws, so a straightforward continuation bet about half the pot makes sense. Bet 300 into 600 to charge worse hands, deny equity, and keep the pot controllable; re-evaluate on the turn if he calls or raises.

Folding Q6 on this flop. While I called pre-flop, the 2c-Jh-3h flop is not favorable for my hand. I have Q-high with only a gutshot straight draw (8 outs = ~17% equity). Minni's aggressive line suggests she likely has a pair of Jacks or better. My equity is insufficient even considering the 3:1 pot odds. As a short-stack, I need to pick higher-equity spots than this marginal holding.

On the button with A♣ J♣ — a strong, well-rounded hand in a three-handed pot. With pot 150 and to-call 100, I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and apply pressure to the blinds. A standard 3xBB open to 300 fits the table norms and keeps SPR reasonable for postflop play. Plan: c-bet favorable flops (A-high, cluby, or dry boards) and use position to extract value; if I face a small 3-bet I will most often call and play postflop in position; against a large 4-bet shove I will re-evaluate and likely fold. Raise to 300 to maximize fold equity and playability.

Folding 6-4o from small blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker with no equity advantage against Minni's raising range. Out of position post-flop, minimal playability, and with 6 hands remaining, should preserve stack for premium holdings.

In the big blind with T9o, I'm facing a standard 3x open from Minni on the button. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650, about 31% equity needed). T9o is a solid defensive hand in 3-handed play and flops reasonably well. Given Minni's high activity level, their range is likely wide, making a call profitable here to see a flop.

In the big blind with T9o on a paired board of 2d-3h-2h, I have two overcards but no made hand. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see their continuation bet. Leading into the raiser on this dry, paired board would be unconventional and likely wouldn't represent much strength. I will check and evaluate based on the size of a potential bet.

Check. I opened to 300 preflop with A♣J♣ and got one caller; the flop (2♦ 3♥ 2♥) is a low paired board with two hearts — it actually favors the caller’s defending range (pairs, any 3 for two‑pair, a single deuce for trips, and plenty of heart‑draw combos). I have only Ace‑high with no hearts or backdoor club draw, so I have very little fold equity and poor showdown value if I start firing. In position my best line is pot control: check and see how Flash proceeds. I will call small to medium turn bets to realize equity (and because an A or J improves me), but I will fold to a sizable check‑raise/all‑in since those lines are heavily weighted toward trips/full houses or strong two‑pair holdings on this texture. If the turn bricks and he checks back I’ll take another free card or can bluff a safe river when appropriate; if I hit an Ace I will begin to extract value. Overall checking minimizes risk and keeps decision flexibility post‑flop.

After a check-check on the flop, the turn brings a third heart and potentially completes some straight draws. I hold the Nine of hearts, which gives me a flush draw. Given Minni's aggressive history but their check on the flop, I will check here to see if they want to bet this turn. If they check back, I get a free card to hit my flush; if they bet, I will evaluate the price to continue based on my draw.

I opened preflop with A♣J♣ and intentionally checked the flop to control the pot on a low, paired, heart‑heavy board. The turn (5♥) completes a three‑heart texture and makes trips/full‑house combos and two‑heart flushes (if the caller held two hearts) much more plausible. I have no pair and poor showdown value, so firing here has limited fold equity and will often get called by worse or crushed by stronger holdings. With a deep stack and a relatively small pot, the disciplined line is pot control: check and see the river. I will call small‑to‑medium bets to realize equity (and because A or J improves me), but I’ll fold to large aggression that strongly represents trips/full house/flush. If I improve on the river (an A, J or a club giving a flush) I will extract value then.

On a board with four hearts (3h, 2h, 5h, 6h), I have a 9-high flush. The hand has been checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting neither player has a very strong made hand or a high heart draw. However, any heart higher than the nine (Jh, Qh, Kh, Ah) beats me. Given the small pot and the fact that most hands calling a bet would likely be a superior flush, I will check to see if Minni checks back or attempts a bluff. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but checking is the most prudent way to realize my showdown value without overcommitting against a potential trap or a higher heart.

I opened preflop with A♣J♣ and checked flop/turn to control pot on a wet, paired board. The final board (2♦ 3♥ 2♥ 5♥ 6♥) now has four hearts — any single heart in Flash’s hand makes a flush — and I have no heart and only Ace‑high. Flash has been passive (called preflop, checked every street), which is ambiguous (could be a missed holding or a slow‑played strong hand). Checking here hands the pot to any showdown value; a small, credible bluff is the best line to fold out non‑heart, non‑made hands that checked back.

I’ll make a modest river stab (~1/3–2/5 pot) to represent a completed heart/very strong hand. Bet = 250 on a 650 pot gives a fold‑threshold of ~28% which is very achievable against a wide check‑back range; the risk to my 20.7k stack is minimal and the size looks like a value attempt. If he calls I’ll concede to the likely flush/trip; if he folds I pick up the pot. Bet 250.

On the river, the board has four hearts and a pair of deuces. I hold the nine of hearts, giving me a nine-high flush. While any heart higher than a nine (A, K, Q, J) beats me, the action was checked through on both the flop and the turn, which decreases the likelihood that Minni holds a high-ranking heart or a very strong hand. Minni's small bet of 250 into a 650 pot provides me with excellent pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 900). Given the small sizing and the passive line taken by Minni until the river, it is highly probable that my nine-high flush is the best hand or that Minni is betting a weaker hand (like a straight or a smaller heart) for value. Raising is risky as it would likely only be called by a superior flush. Therefore, calling is the most profitable decision to realize my showdown value.

I have 6c-3d (six-three offsuit) on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with poor kicker connectivity and low card values. While I'm in a favorable position, the hand quality is too poor to play.
