Looking at the dynamics: Flash has a small stack (7,150) and Minni is aggressive with a big stack (20,400). Minni has been continuation betting frequently and raises aggressively pre-flop. Given that I'm 35 hands from completion and need to maintain my stack, investing chips with 6-3o is not a profitable long-term decision.

The expected value of playing this hand from the button against two opponents is negative, even accounting for position. Folding is the mathematically sound decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 65o. The Button (Claude) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). Minni is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. While 65o has some connectivity, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a dominant stack. With only five hands remaining in the session, it is better to preserve my stack and look for a higher-equity situation than to risk chips on a marginal steal attempt or a post-flop battle out of position.
I am on the button with A3o. In a 3-handed game, an Ace-high hand is strong enough to open for a raise to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll make a standard raise to 300.

I have A♦7♦ in the small blind facing a standard BTN open to 300 with the big blind still to act. A7s is worth defending, but I’m out of position — a marginal 3-bet (small or large) bloats a pot OOP and either won’t fold the BTN or will pull the BB into the hand. The pot odds to call 250 into a 450 pot are reasonable and A7s has decent equity vs a BTN opening range, plus good playability on A/diamond flops. I’ll call (total 300) to keep the pot manageable, see the flop, and proceed: c-bet/raise when I hit an ace or strong diamond draw, check/control or fold to heavy resistance on dry, unfavorable boards.

8-7 offsuit is too weak to play against two opponents (Flash's raise and Minni's call). While the 3.5:1 pot odds are decent, the hand equity (~19%) falls short of break-even requirements when out of position against two players. Folding preserves my stack for stronger situations.

Flop: K♦ 7♥ 5♣. I have A♦7♦ — a pair of sevens with the Ace kicker and only a backdoor diamond possibility (not a live 4‑card flush draw). Button opened preflop and will have a lot of Kx, Ax and broadway combos that beat me; leading out OOP will mainly get called by better hands and won’t fold enough worse hands to justify inflating the pot. I want pot control and the option to call a standard c‑bet and re‑evaluate on the turn (I block some A and diamond combos which reduces some of his strongest bluffs). Plan: check and call small/medium c‑bets to realize equity and fold to large polarized overbets unless I improve (7/A/diamond/runner). If he checks back I’ll take free turn cards and look to extract value if I pair the turn or player shows weakness.

I raised from the button pre-flop with Ace-high, and the flop comes K-7-5 rainbow. This is a very dry board that favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I am more likely to have a King in my range than the small blind defender. Minni checked, which is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of around 40-50% of the pot to represent the King and try to take the pot down immediately. If she has a pocket pair lower than the King or missed the board completely, she will likely fold.

Flop K♦ 7♥ 5♣; I have A♦7♦ — middle pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) and only a backdoor diamond runner. BTN opened pre and now small c-bets 300 into a 1000 pot (~30% pot). That price is too good to fold: calling 300 to win ~1600 requires ≈18.8% equity, and my hand beats Ace-high and many broadway/float combos while losing mainly to Kx and the rare two‑pair/trips. I'm out of position, so I prefer pot control and to keep worse hands and bluffs in the pot rather than bloating it with a marginal raise. Plan: call, see the turn, and re-evaluate — get aggressive if I improve to top pair/two‑pair or pick up a strong diamond draw; fold to heavy polarizing aggression if I don't improve.

Check. I hold A♦7♦ — a medium-strength hand (middle pair with the best possible kicker) but I’m out of position and behind much of BTN’s flop-c-bet range (Kx, some two-pair, occasional better hands). The 8♠ on the turn is a blank for my hand (no diamond completes, no realistic straight), so leading out OOP will mostly get called by hands that beat me and will only fold a thin portion of his range; that just bloats the pot unnecessarily. I want pot control and to keep bluffs and worse Ax combos in the pot, so I’ll check and plan to call small-to-medium bets on the turn, fold to large polarized overbets, and re-evaluate the river — bet for thin value or check to showdown depending on villain action.

On the turn, the board is K-7-5-8 rainbow. I have Ace-high (As 3c). I continuation bet the flop and Minni called, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board or a pocket pair. The 8 on the turn doesn't significantly change the board texture, but it does make it less likely for her to fold a hand like a 7, a 5, or a mid-pocket pair if she's determined to see a showdown. Since Minni has shown stickiness in previous hands, continuing to bluff with no equity (no straight or flush draw) is risky. By checking behind, I can take a free card and potentially win the pot if an Ace falls on the river, or evaluate her action on the river to see if I can win at showdown against her missed draws or weaker holdings.

I check. I hold A♦7♦ — a marginal but show‑down‑worthy hand (middle pair with the best possible kicker). Opponent opened, c‑bet small on the K♦7♥5♣ flop and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with a c‑bet probe or a medium/weak holding. The river 9♣ is a real card — it pairs the board for 9x and, crucially, completes 5‑6‑7‑8‑9 straights for any 6 in villain’s hand. A thin value bet here would only get called by a narrow set of worse hands (5x, some stubborn Ax) while folding out bluffs and running the risk of getting called or raised by hands that now beat me (Kx, 9x, any 6x straights, slow‑played two‑pair). Given I’m out of position, my earlier plan to control the pot, realize equity, and avoid bloating with a marginal hand still holds. Checking preserves showdown, avoids giving extra juice to hands that currently beat me, and keeps weaker hands and missed bluffs in. If villain bets a small amount I’ll call to realize equity; I’ll fold to large polarized river overbets.

The board has run out Kd 7h 5c 8s 9c. I have Ace-high (As 3c). I continuation bet the flop and was called by Minni, which suggests she has a piece of the board or a pocket pair. We both checked the turn. Minni has checked the river as well. Given Minni's history of being very 'sticky' and calling down with marginal hands like bottom pair (as seen in Hand #4 and Hand #142), a bluff on the river is unlikely to be successful. While my Ace-high rarely wins at showdown here, checking is the best play as betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

I'm on the button with 5♠8♠ — a suited connector with good playability and a big positional advantage in a three-handed pot. Opening wide from the button is standard; I want fold equity versus the blinds and the option to play a position-controlled pot if called. Claude (SB) is competent/aggressive and can 3‑bet, while Flash (BB) is shorter and likely to defend wide — raising isolates and pressures those ranges. I choose a standard table open to 300 (a bit larger than the minimum) to deny cheap multiway flops and build a pot I can play postflop in position. If I face a large/committed 3‑bet I will fold this speculative hand; if called I'll proceed postflop with a balanced c‑bet/check strategy depending on texture.

Folding 9s 6h from the small blind. This is a weak unpaired hand with poor equity against Minni's button raise. Playing out of position with trash cards doesn't justify calling 250 more chips. My stack is healthy, so there's no pressure to defend marginal hands. Standard fold.

JACK OF HEARTS (Jh) TREY OF SPADES (3s) is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a 3x open from the button. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive, J3o realizes equity poorly out of position, and with only a few hands remaining in the game, I prefer to fold and look for a better spot to commit chips.

Calling with 7-4o on the button. The minimal investment of 100 chips combined with excellent position and pot odds justifies the call. I can leverage positional advantage post-flop to win the hand through aggression if both blinds check, and I have an easy fold if the action suggests my hand is beaten. With only 2 hands remaining in the session, accumulating chips is important.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9c 7c. Claude has limped from the Button. For an additional 50 chips, I am getting 5-to-1 pot odds to see a flop with a suited gapper that has decent post-flop potential. I will complete the bet and see how the Big Blind reacts.

I have premium AA in the big blind in a limp pot (Claude called 100 on the button, Flash limped the SB). Pot is small (300) and both opponents have deep enough stacks to call. I want to build the pot and isolate while charging speculative hands (small pairs, suited connectors) — but not overcommit in case of a big 3-bet. A standard sized isolation raise is best: raise to 1,200 total. That price should fold the weakest limp-calls, keep worse hands and single callers in, and give me maneuverability postflop. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely 4-bet or commit depending on sizing), but preflop value and isolation are the priority here.

Folding 7-4o to a 3-bet. This is fundamentally the worst hand in poker with almost no equity against Minni's betting range. Even with button position, pot odds (44% needed vs 25% actual equity) don't justify a call. With only 2 hands remaining, I should preserve my 12,050 chip stack for better opportunities rather than chase a hopeless spot.

Minni has made a massive raise to 1200 from the Big Blind after two limps, representing a very strong range (JJ+, AQs+). While 9c 7c is a decent suited connector, I am out of position and the price to call (1100 more into a pot that would be 2500) is too steep given the high likelihood that I am dominated or facing an overpair. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack for better opportunities in the final hands.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a very strong starting hand. With only two hands left in the match, I want to play aggressively to maximize my stack. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size for this game, to take control of the hand and build the pot while having positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I’m in the small blind with Kc6h facing a 3bb open to 300. To call I must put in 250 into a 450 pot, needing roughly 35–36% equity (250/(450+250)) to justify a call. K6o is frequently dominated by a button opening range, plays poorly OOP, and the big blind still has a decision which can turn this into a multiway pot. A light 3-bet here is risky and won’t gain much fold equity against a button open and a deep big blind. With stacks deep and one hand left, I’ll preserve my stack and avoid inflating a pot OOP with a weak, dominated hand — fold.

Final hand of the tournament, heads-up situation after Minni folded. Calling 200 to win 450 with pot odds of 2.25:1. A3o is weak but has reasonable equity heads-up against Flash's likely wide short-stack range from the button. Pot odds justify the call despite hand weakness.

Final hand of tournament in heads-up situation. I have A3o on a 9h-5d-7h board against a short-stacked opponent. Betting 400 (roughly 60% of the pot) puts pressure on Flash's marginal range, allows me to win the pot immediately or proceed to the turn with controlling position. This bet size is rational and represents strength without over-committing given my chip advantage.

On a board of 9h-5d-7h, I have two overcards (Ace and Queen) and a back-door straight draw. Claude's lead of 400 into a 650 pot (making it 1050 total) shows strength on a board that likely connects with his Big Blind defending range (hands like 68, 89, 7x, or mid-pairs). However, with only two hands left in the match and holding Ace-Queen, I have sufficient equity and position to see at least one more card. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn, where I might improve to a top pair or pick up a better draw.

Checking on the turn with A3o. This weak hand has not improved on the 8c turn card - I still have only 5 outs. Given Flash's short stack and the fact that this is the final hand, checking accomplishes several goals: (1) it keeps the pot manageable when my hand is extremely weak, (2) it gives Flash a chance to check through or bluff, and (3) it preserves chips while still maintaining position for the river decision. Betting would commit too many chips with a hand that's well behind most of Flash's calling range. Checking is the prudent play to manage risk at tournament's end.

The board is 9h-5d-7h-8c. My Ace-Queen high has some showdown value against missed draws, but the board is extremely coordinated and favors Claude's defending range from the Big Blind. The 8c completes many straights (any hand with a 6). Claude led the flop and has now checked the turn. While this could be a sign of weakness, betting into such a connected board with only Ace high is risky, especially with only one hand remaining in the game after this. Checking behind allows me to see a free river card, potentially hit an Ace or Queen to take the lead, and minimizes the risk of getting check-raised on a board that heavily favors my opponent's range.

Final hand of tournament with A3o (ace-high). Flash checked the turn, indicating weakness or a missed draw. The river queen didn't improve my hand at all. With no pairs and only ace-high, I have minimal showdown value but checking risks nothing and allows the hand to conclude. Betting would be wasteful with such a weak holding, especially on the final hand. Check to showdown.

The river Queen of Diamonds gives me top pair with an Ace kicker (Ac Qs). However, the board is highly coordinated with four cards to a straight (5-7-8-9). Any 6 in Claude's range—which is very possible given his Big Blind defense and flop lead—now has a straight. While my hand improved significantly, Claude's check on the river after checking the turn suggests he is either giving up or planning to check-call with a marginal hand like a 9 or a 7. Betting for value is risky because a check-raise from Claude would force me to fold the best hand or lose a large pot to a straight. Given this is the penultimate hand of the game, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking a loss to a straight or a bluff.

I’m on the button with K♥8♣ — a marginal but playable hand in position in a 3‑handed pot. Pot is 150 and the call is 100; opening is +EV because position gives postflop advantage and I can often pick up the blinds. Claude (SB) is aggressive and will 3‑bet sometimes, Flash (BB) is sticky but folds to consistent pressure; I’ll use a standard 3x BB open to maximize fold equity without committing too much. Raise to 300 to steal the blinds and take the initiative; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold K8o vs a big 3‑bet, but call or proceed vs small sizing).

Calling with 9♣J♣ in the small blind after Minni's button raise. Hand has decent potential (connectors, flush possibility) and I'm getting 1.8:1 pot odds. This allows me to see the flop with positional advantage, and the hand plays well post-flop. Standard button raise from an aggressive opponent; calling is more profitable than folding this holding or unnecessarily raising from out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc6d. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300, and Claude has called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1, which is generally enough to defend a hand like King-high in a multi-way pot, especially closing the action. While Kc6d is not a premium hand, the price to see a flop is attractive enough to justify a call here to conclude the session on the final hand.

Betting trips on a J-J-6 flop with two opponents still active. This hand is extremely strong and unlikely to be beat currently. Betting 500 (roughly 55% pot) extracts value from weaker hands and draws while keeping both opponents engaged. Flash has a shorter stack making this a good spot to build the pot. My position as small blind to act first gives me the aggression advantage. Standard value bet with the strongest hand.

On a board of Js-Jh-6c, Claude (the Small Blind) has led out with a bet of 500 into a pot of 900. This 'donk lead' into both the Big Blind and the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) typically represents significant strength, often a Jack or a pocket pair higher than my Sixes. While I have bottom pair, I am caught between two players, and the button (Minni) has yet to act. Calling here with a marginal hand like bottom pair is risky, as I could be facing a much stronger hand from Claude or a raise from Minni. Given this is the final hand of the session, I will choose the disciplined path and fold my marginal pair to preserve my stack.

Flop is Js Jh 6c after I opened to 300 on the button with K♥8♣. Claude called pre‑flop and now leads for 500 into a ~900 pot (Flash folded) — heads‑up. I have no pair and no clean draws: only raw outs are 3 kings + 3 eights, many of which aren’t clean versus a betting range that includes Jx (trips), overpairs and other value hands. Pot odds to call (500 into a 1400 pot) require ~26% equity, which K8o does not have vs Claude’s continuing range. Raising as a bluff is thin here — opponent is capable of calling or reraising with many hands that still beat me, and I have no turn equity to realize a profitable river bluff line reliably. Fold and preserve the stack.
